244	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
245	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02ME.
246	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its
247	  % generic point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at
248	  % \(\eta\) is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the
249	  % function field of \(X\). We call the corresponding discrete valuation
250	  % \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
251	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function
252	  % on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has
253	  % a \emph{zero} along \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\)
254	  % has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
255	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\)).}
256	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a prime divisor \(Y \subset X\) with generic
257	  point \(\eta \in Y\), the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
258	  valuation ring (by regularity in codimension one) with quotient field the
259	  function field \(K(X)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\)
260	  the \emph{order of \(f\) along \(Y\)} is the integer
261	  \[
262	    \ord_Y(f) \;:=\; v_Y(f) \;\in\; \mathbb Z,
263	  \]
264	  where \(v_Y\) is the normalised discrete valuation associated to
265	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\). If \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{zero of
266	  order \(\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}; if \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\), \(f\) has a \emph{pole
267	  of order \(-\ord_Y(f)\) along \(Y\)}.
268	  Specialisation to a curve. If \(C\) is a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)
269	  and \(P \in C\) is a closed point, then \(P\) is automatically a prime
270	  divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the \(1\)-dimensional
271	  scheme \(C\)); the local ring \(\mathcal O_{C, P}\) is a discrete valuation
272	  ring by smoothness, and \(\ord_P(f) = v_P(f)\) is the standard DVR
273	  valuation of \(f\) at \(P\).
274	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-175 pin, per analogist
275	  \texttt{dvr-rationalmap-order}).} The iter-175 Lean signature is
276	  \begin{quote}
277	    \texttt{noncomputable def order \{X : Scheme.\{u\}\} [IsIntegral X]\\
278	    \phantom{}\quad[IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)\\
279	    \phantom{}\quad[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]\\
280	    \phantom{}\quad(f : X.functionField) : \(\mathbb Z\)}
281	  \end{quote}
282	  with \emph{no} \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis (junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention,
283	  see below). The body is
284	  \[
285	    \texttt{order}\ Y\ f \;:=\;
286	      \texttt{WithZero.log}\ \bigl(
287	        \texttt{Ring.ordFrac}\ (X.\texttt{presheaf.stalk}\ Y.\texttt{point})\ f
288	      \bigr).
289	  \]
290	  \emph{Mathlib API path.}
291	  \begin{itemize}
292	    \item \texttt{Ring.ordFrac R : K \(\to\!\!*_0\) \(\mathbb Z^{m_0}\)}
293	      (with \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} = \texttt{WithZero (Multiplicative}\ \mathbb Z)\)),
294	      from \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic} (Stacks
295	      tag~\texttt{02MD}), is Mathlib's canonical monoid-with-zero hom
296	      extending the length-of-quotient valuation
297	      \texttt{Ring.ord} from a Noetherian Krull-dim-\(\leq 1\) ring to
298	      its fraction field via \texttt{Submonoid.LocalizationMap.lift\textsubscript{0}}.
299	      For a DVR-uniformised local ring its value on \(r \in R\) is the
300	      length of \(R/(r)\) (the DVR valuation); on \(a/b \in K\), it is
301	      \(\texttt{ord}(a) - \texttt{ord}(b)\). The hypotheses
302	      \texttt{[CommRing R] [Nontrivial R] [IsNoetherianRing R]\\
303	      [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]} are exactly the typeclasses the body
304	      synthesises from \([\texttt{IsIntegral}\ X]\) (delivers
305	      \texttt{IsDomain} hence \texttt{Nontrivial} on the stalk via
306	      \texttt{instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral},
307	      and \texttt{IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
308	      X.functionField} via
309	      \texttt{instIsFractionRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafFunctionField}),
310	      from \([\texttt{IsLocallyNoetherian}\ X]\) (delivers
311	      \texttt{IsNoetherianRing} on the stalk via
312	      \texttt{instIsNoetherianRingCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsLocallyNoetherian}),
313	      and from the explicitly threaded
314	      \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]}.
315	    \item \texttt{WithZero.log : \(\mathbb Z^{m_0} \to \mathbb Z\)}
316	      from \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero} is Mathlib's
317	      canonical projection to the additive value group with
318	      junk-on-zero: \texttt{log\_zero}: \(\log 0 = 0\);
319	      \texttt{log\_mul}: \(\log(x \cdot y) = \log x + \log y\) for
320	      \(x, y \neq 0\). This makes the downstream additive identities
321	      \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\), \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\),
322	      \(v_Y(1) = 0\) available as named lemmas for
323	      \ref{def:order_at_point}.
324	  \end{itemize}
325	  \emph{Junk-on-\(f = 0\) convention.} Setting \(f = 0\) in
326	  \texttt{X.functionField} feeds \(0\) into
327	  \texttt{Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)} (zero of the
328	  monoid-with-zero hom; \texttt{Ring.ordFrac} maps \(0\) to
329	  \(0 \in \mathbb Z^{m_0}\)); then \texttt{WithZero.log 0 = 0} so
330	  \(\texttt{order}\ Y\ 0 = 0\). This is the canonical Mathlib junk
331	  choice; no separate \(f \neq 0\) hypothesis is threaded on the
332	  signature.
333	  \emph{In-tree gap-fill (project-side; Mathlib upstream opportunity).}
334	  Mathlib (as of \texttt{b80f227}) has no direct bridge from the
335	  topological condition \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ Y.\texttt{point} = 1\)
336	  on \texttt{X.carrier} to the algebraic condition
337	  \(\texttt{Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)}\) on the
338	  stalk. The missing fact (Stacks tag~\texttt{02IZ} / \texttt{005X}) is
339	  the topological identity ``coheight of \(x\) in the scheme's
340	  specialisation preorder equals the height of the prime ideal
341	  corresponding to \(x\) in any affine chart''; the algebraic step
342	  ``ideal height \(=\) stalk Krull dim'' is in Mathlib as
343	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
344	  (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height}) and
345	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
346	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}). The iter-175
347	  workaround is to thread the explicit instance
348	  \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} on
349	  \texttt{order} (and downstream consumers); a future two-line
350	  Mathlib upstream PR pinning the coheight-height bridge would let
351	  the project drop the explicit argument in favour of synthesis from
352	  \texttt{coheight Y.point = 1} via the \texttt{PrimeDivisor} carrier.
353	\end{definition}
354	\section{Divisor of a closed point on a curve}
355	\begin{definition}
356	\leanok
357	[Divisor of a closed point on a curve]
358	  \label{def:divisor_closed_point}
359	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
360	  
361	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint}
362	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
363	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154):
364	  % "A prime divisor is just a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can
365	  % be written \(D = \sum n_i P_i\), where the \(P_i\) are closed points, and
366	  % \(n_i \in \mathbf Z\)."
367	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now we come to the study of divisors on curves. If \(X\)
368	  % is a nonsingular curve, then \(X\) satisfies the condition \((\ast)\) used
369	  % above, so we can talk about divisors on \(X\). A prime divisor is just
370	  % a closed point, so an arbitrary divisor can be written $D = \sum n_i
371	  % P_i\(, where the \)P_i\( are closed points, and \)n_i \in \mathbf Z$."
372	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (``Divisors on Curves'').}
373	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over a field \(k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a
374	  closed point. The \emph{Weil divisor associated to \(P\)} is the element
375	  \[
376	    [P] \;:=\; 1 \cdot P \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C)
377	  \]
378	  i.e.\ the prime divisor \(P\) with multiplicity one. The assignment is
379	  well-defined because the closed point \(P\) is automatically a codimension-\(1\)
380	  integral closed subscheme of the \(1\)-dimensional integral scheme \(C\).
381	  Conversely, every prime divisor on \(C\) is of this form, so an arbitrary
382	  divisor on \(C\) is a finite formal \(\mathbb Z\)-linear combination
383	  \(\sum n_i [P_i]\) of closed points.
384	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope (iter-174 pin).} The Lean signature
385	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint} accepts a
386	  scheme \texttt{C : Scheme.\{u\}}, a point \texttt{P : C}, and a
387	  hypothesis \texttt{(\_hP : IsClosed (\{P\} : Set C))}. It does
388	  \emph{not} typeclass-pin ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' at the
389	  carrier level, and it does \emph{not} thread the
390	  one-dimensional-integral-scheme content into the declaration head.
391	  Instead the iter-174 encoding is \emph{junk-defined} outside the
392	  codim-\(1\) regime:
393	  \[
394	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP\ :=\
395	      \begin{cases}
396	        \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1 &
397	          \text{if } h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1,\\
398	        0 & \text{otherwise.}
399	      \end{cases}
400	  \]
401	  On the off-branch --- where \texttt{P} is not a codimension-one point
402	  (e.g.\ \texttt{P} is the generic point of \texttt{C}, or a closed point
403	  of codimension \(\geq 2\) on a higher-dimensional scheme) ---
404	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} returns the zero divisor \(0 \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). This
405	  convention lets the Lean signature avoid threading
406	  \texttt{[OneDimensional C]} / \texttt{[IsIntegral C]} typeclass payload
407	  at the declaration head; the well-definedness consumer must instead
408	  invoke the bridge equation lemmas
409	  \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single} and \ref{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
410	  to expose the two branches at the point of use.
411	  On the intended regime (one-dimensional integral scheme \(C\), \(P\) a
412	  closed point), the chapter's mathematical statement
413	  \([P] := 1 \cdot P \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) is recovered by combining
414	  \ref{def:divisor_closed_point} with the chain
415	  \(\texttt{IsClosed \{P\}} \Rightarrow \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\)
416	  that the prover threads inside the consumer (using the
417	  one-dimensional-integrality content).
418	\end{definition}
419	\begin{lemma}
420	\leanok
421	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} in the codim-\(1\) regime]
422	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_single}
424	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
425	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
426	  \(h : \texttt{Order.coheight}\ P = 1\) (the codimension-one witness
427	  promoting \(P\) to a \texttt{PrimeDivisor} via \ref{def:divisor_closed_point}),
428	  then
429	  \[
430	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
431	      \texttt{Finsupp.single}\ \langle P, h\rangle\ 1
432	      \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
433	  \]
434	\end{lemma}
435	\begin{proof}
436	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
437	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
438	  positive branch using the hypothesis \(h\).
439	\end{proof}
440	\begin{lemma}
441	\leanok
442	[Bridge equation: \texttt{ofClosedPoint} junk-branch]
443	  \label{lem:ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint_eq_zero}
445	  Let \(C : \texttt{Scheme.\{u\}}\) be a scheme and let \(P \in C\) be a
446	  point with \(\texttt{IsClosed}\,(\{P\} : \texttt{Set}\ C)\). If
447	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\) (the off-branch: \(P\) is not a
448	  codimension-one point of \(C\) --- e.g.\ \(P\) is the generic point of an
449	  integral scheme, or a higher-codim point of a higher-dimensional
450	  scheme), then
451	  \[
452	    \texttt{ofClosedPoint}\ P\ \_\!hP \;=\;
453	      0 \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(C).
454	  \]
455	\end{lemma}
456	\begin{proof}
457	  Unfolds the dependent-\texttt{if} in the definition of
458	  \texttt{ofClosedPoint} (\ref{def:divisor_closed_point}) on the
459	  negative branch using the hypothesis
460	  \(\texttt{Order.coheight}\ P \neq 1\).
461	\end{proof}
462	\section{Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base}
463	\begin{definition}
464	\leanok
465	[Degree of a divisor on a curve over \(\bar k\)]
466	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles}
467	  \label{def:divisor_degree}
468	  
469	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree}
470	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6 ("Divisors on Curves"), p.~137 (read from
471	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
472	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE0.
473	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We define the \emph{degree} of \(D\) to be \(\sum n_i\)."
474	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~137 (definition of degree on a
475	  nonsingular curve).}
476	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
477	  \(\bar k\). The \emph{degree} of a Weil divisor $D = \sum n_i [P_i] \in
478	  \mathrm{Div}(C)$ is the integer
479	  \[
480	    \deg(D) \;:=\; \sum_i n_i \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
481	  \]
482	  The definition uses that every closed point of \(C\) has residue field
483	  \(\bar k\) (so each prime divisor contributes degree one); over a general
484	  field \(k\) the same definition with the residue-field-degree weight
485	  \(\deg(D) := \sum_i n_i \, [\kappa(P_i) : k]\) recovers the
486	  \emph{geometric} degree, but the project's RR bridge needs only the
487	  \(\bar k\) specialisation.
488	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean definition
489	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree} is given for an
490	  arbitrary scheme \texttt{X : Scheme.\{u\}} as the literal sum of
491	  coefficients via \texttt{Finsupp.sum D (fun \_ n => n)}; the typeclass
492	  set ``smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\)'' is \emph{not} pinned on the
493	  base declaration. Mathematically this assigns to every Weil divisor
494	  \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) on any scheme \(X\) the integer-valued sum of its
495	  multiplicities; for a smooth proper curve over \(\bar k\) this coincides
496	  with the curve-theoretic degree of the prose (every closed point has
497	  residue field \(\bar k\), so the residue-field-degree weight is identically
498	  one). The chapter's prose pins the curve hypothesis for clarity and to
499	  flag that, on a scheme with non-trivial residue-field extensions, the
500	  bare-sum signature does \emph{not} compute the geometric degree; the
501	  curve hypothesis is threaded at the call sites that consume
502	  \texttt{degree} for arithmetic statements
503	  (\ref{thm:principal_deg_zero}, \texttt{RR.2}--\texttt{RR.4}).
504	\end{definition}
505	\begin{theorem}
506	\leanok
507	[The degree map is a group homomorphism]
508	  \label{thm:divisor_degree_hom}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom}
510	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
511	  \(\bar k\). The degree map of \ref{def:divisor_degree} is a group
512	  homomorphism
513	  \[
514	    \deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \longrightarrow \mathbb Z,
515	    \qquad
516	    \deg(D_1 + D_2) \;=\; \deg(D_1) + \deg(D_2),
517	    \qquad
518	    \deg(-D) \;=\; -\deg(D).
519	  \]
520	\end{theorem}
521	\begin{proof}
522	  Immediate from the definition: \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) is the free abelian group on the
523	  set of closed points of \(C\), and \(\deg\) is the unique group homomorphism
524	  sending each generator \([P]\) to \(1 \in \mathbb Z\) (equivalently: the sum
525	  of the multiplicities). Both additivity and negation thus reduce to the
526	  corresponding identities for finite sums of integers.
527	\end{proof}
528	\section{Principal divisors}
529	\begin{definition}
530	\leanok
531	  \uses{def:codim1_cycles, def:order_at_point}
532	[Principal divisor of a rational function]
533	  \label{def:principal_divisor}
534	  
535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal}
536	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Lemma~6.1 + the definition of \(\operatorname{div}(f) = (f)\),
537	  % p.~131 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
538	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Lemma 6.1. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\), and let \(f \in K^\ast\)
539	  % be a nonzero function on \(X\). Then \(v_Y(f) = 0\) for all except finitely
540	  % many prime divisors \(Y\)."
541	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\) and let \(f \in K^\ast\).
542	  % We define the \emph{divisor of \(f\)}, denoted \((f)\), by
543	  % \((f) = \sum v_Y(f) \cdot Y\), where the sum is taken over all prime
544	  % divisors \(Y\) of \(X\). By the lemma, this is a finite sum, hence is a
545	  % divisor. Any divisor which is equal to the divisor of a function is
546	  % called a \emph{principal divisor}."
547	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Lemma~6.1 and the following definition,
548	  p.~131.}
549	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). For a nonzero rational function \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\),
550	  Hartshorne's Lemma~6.1 asserts that \(\ord_Y(f) = 0\) for all but finitely
551	  many prime divisors \(Y\) on \(X\); hence the formal sum
552	  \[
553	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;:=\; \sum_{\substack{Y \subset X \\ \text{prime divisor}}}
554	    \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X)
555	  \]
556	  has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor, called the
557	  \emph{principal divisor of \(f\)}. On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\)
558	  the same definition specialises to
559	  \(\mathrm{div}(f) = \sum_{P \in C \text{ closed}} \ord_P(f) \cdot [P]\), the formal
560	  sum over closed points.
561	\end{definition}
562	\begin{theorem}
563	\leanok
564	[The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism]
565	  \label{thm:principal_hom}
566	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom}
567	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, the paragraph after the principal-divisor
568	  % definition, p.~131 (read from
569	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
570	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Note that if \(f, g \in K^\ast\), then \((f/g) = (f) - (g)\)
571	  % because of the properties of valuations. Therefore sending a function
572	  % \(f\) to its divisor \((f)\) gives a homomorphism of the multiplicative
573	  % group \(K^\ast\) to the additive group \(\operatorname{Div} X\), and the
574	  % image, which consists of the principal divisors, is a subgroup of
575	  % \(\operatorname{Div} X\)."
576	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (group-homomorphism remark).}
577	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). The principal-divisor map
578	  \(\mathrm{div} \colon K(X)^{\times} \to \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is a group homomorphism from the
579	  multiplicative group of nonzero rational functions to the additive group
580	  of Weil divisors. Concretely
581	  \[
582	    \mathrm{div}(fg) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g),
583	    \qquad
584	    \mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) \;=\; -\mathrm{div}(f),
585	    \qquad
586	    \mathrm{div}(1) \;=\; 0.
587	  \]
588	\end{theorem}
589	\begin{proof}
590	  Each prime divisor \(Y\) on \(X\) contributes a discrete valuation \(v_Y\) on
591	  \(K(X)\) (\ref{def:principal_divisor}); by the standard DVR axioms,
592	  \(v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)\) and \(v_Y(f^{-1}) = -v_Y(f)\) for $f, g \in
593	  K(X)^{\times}\(, and \)v_Y(1) = 0$. Summing these identities over all prime
594	  divisors of \(X\) (a sum that has finite support on each factor by
595	  \ref{thm:principal_hom}, so the support of the sum is the union
596	  of the two finite supports and remains finite) gives the three displayed
597	  identities.
598	\end{proof}
599	\begin{theorem}
600	\leanok
601	[Principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve]
602	  \label{thm:principal_deg_zero}
603	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero}
604	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138 (read from
605	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 155).
606	  % Stacks pointer: tag 0BE3.
607	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.10. \emph{A principal divisor on a complete
608	  % nonsingular curve \(X\) has degree zero. Consequently the degree function
609	  % induces a surjective homomorphism $\deg \colon \operatorname{Cl} X \to
610	  % \mathbf Z$.}"
611	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Corollary~6.10, p.~138.}
612	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper curve over an algebraically closed field
613	  \(\bar k\). For every nonzero rational function \(f \in K(C)^{\times}\) the
614	  associated principal divisor has degree zero:
615	  \[
616	    \deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) \;=\; 0 \;\in\; \mathbb Z.
617	  \]
618	\end{theorem}
619	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Let \(f \in K(X)^\ast\). If \(f \in k\), then
620	% \((f) = 0\), so there is nothing to prove. If \(f \notin k\), then the
621	% inclusion of fields \(k(f) \subset K(X)\) induces a finite morphism
622	% \(\varphi \colon X \to \mathbf P^1\). It is a morphism by (I, 6.12). We
623	% have \((f) = \varphi^\ast(\{0\} - \{\infty\})\). Since \(\{0\} - \{\infty\}\)
624	% is a divisor of degree 0 on \(\mathbf P^1\), we conclude that \((f)\) has
625	% degree 0 on \(X\)."
626	\begin{proof}
627	  Following Hartshorne's Corollary~6.10. If \(f \in \bar k \subset K(C)\) is
628	  a constant scalar, then \(f\) has no zeros or poles, so \(\mathrm{div}(f) = 0\) and
629	  \(\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = 0\). Otherwise the inclusion of fields
630	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) exhibits \(K(C)\) as a finite extension of the
631	  rational function field \(\bar k(f) \cong \bar k(t)\), so the corresponding
632	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is finite (by the
633	  smooth-proper-curve / function-field correspondence on which a curve is
634	  determined by its function field, classical I.6.12). The principal
635	  divisor \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) equals the pullback \(\varphi^{*}([0] - [\infty])\)
636	  along \(\varphi\) of the degree-\(0\) divisor \([0] - [\infty]\) on
637	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\); since pullback along a finite morphism
638	  multiplies degrees by \(\deg(\varphi)\), we conclude
639	  $\deg(\mathrm{div}(f)) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg([0] - [\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)
640	  \cdot 0 = 0$.
641	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The two auxiliary statements ``the inclusion
642	  \(\bar k(f) \subset K(C)\) defines a finite morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\)''
643	  and ``pullback along a finite morphism of curves multiplies degrees by
644	  the degree of the morphism'' (Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9, the
645	  multiplicativity of degree) are themselves separable sub-lemmas of
646	  \texttt{RR.1}; they are stated here for completeness and slotted to be
647	  closed in a follow-up iteration. The signature
648	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal\_degree\_zero}
649	  is recorded as the public face of this theorem and is depended on by
650	  the \texttt{RR.3}/\texttt{RR.4} chain.
651	\end{proof}
652	\section{Positive part of a Weil divisor}
653	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase): added to host the Pin 2 corrective per
654	% `task_results/AlgebraicJacobian_RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso.lean.md`
655	% (iter-189). `Hom.poleDivisor` is being refactored to return the
656	% positive part of `principal (φ^* t_∞)` so that
657	% `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is mathematically correct (the principal
658	% divisor has degree 0 by `thm:principal_deg_zero`; only its
659	% positive part can have positive degree).
660	\begin{definition}
661	
662	
663	\leanok
664	[Positive part of a Weil divisor]
665	  \label{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}
666	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart}
667	  \textit{Source: Project-bespoke (Hartshorne II.6.10 phrasing).}
668	  Let \(X\) be a scheme satisfying the codim-1 hypotheses of
669	  \ref{def:codim1_cycles}. For a Weil divisor
670	  \(D = \sum_Y n_Y \cdot Y \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\), the \emph{positive part}
671	  \((D)_0 \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) is the Weil divisor
672	  \[
673	    (D)_0 \;:=\; \sum_{Y \,:\, n_Y > 0} n_Y \cdot Y.
674	  \]
675	  Concretely, \((D)_0\) is obtained from \(D\) by replacing each coefficient
676	  \(n_Y\) with \(\max(n_Y, 0)\). Equivalently, in the \(\Finsupp{\mathrm{PrimeDivisor}(X)}{\mathbb Z}\)
677	  presentation, the positive part is obtained either by the pointwise lattice
678	  join with the zero divisor — \(\,(D)_0 = D \sqcup 0\,\) — or, equivalently
679	  and more transparently for typeclass synthesis, by mapping the coefficients
680	  through the zero-preserving function \(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0\):
681	  \(\,(D)_0 = \mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\;(n \mapsto n \sqcup 0)\;D\). The two
682	  forms agree by the universal property of \(\mathtt{Finsupp.mapRange}\). The
683	  Lean implementation uses the \(\mathtt{mapRange}\) form to avoid the
684	  noncomputable \(\Finsupp.\mathtt{semilatticeSup}\) synthesis. The complementary \emph{negative part}
685	  \((D)_\infty\) is defined by \((D)_\infty := (-D)_0\), so that
686	  \(D = (D)_0 - (D)_\infty\) is the canonical decomposition into an
687	  effective (non-negative-coefficient) divisor minus an effective divisor.
688	  On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\), where the prime divisors
689	  are the closed points and the coefficients are integers, the positive part
690	  reduces to keeping only the closed points whose multiplicity is positive
691	  — i.e. precisely the zeros of any rational function whose principal
692	  divisor is \(D\), in the case \(D = \mathrm{div}(f)\).
693	\end{definition}
694	\begin{lemma}
695	
696	\leanok
697	[Degree of the positive part of \(\mathrm{div}(f)\) for a non-constant rational
698	function equals the function-field extension degree]
699	  \label{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
700	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}
701	  % NOTE (iter-193): the iter-191 equation-form Lean signature (with
702	  % explicit `(t : K)` + `(halg : ... ≠ 0)` arguments only) was diagnosed
703	  % MATHEMATICALLY FALSE iter-192 via the counter-witness
704	  % `K = K(C), t = 1` (LHS `degree 0 = 0`, RHS `Module.finrank K(C) K(C)
705	  % = 1`). The iter-193 plan-phase refactor
706	  % `lane-i-localparameter-signature` adds the missing local-parameter
707	  % constraint as an existential argument
708	  %   `(hlp : ∃ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor,
709	  %      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (algebraMap K K(C) t) = 1)`,
710	  % matching the chapter prose's "`t_∞` is a local parameter at `∞ ∈ ℙ¹`"
711	  % requirement. The consumer
712	  % `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank`
713	  % (`RationalCurveIso.lean`) supplies the witness via
714	  % `localParameterAtInfty kbar` (currently as a typed sorry owed
715	  % iter-194+; the prime divisor is `φ^{-1}(\infty)` at order 1 by
716	  % smoothness + Hartshorne II.6.9 affine chart).
717	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137 (multiplicativity
718	  % of degree under finite pullback) (read from
719	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 154).
720	  % Stacks pointer: tag 02RW (pullback of divisors under finite
721	  % morphisms), tag 0BE6 (degree under finite morphism).
722	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 6.9. \emph{Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a finite
723	  % morphism of nonsingular curves. Then for any divisor \(D\) on \(Y\),
724	  % \(\deg(f^\ast D) = [K(X) : K(Y)] \cdot \deg D\).}"
725	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, Proposition~6.9, p.~137.}
726	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over an
727	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let
728	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism.
729	  Let \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) be a local parameter at \(\infty \in \mathbb P^1\)
730	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
731	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)). Write \(f := \varphi^{\#} t_\infty
732	  \in K(C)\) for the pullback of \(t_\infty\) along \(\varphi\). Then \(f\) is
733	  nonzero (because \(\varphi\) is non-constant; equivalently \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
734	  is a proper closed subscheme of \(C\)) and the degree of the positive
735	  part of the principal divisor of \(f\) equals the function-field
736	  extension degree:
737	  \[
738	    \deg\bigl((\mathrm{div}(f))_0\bigr)
739	    \;=\; \bigl[\,K(C) \,:\, \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,\bigr]
740	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
741	  \]
742	  Equivalently, \((\mathrm{div}(f))_0 = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\) (the effective
743	  preimage divisor of \(\infty\)) and \(\deg(\varphi^{*}[\infty]) = \deg(\varphi)\).
744	\end{lemma}
745	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The morphism \(f \colon X \to Y\) is finite,
746	% so locally on \(Y\) corresponds to a finite extension of Dedekind domains
747	% \(A \subset B\). For each closed point \(P \in Y\) the ramification--inertia
748	% formula gives \(\sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q = [K(X) : K(Y)]\), where the sum runs
749	% over closed points \(Q \in X\) above \(P\). Summing this identity over
750	% \(P \in Y\) using the affine cover and noting that \(D = \sum_P n_P [P]\)
751	% gives \(\deg(f^\ast D) = \sum_{P} n_P \sum_{Q | P} e_Q f_Q
752	% = \sum_P n_P [K(X) : K(Y)] = [K(X) : K(Y)] \deg(D)\)."
753	\begin{proof}
754	  Following Hartshorne's Proposition~II.6.9 specialised to the divisor
755	  \(D = [\infty]\) on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). A non-constant morphism
756	  between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is automatically finite (the
757	  image is a closed irreducible subset that is not a point and not empty,
758	  hence all of \(\mathbb P^1\); a surjective morphism between smooth proper
759	  irreducible curves of equal dimension is proper + quasi-finite, hence
760	  finite; cf.\ Hartshorne II.6.8). Hence the function-field extension
761	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) is finite of degree
762	  \(d := [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)] = \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\).
763	  Pick an affine open \(\mathrm{Spec}(A) \subset \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) containing
764	  \(\infty\); explicitly take \(A = \bar k[t_\infty]\) the standard chart at
765	  \(\infty\), with \(\infty\) corresponding to the maximal ideal
766	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty = (t_\infty) \subset A\). The preimage
767	  \(\mathrm{Spec}(B) := \varphi^{-1}(\mathrm{Spec}(A))\) is an affine open of \(C\)
768	  with \(B\) a finite (and integrally closed, by \(C\) smooth) extension of
769	  \(A\); both are Dedekind domains. Apply
770	  \texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia} (Mathlib,
771	  \texttt{Mathlib.NumberTheory.RamificationInertia.Basic}) to the
772	  ring-theoretic extension \(A \to B\) at the maximal ideal
773	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\):
774	  \[
775	    \sum_{Q \,\colon\, \mathfrak m_\infty \subset Q} e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty} \cdot f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}
776	    \;=\; \dim_{A / \mathfrak m_\infty}\!\bigl(B / \mathfrak m_\infty B\bigr)
777	    \;=\; \dim_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) \;=\; d,
778	  \]
779	  where the second equality uses
780	  \texttt{Ideal.finrank\_quotient\_map} (which identifies the residue-field
781	  quotient with the function-field extension degree for a Dedekind extension
782	  at a maximal ideal). The closed points \(Q \subset \mathrm{Spec}(B)\) above
783	  \(\mathfrak m_\infty\) are precisely the closed points
784	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty) \subset C\); the ramification index
785	  \(e_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is the order \(\ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\)
786	  of the pulled-back local parameter at \(P\); the residue degree
787	  \(f_{Q | \mathfrak m_\infty}\) is \([\kappa(P) : \bar k] = 1\) (every closed
788	  point of \(C\) over \(\bar k\) has residue field \(\bar k\)). Therefore
789	  \[
790	    \sum_{P \,\in\, \varphi^{-1}(\infty)} \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \;=\; d
791	    \;=\; \mathrm{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C).
792	  \]
793	  The left-hand side equals the degree of the positive part of
794	  \(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty)\), because: at every closed point
795	  \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the order is \(\geq 1\) (pullback of a local
796	  parameter under a finite morphism is itself a uniformiser-power, hence
797	  has positive order at every preimage); at every closed point
798	  \(P \in C \setminus \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\) the rational function
799	  \(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty\) is regular and nonzero (because \(t_\infty\) is
800	  regular and nonzero on \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)), hence the
801	  order at such a \(P\) is \(\geq 0\); the points where the order is
802	  \emph{strictly} positive are exactly the points of \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty)\).
803	  Therefore
804	  \(\,(\mathrm{div}(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0 = \sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
805	  \ord_P(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty) \cdot [P] = \varphi^{*}[\infty]\,\), and
806	  taking degree completes the proof.
807	\end{proof}
808	\section{Divisor class group}
809	We follow Hartshorne II.6 (pp.~131--132) and define the divisor class group
810	of a curve as the cokernel of the principal-divisor homomorphism. This is
811	the \emph{Picard group} of \(C\) in the curve-specific sense; the line-bundle
812	interpretation \(\Cl(C) \cong \Pic(C)\) requires the construction of
813	\(\mathcal O_C(D)\), which lives in the sibling chapter \texttt{RR.3} and is
814	out of scope here.
815	\begin{definition}
816	\leanok
817	[Linear equivalence of divisors]
818	  \label{def:linear_equivalence}
819	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence}
820	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~131 (definition of linear equivalence
821	  % and of \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)) (read from
822	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 148).
823	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Definition. Let \(X\) satisfy \((\ast)\). Two divisors \(D\)
824	  % and \(D'\) are said to be \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\),
825	  % if \(D - D'\) is a principal divisor. The group \(\operatorname{Div} X\) of
826	  % all divisors divided by the subgroup of principal divisors is called
827	  % the \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\), and is denoted by
828	  % \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)."
829	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, p.~131 (linear equivalence and
830	  \(\operatorname{Cl} X\)).}
831	  Let \(X\) satisfy \((*)\). Two Weil divisors \(D, D' \in \mathrm{Div}(X)\) are
832	  \emph{linearly equivalent}, written \(D \sim D'\), if and only if there
833	  exists \(f \in K(X)^{\times}\) with
834	  \[
835	    D - D' \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f) \;\in\; \mathrm{Div}(X).
836	  \]
837	  The relation \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation on \(\mathrm{Div}(X)\) (the
838	  reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity all transport from the analogous
839	  identities \(\mathrm{div}(1) = 0\), \(\mathrm{div}(f^{-1}) = -\mathrm{div}(f)\), $\mathrm{div}(fg) =
840	  \mathrm{div}(f) + \mathrm{div}(g)$ via \ref{thm:principal_hom}); the corresponding
841	  quotient group \(\Cl(X) := \mathrm{Div}(X) / \mathrm{im}(\div)\) is the
842	  \emph{divisor class group} of \(X\). On a smooth proper curve \(C\) over
843	  \(\bar k\), \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} implies that the degree map
844	  \(\deg \colon \mathrm{Div}(C) \to \mathbb Z\) descends to a homomorphism
845	  \(\deg \colon \Cl(C) \to \mathbb Z\).
846	\end{definition}
847	\section{Out of scope}
848	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.1} and
849	are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
850	\begin{itemize}
851	  \item The line-bundle construction \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) associated to a Weil
852	    divisor \(D\), the calculation of its global sections, and the
853	    identification \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when
854	    \(D \sim D'\), lives in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
855	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added).
856	  \item The Riemann--Roch dimension formula
857	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) and its genus-\(0\) specialisation
858	    \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) live in \texttt{RR.2}
859	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RRFormula.tex}, to be added).
860	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
861	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)''
862	    (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5) is the headline of \texttt{RR.4}
863	    (sibling chapter \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added)
864	    and assembles \texttt{RR.1}/\texttt{RR.2}/\texttt{RR.3} into the
865	    \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} morphism into \(\mathbb P^1\) together
866	    with its degree-\(1\) identification.
867	  \item \emph{Cartier divisors} (locally principal divisors on a possibly
868	    singular scheme) are out of scope: the project's curves are smooth, so
869	    Weil and Cartier divisors coincide, and the simpler Weil form suffices.
870	    A future Mathlib upstream PR could introduce the Cartier formalism.
871	  \item \emph{Higher-codimension cycles} (the Chow group
872	    \(\bigoplus_i \mathrm{CH}^i(X)\)) are out of scope: this chapter is
873	    strictly curve-focused.
874	  \item The proof of \ref{thm:principal_deg_zero} factors through two
875	    auxiliary statements (``finite morphism induced by a non-constant
876	    rational function'' and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite
877	    pullback'', Hartshorne II.6.9) which are themselves separable
878	    sub-lemmas of \texttt{RR.1}; they are flagged in the proof body but
879	    deliberately not unfolded as standalone declarations here.
880	\end{itemize}

80	\section{The morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from two global sections}
81	\begin{lemma}
82	
83	\leanok
84	[Morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) from a globally generating pair of sections]
85	  \label{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}
86	  
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme over a field \(\bar k\), let \(\mathcal L\) be an invertible
89	  sheaf on \(X\), and let \(s_0, s_1 \in H^0(X, \mathcal L)\) be two global
90	  sections whose common zero locus is empty:
91	  \[
92	    \{x \in X : s_0(x) = 0 \text{ and } s_1(x) = 0\} \;=\; \emptyset.
93	  \]
94	  (Equivalently, \(s_0\) and \(s_1\) generate \(\mathcal L\) at every point.) Then
95	  there exists a unique morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
96	  \[
97	    \varphi_{(s_0, s_1)} \colon X \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
98	  \]
99	  whose pullback of \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(1)\) is \(\mathcal L\) and whose
100	  pullbacks of the standard coordinate sections $x_0, x_1 \in
101	  H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal O(1))\( are \)s_0\( and \)s_1$ respectively. On the
102	  basic-open chart $D_+(x_0) = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k} \subset
103	  \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\( the morphism \)\varphi_{(s_0, s_1)}\( is the map ``\)x
104	  \mapsto s_1(x) / s_0(x)\(''; on the chart \)D_+(x_1)\( it is the map ``\)x
105	  \mapsto s_0(x) / s_1(x)$''; the two definitions agree on the overlap
106	  \(D_+(x_0) \cap D_+(x_1) = \mathbb G_{m, \bar k}\) by the cocycle identity
107	  \((s_1 / s_0) \cdot (s_0 / s_1) = 1\).
108	\end{lemma}
109	\begin{proof}
110	  This is the standard linear-system construction in the form of
111	  Hartshorne's Theorem~II.7.1 (``Morphisms to \(\mathbb P^n\)''), specialised to
112	  \(n = 1\). Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) ships the corresponding API
113	  under the name
114	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} in
115	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}:
116	  \begin{quote}
117	  \small
118	  \texttt{def Proj.fromOfGlobalSections \{\(\sigma\) A\} [CommRing A]
119	    [SetLike \(\sigma\) A] [AddSubgroupClass \(\sigma\) A] (\(\mathcal A\) :
120	    \(\mathbb N\) → \(\sigma\)) [GradedRing \(\mathcal A\)] \{X : Scheme\}\\
121	    \phantom{}\quad(f : A →+* X.presheaf.obj \(\top\))\\
122	    \phantom{}\quad(hf : Ideal.map f (HomogeneousIdeal.irrelevant \(\mathcal A\)).toIdeal = \(\top\)) :\\
123	    \phantom{}\quad(X \(\longrightarrow\) Proj \(\mathcal A\))}
124	  \end{quote}
125	  Specialising to \(\mathcal A = \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) with the standard $\mathbb
126	  N\(-grading by total degree, the inputs are a graded \)\bar k$-algebra
127	  homomorphism \(f \colon \bar k[x_0, x_1] \to H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) and a
128	  proof that the irrelevant ideal \((x_0, x_1) \cdot \bar k[x_0, x_1]\) maps to
129	  the unit ideal of \(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X)\) (i.e.\ no common zeros). The
130	  resulting morphism lands in $\mathrm{Proj}\,\bar k[x_0, x_1] = \mathbb
131	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (Hartshorne~II, Example~7.1.3, the construction of \)\mathbb
132	  P^n\( as a \)\mathrm{Proj}\(). When \)\mathcal L$ is non-trivial the present
133	  lemma's signature is reduced to the Mathlib API by working on the open
134	  cover \(X = D(s_0) \cup D(s_1)\), where on each chart the sections become
135	  honest global functions (\(s_1 / s_0\) on \(D(s_0)\), \(s_0 / s_1\) on \(D(s_1)\),
136	  agreeing on the overlap). The chart-glue is performed by the
137	  project-bespoke wrapper
138	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphismToP1OfGlobalSections}, whose body
139	  in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} chains
140	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} with the chart-restriction-and-glue
141	  pattern of \texttt{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}.
142	  Uniqueness follows from the standard separation argument for morphisms to
143	  a separated scheme: two morphisms \(\varphi, \psi \colon X \to \mathbb P^1\)
144	  with \(\varphi^* x_i = \psi^* x_i\) for \(i = 0, 1\) agree on the basic-open
145	  charts \(D_+(x_0), D_+(x_1)\) (where they pull back the affine coordinates to
146	  the same rational function), and these charts cover \(\mathbb P^1\), so the
147	  two morphisms agree globally by gluing.
148	\end{proof}
149	\section{Degree of a morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via its pole divisor}
150	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase Pin 2 corrective; supersedes iter-182 narrative).
151	% `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \varphi` is the Weil divisor \(\varphi^*[\infty]\),
152	% defined as `positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))` in Lean.
153	% The supporting `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` def lives in
154	% `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (\ref{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart}); the
155	% Hartshorne~II.6.9 identity that powers the existential degree statement
156	% lives there as \ref{lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank}.
157	\begin{lemma}
158	
159	\leanok
160	[Degree of a non-constant morphism to \(\mathbb P^1\) via the preimage of \(\infty\)]
161	  \label{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor}
162	  
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.morphism_degree_via_pole_divisor}
164	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV \S2, opening paragraph of "Hurwitz's Theorem"
165	  % (definition of the degree of a finite morphism of curves) (read from
166	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
168	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
169	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
170	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.2, p.~299, opening of \S2.}
171	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) be a non-constant morphism
172	  from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\). Then
173	  \(\varphi\) is finite, its function-field extension
174	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\) (the canonical
175	  \(\varphi\)-induced map, supplied as an
176	  \texttt{[Algebra (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField C.functionField]}
177	  instance argument on the Lean side) is finite, and there exists a Weil
178	  divisor \(D \in \Div(C)\) such that
179	  \[
180	    D \;=\; \varphi^*[\infty]
181	    \quad\text{and}\quad
182	    \deg(D) \;=\; [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,].
183	  \]
184	  Equivalently, the morphism \(\varphi\) satisfies
185	  \[
186	    [\,K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)\,] \;=\;
187	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
188	                  \ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [P]\,\right),
189	  \]
190	  where \(t_\infty \in K(\mathbb P^1)\) is a local parameter at \(\infty\)
191	  (equivalently \(t_\infty = 1/u\) for the standard affine coordinate \(u\) on
192	  \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\)); and for any closed point
193	  \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) the same identification gives
194	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C):\bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\).
195	  \emph{Convention on the pole divisor.} The Weil divisor
196	  \(\varphi^*[\infty]\) is exposed in Lean as
197	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor \(\varphi\)}, a
198	  noncomputable definition introduced (as a typed \texttt{sorry}) at the
199	  iter-182 plan-phase signature strengthening of this lemma. The body of
200	  \texttt{poleDivisor} is queued for iter-183+ closure via the standard
201	  affine-chart construction (\texttt{Ideal.sum\_ramification\_inertia}
202	  in Mathlib, applied at each closed point \(P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)\)
203	  to read off the local ramification multiplicity
204	  \(\ord_P(\varphi^* t_\infty)\) from the maximal ideal at \(\infty\) in
205	  \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)). On the Lean side, the existential output of
206	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} packages
207	  \(D = \texttt{Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor}\;\varphi\) together with
208	  \(\deg(D) = \operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(\mathbb P^1)} K(C)\),
209	  mirroring the OCofP pattern in which a typed-sorry def
210	  (\texttt{def:lineBundleAtClosedPoint_toFunctionField}) is introduced
211	  upstream of its consumer lemma.
212	\end{lemma}
213	% NOTE (iter-190 plan-phase, supersedes iter-189 review NOTE):
214	% Pin 2 corrective COMMITTED — Option (a). `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` is
215	% being refactored to return the *positive part* of the principal divisor
216	% of `\varphi^{\#} t_\infty`, i.e. `(div(\varphi^{\#} t_\infty))_0
217	% = \varphi^*[\infty]`. The supporting Weil-divisor `positivePart`
218	% definition lives in `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` as
219	% `def:WeilDivisor_positivePart`; the substantive Hartshorne~II.6.9
220	% identity that powers `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is now pinned as
221	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` in `WeilDivisor.tex`.
222	% With these substrates in place, `lem:degree_via_pole_divisor` is
223	% mathematically correct as stated: the body's existential witness `D`
224	% is `Hom.poleDivisor \varphi = positivePart (principal (\varphi^{\#}
225	% t_\infty))`, and the degree identity is a one-line corollary of
226	% `lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`. iter-190 prover lane
227	% (Lane I) implements the refactor:
228	%   1. New def `Scheme.WeilDivisor.positivePart` in `WeilDivisor.lean`
229	%      (~10-15 LOC; lattice `D \sqcup 0`).
230	%   2. New lemma
231	%      `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank` as a
232	%      named typed-sorry pin in `WeilDivisor.lean` (~5 LOC signature; body
233	%      ~50-80 LOC via affine-chart `Ideal.sum_ramification_inertia` route
234	%      owed iter-191+).
235	%   3. Refactor `Scheme.Hom.poleDivisor` body in `RationalCurveIso.lean`
236	%      from `principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg` to
237	%      `positivePart (principal (algebraMap _ _ t.val) halg)` (~5 LOC).
238	%   4. Close `Hom.poleDivisor_degree_eq_finrank` body via a 1-line
239	%      rewrite consuming (2) (~3 LOC).
240	% Net iter-190 Lane I: 1 axiom-clean closure (Pin 2 body, modulo named
241	% typed sorry on (2)); 1 NEW named typed-sorry pin
242	% (`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`) which is itself an
243	% iter-191+ Hartshorne II.6.9 substrate target. See iter-190 sidecar
244	% `iter/iter-190/plan.md` for the full rationale.
245	\begin{proof}
246	  \uses{def:WeilDivisor_positivePart, lem:degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank, def:principal_divisor, def:divisor_degree, thm:principal_deg_zero}
247	  A non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves over \(\bar k\) is
248	  automatically finite. Indeed, the image of \(\varphi\) is a closed
249	  irreducible subset of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) that is not a point (else
250	  \(\varphi\) would be constant) and not empty, so it is all of $\mathbb
251	  P^1_{\bar k}\( (the only proper irreducible closed subset of \)\mathbb
252	  P^1_{\bar k}\( is a point); hence \)\varphi$ is surjective. A surjective
253	  morphism between smooth proper irreducible curves of equal dimension is
254	  finite (proper + quasi-finite, where quasi-finiteness comes from the
255	  fact that \(\varphi\) has zero-dimensional fibres: a positive-dimensional
256	  fibre would force a one-dimensional irreducible component in the fibre,
257	  contradicting irreducibility of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(C\) at
258	  generic points). Hence the function-field extension
259	  \(\bar k(\mathbb P^1) = \varphi^\# K(\mathbb P^1) \hookrightarrow K(C)\)
260	  is finite.
261	  The identification \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\) for
262	  any closed point \(Q \in \mathbb P^1\) is the multiplicativity of the
263	  degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9): for a finite morphism
264	  \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) of smooth proper curves and any divisor $D \in
265	  \Div(C')\(, \)\deg(\varphi^* D) = \deg(\varphi) \cdot \deg(D)$. Specialising
266	  to \(D = [Q]\) (a divisor of degree \(1\) since \(\kappa(Q) = \bar k\)) gives
267	  \(\deg(\varphi^*[Q]) = \deg(\varphi)\). Equivalently, the degree of
268	  \(\varphi\) equals the number of preimages of any closed point of $\mathbb
269	  P^1\(, counted with the ramification multiplicities \)\ord_P(\varphi^*
270	  t_\infty)\( at the preimage points \)P \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)$.
271	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The lemma threads two auxiliary inputs:
272	  ``non-constant morphism between smooth proper curves is finite'' (the
273	  surjective-and-proper-and-quasi-finite argument above; Hartshorne~II.6.8)
274	  and ``multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback'' (Hartshorne~II.6.9
275	  and the multiplicativity of \(\deg\) under composition of \(\div\) and
276	  \(\deg\); the closely related ``\(\mathrm{div}(f)\) has degree zero'' specialisation
277	  is \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}). Both are separable
278	  sub-lemmas inside \texttt{RR.4}'s Lean file
279	  (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean}) and are
280	  slotted for closure in a follow-up iteration after the
281	  \texttt{morphism\_degree\_via\_pole\_divisor} skeleton lands.
282	\end{proof}
283	\section{A degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism}
284	% NOTE (iter-181 Lane I signature refinement; see analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md
285	% Decision 2, verdict DIVERGE_INTENTIONALLY). The iter-177 file-skeleton
286	% placeholder hypothesis "Nonempty (C'.functionField ≃+* C.functionField)"
287	% (an abstract function-field iso, unrelated to \(\varphi\)) was strictly
288	% weaker than the birational-extension body needs and was refined in
289	% iter-181 to the explicit function-field-extension-degree formulation
290	% [K(C):K(C')] = 1, i.e. Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField
291	% = 1, with C.functionField a C'.functionField-algebra via the canonical
292	% \varphi-induced map (Scheme.Hom.stalkMap at the generic point composed
293	% with IsFractionRing.lift). The proof-prose body below (the Hartshorne
294	% I.6.12 chain + scheme-theoretic alternative argument) is unchanged ---
295	% only the input shape changes.
296	\begin{lemma}
297	
298	\leanok
299	[Degree-\(1\) morphism between smooth proper curves is an isomorphism]
300	  \label{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso}
301	  
302	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso_of_degree_one}
303	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], I \S6 Corollary~6.12 (function-field-to-curve
304	  % equivalence of categories) (read from
305	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 62, doc p.~45)
306	  % + [Hartshorne], IV \S2 opening paragraph (degree of a finite morphism
307	  % of curves is the function-field extension degree) (read from
308	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 316, doc p.~299).
309	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Corollary 6.12. \emph{The following three categories are
310	  % equivalent:}
311	  % (i) \emph{nonsingular projective curves, and dominant morphisms;}
312	  % (ii) \emph{quasi-projective curves, and dominant rational maps;}
313	  % (iii) \emph{function fields of dimension 1 over \(k\), and \(k\)-homomorphisms.}"
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall that the \emph{degree} of a finite morphism
315	  % \(f: X \to Y\) of curves is defined to be the degree \([K(X):K(Y)]\) of the
316	  % extension of function fields (II, \S 6)."
317	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, I.6 Corollary~6.12 (p.~45) + IV.2 opening
318	  (p.~299).}
319	  Let \(C, C'\) be smooth proper geometrically irreducible curves over an
320	  algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), and let \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) be
321	  a non-constant morphism whose induced function-field extension is of
322	  degree one,
323	  \[
324	    [\,K(C) : K(C')\,] \;=\; 1,
325	  \]
326	  formally
327	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{K(C')} K(C) = 1\) in the Lean signature.
328	  Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
329	  \emph{Convention on the function-field map.} At each call-site of this
330	  lemma, the \(K(C')\)-algebra structure on \(K(C)\) used to interpret the
331	  degree \([K(C) : K(C')]\) is the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
332	  function-field map, i.e.\ the composite
333	  \[
334	    K(C') \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{germ}\;}\;
335	    \mathcal O_{C', \varphi(\xi_C)}
336	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\varphi^\#\;}\;
337	    \mathcal O_{C, \xi_C}
338	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\text{frac.}\,\text{lift}\;}\; K(C)
339	  \]
340	  of the germ at the image of the generic point \(\xi_C \in C\) with the
341	  stalk-map of \(\varphi\) at \(\xi_C\) and the fraction-field lift at
342	  \(\xi_C\) (per \texttt{analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md}, Decision~2). On
343	  the Lean side this canonical instance is carried as a
344	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
345	  to \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one}; consumers
346	  supply it explicitly at each call-site.
347	\end{lemma}
348	% NOTE (iter-189 review): Pin 3 Step 1 (function-field iso K(C') ≃+* K(C)
349	% from `Module.finrank = 1`) closed axiom-clean inline iter-189 (~10 LOC via
350	% `Subalgebra.bot_eq_top_of_finrank_eq_one` + `Algebra.surjective_algebraMap_iff`
351	% + `RingHom.injective` + `RingEquiv.ofBijective`). Step 2 (scheme-level lift
352	% via `Scheme.Hom.toNormalization`) remains a typed sorry in the body, with 4
353	% sub-obligations (a)-(d) documented inline at the sorry. Iter-190+ closure
354	% budget ~80-150 LOC per `analogies/ratcurveiso-pin3.md`.
355	\begin{proof}
356	  The function-field hypothesis \([K(C) : K(C')] = 1\) states that the
357	  canonical \(\varphi\)-induced map \(\varphi^\# \colon K(C') \to K(C)\) is a
358	  finite extension of degree one; since \(K(C')\) is a field and the
359	  extension has dimension one as a \(K(C')\)-vector space, \(\varphi^\#\) is
360	  an isomorphism of function fields \(K(C') \cong K(C)\). (Equivalently, by
361	  the IV.2-opening identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C):K(C')]\), this is
362	  the statement \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\); the morphism \(\varphi\) is finite in
363	  this case by the standard argument that a non-constant morphism of
364	  smooth proper curves is finite, identical to the
365	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} preamble.)
366	  By Hartshorne's Corollary~I.6.12, the assignment \(C \mapsto \bar k(C)\) is
367	  an equivalence of categories between smooth projective curves over $\bar
368	  k$ (with dominant morphisms) and function fields of transcendence degree
369	  one over \(\bar k\) (with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms). Under this equivalence,
370	  the isomorphism \(\bar k(C') \cong \bar k(C)\) produced by the previous
371	  paragraph lifts to a unique isomorphism of smooth proper curves $C \to
372	  C'\(, and this isomorphism agrees with \)\varphi$ on the generic point (by
373	  construction: it is the inverse-image of \(\varphi^\#\) under the
374	  equivalence). The two morphisms \(C \to C'\) that agree on the generic
375	  point of the integral scheme \(C\) agree everywhere (a morphism out of an
376	  integral scheme to a separated scheme is determined by its restriction
377	  to the generic point, Hartshorne~II Ex.~4.2). Hence \(\varphi\) is itself
378	  the lift, and is therefore an isomorphism.
379	  \emph{Alternative direct argument.} Without invoking the
380	  equivalence-of-categories framing, the same conclusion is obtained
381	  scheme-theoretically: a finite degree-\(1\) morphism $\varphi \colon C \to
382	  C'$ between integral normal noetherian schemes is an isomorphism.
383	  Indeed, the direct image \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C\) is a coherent
384	  \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module that is generically of rank $\deg(\varphi) =
385	  1\(, and an inclusion \)\mathcal O_{C'} \hookrightarrow \varphi_*
386	  \mathcal O_C\( of coherent rank-\)1$ sheaves whose generic ranks agree
387	  is an isomorphism over the locus where the quotient
388	  \(\varphi_* \mathcal O_C / \mathcal O_{C'}\) has trivial stalk. The
389	  quotient is a torsion coherent \(\mathcal O_{C'}\)-module of generic rank
390	  \(\deg(\varphi) - 1 = 0\), hence is itself torsion-free (a
391	  finitely-generated torsion module over a Dedekind domain of generic
392	  rank zero is zero), so vanishes. Therefore $\varphi_* \mathcal O_C =
393	  \mathcal O_{C'}\( and \)\varphi$ is the structure morphism of an
394	  isomorphism. Cf.\ Stacks tag~\textbf{0AVX} for the categorical version.
395	  \emph{Sub-build note.} The Lean signature
396	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.iso\_of\_degree\_one} consumes a
397	  non-constant morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to C'\) together with a
398	  \texttt{[Algebra C'.functionField C.functionField]} instance argument
399	  (the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced function-field map) and the
400	  function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis
401	  \texttt{Module.finrank C'.functionField C.functionField = 1}, and
402	  produces an isomorphism \(C \cong C'\) in the slice category
403	  \(\mathtt{Over (Spec (.of\ kbar))}\). The proof body threads the Mathlib
404	  API \texttt{IsIntegralHom.iso\_of\_isUnit} (or the equivalent
405	  \texttt{IsAffineHom + Finite + RankOne} chain) and, where needed, the
406	  project-bespoke ``finite-morphism-of-degree-one is an isomorphism''
407	  helper. The Hartshorne~I.6.12 equivalence-of-categories framing is the
408	  intended high-level proof structure; the scheme-theoretic argument
409	  above is the spelled-out form for the Lean body.
410	\end{proof}
411	\section{Headline: genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)}
412	\begin{theorem}
413	
414	\leanok
415	[Genus-\(0\) curve over \(\bar k\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\)]
416	  \label{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}
417	  
418	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero_curve_iso_P1}
419	  % already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so conversely
420	  % suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two distinct
421	  % points of \(X\) and apply Riemann-Roch to the divisor \(D = P - Q\).
422	  % Since \(\deg(K - D) = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have \(l(K - D) = 0\),
423	  % and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of degree 0, so by
424	  % (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words \(P \sim Q\). But this implies
425	  % \(X\) is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
426	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
427	  Let \(\bar k\) be an algebraically closed field, and let \(C\) be a smooth
428	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\).
429	  Then there exists an isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
430	  \[
431	    \varphi \colon C \stackrel{\sim}{\longrightarrow} \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}.
432	  \]
433	  Concretely, the Lean signature
434	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} (the existing pinned
435	  target at \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:290}) is
436	  closed by the proof below; the existing interface block
437	  \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} of
438	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr} is its consumer-side restatement,
439	  pinning the same Lean declaration.
440	\end{theorem}
441	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the verbatim Example IV.1.3.5 text in the
442	% statement block above (Hartshorne states the classification and its
443	% proof inside the single Example; the proof is the second half beginning
444	% "We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) \ldots"). The present
445	% proof body restates Hartshorne's argument in the project's notation
446	% (linear-system construction \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C
447	% ([P]))\(, degree-\)1$ identification, isomorphism).
448	\begin{proof}
449	  Over the algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) the smooth proper
450	  geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) has closed points, all of which are
451	  \(\bar k\)-rational (the residue field of a closed point of a curve over
452	  \(\bar k\) is a finite extension of \(\bar k\), hence is \(\bar k\) itself by
453	  algebraic closedness). Fix one such \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P \in C\);
454	  this is the marked point used to build the linear system below.
455	  \emph{Step 1: non-constant function \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\).}
456	  By Hartshorne's Riemann--Roch in genus zero
457	  (\texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}) applied to the
458	  effective divisor \([P]\) of degree \(1\), we obtain
459	  \(\ell([P]) = \deg([P]) + 1 = 2\). The constant section
460	  \(1 \in \bar k \cdot 1 \subset H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) accounts for a
461	  one-dimensional \(\bar k\)-subspace; the two-dimensional quotient supplies
462	  a non-constant section $f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \setminus
463	  \bar k \cdot 1$. This is the corollary
464	  \texttt{cor:nonconstant\_function\_genus\_zero} of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling
465	  chapter, \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OCofP.tex}).
466	  \emph{Step 2: morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1\) via the linear system \((1, f)\).}
467	  The pair \((1, f) \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))^2\) generates the
468	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) at every point of \(C\): at points
469	  \(Q \in C \setminus \{P\}\) the section \(1\) does not vanish in
470	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])_Q \cong \mathcal O_{C, Q}\), and at the marked point
471	  \(P\) the section \(f\) does not vanish in \(\mathcal O_C([P])_P\) (because \(f\)
472	  has a pole of order exactly \(1\) at \(P\) in the rational-function
473	  picture: \(f \in K(C) \setminus \bar k\) with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = [P]\)).
474	  Therefore by \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections} there
475	  exists a unique morphism
476	  \[
477	    \varphi \;:=\; \varphi_{(1, f)} \colon C \longrightarrow \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}
478	  \]
479	  with \(\varphi^* x_0 = 1\) and \(\varphi^* x_1 = f\) (so the pullback of
480	  the standard ratio coordinate \(u = x_1 / x_0\) on $\mathbb P^1 \setminus
481	  \{\infty\}\( is \)f$, and the pullback of the inverse coordinate
482	  \(t_\infty = x_0 / x_1 = 1 / f\) is the local parameter \(1/f\) at \(P\)).
483	  \emph{Step 3: \(\deg(\varphi) = 1\) via the pole divisor of \(f\).} The
484	  morphism \(\varphi\) is non-constant (since \(f\) is non-constant as a
485	  rational function on \(C\), the pullback \(\varphi^* u = f\) takes
486	  infinitely many values, ruling out a constant \(\varphi\)). By
487	  \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor} applied to \(\varphi\),
488	  \[
489	    \deg(\varphi)
490	    \;=\;
491	    \deg\!\left(\,\sum_{Q \in \varphi^{-1}(\infty)}
492	                  \ord_Q(\varphi^* t_\infty) \cdot [Q]\,\right).
493	  \]
494	  We compute the pole divisor of \(f\) as a rational function on \(C\):
495	  since \(f \in H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\) with \(f\) non-constant and
496	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) the line bundle of the effective divisor \([P]\), the
497	  rational-function \(f\) has pole divisor \((f)_\infty = [P]\) (the
498	  unique pole of order \(1\) at \(P\), no other poles; this is the standard
499	  identification of \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\) with the rational functions
500	  \(g \in K(C)\) satisfying \(\mathrm{div}(g) + D \geq 0\), specialised to
501	  \(D = [P]\)). Hence \(\varphi^{-1}(\infty) = \{P\}\) with multiplicity~\(1\)
502	  and
503	  \[
504	    \deg(\varphi) \;=\; \deg([P]) \;=\; 1.
505	  \]
506	  \emph{Step 4: \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.} By Hartshorne's IV.2
507	  identification \(\deg(\varphi) = [K(C) : \bar k(\mathbb P^1)]\), the
508	  degree-one conclusion of Step~3 rewrites as
509	  \(\operatorname{Module.finrank}_{\bar k(\mathbb P^1)} K(C) = 1\) (with
510	  the canonical \(\varphi\)-induced
511	  \texttt{[Algebra \,(ProjectiveLineBar kbar).functionField\,
512	   C.functionField]} instance). By
513	  \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} applied to the non-constant
514	  morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (smooth proper
515	  geometrically irreducible curves over \(\bar k\) on both sides), under
516	  this function-field-extension-degree-one hypothesis, \(\varphi\) is an
517	  isomorphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes.
518	  This produces the required isomorphism \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\)
519	  closing the headline target
520	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1}.
521	  \emph{Comparison with Hartshorne's original argument.} Hartshorne's
522	  Example~IV.1.3.5 phrases the argument in terms of two distinct points
523	  \(P, Q \in C\) and the degree-zero divisor \(D = P - Q\), deriving \(P \sim Q\)
524	  and concluding via II.6.10.1 that \(C\) is rational; combined with I.6.12
525	  this yields \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\). The present proof restates the same
526	  combinatorial content via the more directly formalisable linear-system
527	  construction: instead of going through the \(P \sim Q\) statement and
528	  invoking II.6.10.1 as a black box, we produce the explicit morphism
529	  \(\varphi \colon C \to \mathbb P^1\) from the two sections \((1, f)\) of
530	  \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) given by Riemann--Roch and identify its degree
531	  directly. The two arguments are mathematically equivalent and have the
532	  same dependency footprint (Riemann--Roch in genus zero \(\Rightarrow\)
533	  \(\ell([P]) = 2\) \(\Rightarrow\) non-constant \(f\) \(\Rightarrow\) degree-\(1\)
534	  map to \(\mathbb P^1\) \(\Rightarrow\) iso); the linear-system phrasing maps
535	  more cleanly onto the Mathlib \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} API and
536	  the project's existing Weil-divisor/degree pipeline of \texttt{RR.1}.
537	\end{proof}
538	\begin{remark}
539	[Drop of \texttt{sorryAx} on the genus-\(0\) Albanese keystone]
540	  \label{rmk:rr4_unblocks_sorryAx_on_rigidity_genus0}
541	  Closure of \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} retires the
542	  \texttt{sorryAx} propagation tagged on
543	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}
544	  (\texttt{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of
545	  \texttt{chap:avr_for_rr}, body landed iter-166), whose
546	  \emph{first} of two upstream \texttt{sorry} sources is the
547	  \texttt{genusZero\_curve\_iso\_P1} target this chapter closes. The
548	  \emph{second} upstream is the helper
549	  \texttt{morphism\_P1\_to\_grpScheme\_const}'s residual sorries, tracked
550	  separately under the genus-\(0\) Albanese arm of \texttt{Jacobian.tex}.
551	\end{remark}
552	\section{Out of scope}
553	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4} and
554	are sequenced elsewhere:
555	\begin{itemize}
556	  \item The general ``smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) of
557	    genus \(0\) over a \emph{non-closed} field \(k\) with a \(k\)-rational point
558	    \(\cong \mathbb P^1_k\)'' statement is the \(\bar k \to k\) descent of
559	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso}. The descent is hosted in
560	    \texttt{Jacobian.tex} via \texttt{genusZeroWitness.key}; it consumes
561	    \texttt{chap:RiemannRoch_RationalCurveIso} together with Galois descent for
562	    smooth proper curves and is out of scope for \texttt{RR.4}.
563	  \item The Mathlib API \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Proj.fromOfGlobalSections}
564	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveSpectrum.Basic}) is
565	    consumed as a black box by
566	    \texttt{lem:morphism_to_p1_from_global_sections}; its own proof inside
567	    Mathlib (the universal property of \(\mathrm{Proj}\), graded-ring
568	    presentation of \(\mathbb P^n\)) is not re-derived here.
569	  \item Multiplicativity of degree under finite pullback (Hartshorne~II.6.9)
570	    is consumed as a black box by \texttt{lem:degree_via_pole_divisor};
571	    its proof is a separable sub-lemma threaded inside
572	    \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RationalCurveIso.lean} and is
573	    \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.4}'s blueprint statement (it lives at
574	    the same logical level as the closely related
575	    \texttt{thm:principal_deg_zero} of \texttt{RR.1}).
576	  \item The full equivalence of categories ``smooth projective curves over
577	    \(\bar k\) with dominant morphisms'' \(\cong\) ``function fields of
578	    transcendence degree~\(1\) over \(\bar k\) with \(\bar k\)-homomorphisms''
579	    (Hartshorne~I.6.12) is consumed as a high-level framing in
580	    \texttt{lem:degree_one_morphism_iso} but is \emph{not} re-formalised at
581	    the equivalence-of-categories level here. The proof body in the Lean
582	    file threads the spelled-out scheme-theoretic ``finite-degree-one
583	    morphism between integral normal schemes is an isomorphism''
584	    argument (Stacks~\textbf{0AVX}), which is the minimal-Mathlib-dependence
585	    pin compatible with the chapter's downstream needs.
586	  \item ``A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(\bar k\)
587	    has at least two distinct \(\bar k\)-points'' (the non-empty / infinite
588	    cardinality of \(C(\bar k)\)) is consumed implicitly by the present
589	    chapter but its formal statement lives in the project's curve API
590	    (\texttt{Genus.tex} / \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus}); the chapter
591	    does not restate it.
592	  \item Cohomology of the line bundle \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a curve
593	    (Hartshorne~III.5 + IV.1) is fully consumed via \texttt{RR.3}; in
594	    particular the \(H^1\)-vanishing of \(\mathcal O_C([P])\) on a genus-\(0\)
595	    curve is part of \texttt{RR.3}'s payload feeding
596	    \texttt{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero} of \texttt{RR.2}, and is not
597	    re-derived here.
598	\end{itemize}
