2	
3	\chapter{Vanishing of \(H^1\) for skyscraper sheaves on a curve (RR.2.H\(^1\))}
4	\label{chap:RR_H1Vanishing}
5	
6	% SOURCE: Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.1, Exercise~1.16
7	% (flasque sheaves), Exercise~1.17 (skyscraper sheaves); III.2,
8	% Proposition~2.5 (flasque sheaves are acyclic) (read from
9	% references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 67--68 and
10	% 225). All verbatim quotes pasted in the declaration blocks below were
11	% copied character-by-character from the rendered page images of the
12	% scanned PDF (no text layer is present).
13	
14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	
16	This chapter is the project-side build of the
17	\texttt{RR.2.H\(^1\)} sub-phase, committed in iter-188 of the strategy
18	revision: \emph{the first cohomology of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf
19	on a smooth proper curve vanishes}. Concretely, the chapter pins the
20	identity
21	\[
22	  \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
23	    (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0
24	\]
25	on a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\), where
26	\(P \in C\) is a closed point and the cohomology is computed through the
27	project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured \(\Ext\)-cohomology pipeline
28	\(\HModule \bar k\) (chapter~REF, sibling
29	\texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}).
30	
31	\paragraph{Why a dedicated chapter.} Mathlib (snapshot
32	\texttt{b80f227}) does \emph{not} ship a flasque-cohomology vanishing
33	lemma at the \(\Ext\)-cohomology level used by
34	\(\HModule \bar k\), nor a Serre-duality theorem for curves. The
35	genus-\(0\) critical path nevertheless needs the skyscraper
36	\(H^1\)-vanishing twice: once inside the Hartshorne~IV.1.3
37	\(\chi\)-additivity assembly
38	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_RRFormula}, the parent lemma
39	\texttt{eulerCharacteristic\_skyscraperSheaf}), and once inside the
40	\texttt{RR.3} chain that produces a non-constant section
41	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_OCofP}, the downstream consumer
42	\texttt{h1\_vanishing\_genusZero}). The present chapter unifies the
43	substrate behind both consumers into a single short, self-contained
44	project-side build.
45	
46	\paragraph{Strategy of the chapter.} The argument is the classical
47	Hartshorne~III~\S2 one: an injective resolution of a flasque sheaf
48	yields a flasque quotient at each stage (since both the injective object
49	and the original sheaf are flasque), and the global-sections functor is
50	exact on a short exact sequence of flasque sheaves. Iterating, every
51	derived functor of \(\Gamma\) vanishes on a flasque input in positive
52	degree. The chapter then specialises to the closed-point skyscraper
53	sheaf, which is flasque by inspection (it is the pushforward, along the
54	inclusion of a one-point closed subspace, of the constant sheaf on an
55	irreducible base — and pushforwards of flasque sheaves are flasque).
56	
57	\paragraph{Standing hypotheses.} Throughout this chapter, \(\bar k\) is
58	an algebraically closed field and \(C\) is a smooth proper geometrically
59	irreducible curve over \(\bar k\). The Lean signatures (planned location
60	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/H1Vanishing.lean}) carry the same
61	typeclass package as the rest of the project's curve API
62	(\(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\, 1\),
63	\(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{IsIntegral}\)). The
64	flasque-vanishing lemma of \S2 below is stated more generally for a
65	noetherian topological space (the natural generality of
66	Hartshorne~III.2.5); the curve specialisation is read off by instance
67	synthesis on the underlying space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\).
68	
69	\section{Flasque sheaves and their cohomology vanishing}
70	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_flasque}
71	
72	\begin{definition}
73	
74	\leanok
75	[Flasque sheaf]
76	  \label{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque}
78	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition of flasque
79	  % sheaf, opening paragraph) (read from
80	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67); III.2,
81	  % paragraph preceding Lemma~2.4 (Hartshorne's own recall of the
82	  % definition) (PDF page 224).
83	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Flasque Sheaves. A sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on a
84	  % topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
85	  % \(V \subseteq U\) of open sets, the restriction map
86	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Recall (II, Ex. 1.16) that a sheaf \(\mathscr{F}\) on
88	  % a topological space \(X\) is \emph{flasque} if for every inclusion
89	  % of open sets \(V \subseteq U\), the restriction map
90	  % \(\mathscr{F}(U) \to \mathscr{F}(V)\) is surjective."
91	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16 (definition); recalled
92	  III.2 preceding Lemma~2.4.}
93	  Let \((X, \mathcal O_X)\) be a ringed space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a
94	  sheaf of \(\mathcal O_X\)-modules on \(X\). We say that \(\mathcal F\)
95	  is \emph{flasque} if, for every inclusion of open sets
96	  \(V \subseteq U\) in \(X\), the restriction map
97	  \(\mathcal F(U) \to \mathcal F(V)\) is surjective.
98	
99	  In the project's \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured setup the predicate is
100	  applied to a sheaf
101	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;J\;(\Module \bar k)\), where \(J\) is the
102	  Grothendieck topology of opens on the underlying topological space of
103	  the curve \(C\). The surjectivity condition is read on the underlying
104	  presheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules; the project predicate
105	  \texttt{Scheme.IsFlasque} packages exactly this condition.
106	
107	  \paragraph{Lean signature scope.} The Lean predicate
108	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque} takes a sheaf
109	  \(\mathcal F : \mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
110	  (\Module \bar k)\) and asserts that for every pair \(V \le U\) of opens
111	  of \(X\), the restriction morphism
112	  \((\mathcal F.\mathtt{val.map}\;(\mathrm{leOp}\;V\;U)) :
113	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;U) \to
114	  \mathcal F.\mathtt{val.obj}\;(\mathrm{op}\;V)\) is surjective as a map of
115	  \(\bar k\)-modules.
116	\end{definition}
117	
118	\begin{lemma}
119	
120	\leanok
121	[Pushforward of a flasque sheaf is flasque]
122	  \label{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}
123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.pushforward}
124	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
125	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(d) (read from
126	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
127	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(f: X \to Y\) is a continuous map, and if
128	  % \(\mathscr{F}\) is a flasque sheaf on \(X\), then \(f_*\mathscr{F}\)
129	  % is a flasque sheaf on \(Y\)."
130	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(d).}
131	  Let \(f \colon X \to Y\) be a continuous map of topological spaces and
132	  let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque sheaf on \(X\). Then the pushforward
133	  sheaf \(f_*\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(Y\).
134	\end{lemma}
135	
136	\begin{proof}
137	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
138	  \leanok
139	  For an inclusion of opens \(V \subseteq U\) in \(Y\), the restriction
140	  map of \(f_*\mathcal F\) is by definition the restriction map of
141	  \(\mathcal F\) on the open sets \(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) of
142	  \(X\):
143	  \[
144	    (f_*\mathcal F)(U) \;=\; \mathcal F(f^{-1}(U))
145	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathcal F(f^{-1}(V) \subseteq f^{-1}(U))\;}\;
146	    \mathcal F(f^{-1}(V)) \;=\; (f_*\mathcal F)(V).
147	  \]
148	  Since \(\mathcal F\) is flasque on \(X\), the right-hand restriction is
149	  surjective, hence so is the left-hand one. Therefore \(f_*\mathcal F\)
150	  is flasque on \(Y\).
151	\end{proof}
152	
153	\begin{lemma}
154	
155	\leanok
156	[Constant sheaf on an irreducible space is flasque]
157	  \label{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
158	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.IsFlasque.constant_of_irreducible}
159	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
160	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.16(a) (read from
161	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 67).
162	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Show that a constant sheaf on an irreducible
163	  % topological space is flasque. See (I, \S1) for irreducible
164	  % topological spaces."
165	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.16(a).}
166	  Let \(X\) be an irreducible topological space and let \(A\) be a
167	  \(\bar k\)-module. Then the constant sheaf
168	  \((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;J\;\_)\;A\) on \(X\) is flasque.
169	\end{lemma}
170	
171	\begin{proof}
172	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
173	  On an irreducible topological space every non-empty open is connected
174	  and dense, so the constant presheaf
175	  \(U \mapsto A\) (with all non-empty restriction maps the identity)
176	  already satisfies the sheaf condition, and equals its sheafification.
177	  For every inclusion of non-empty opens \(V \subseteq U\), the
178	  restriction map is the identity on \(A\) — in particular surjective.
179	  For an inclusion involving the empty open, the target is the zero
180	  module, so surjectivity is immediate. Therefore the constant sheaf is
181	  flasque.
182	\end{proof}
183	
184	\begin{theorem}
185	
186	\leanok
187	[Flasque sheaves have vanishing higher cohomology]
188	  \label{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}
189	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.HModule_flasque_eq_zero}
190	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
191	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.2, Proposition~2.5 (read from
192	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 225).
193	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Proposition 2.5. \emph{If \(\mathscr{F}\) is a
194	  % flasque sheaf on a topological space \(X\), then
195	  % \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) for all \(i > 0\).}"
196	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.2, Proposition~2.5, p.~208.}
197	  Let \(X\) be a topological space and let \(\mathcal F\) be a flasque
198	  sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(X\), viewed as an object of
199	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
200	  (\Module \bar k)\). Then, for every \(i \ge 1\),
201	  \[
202	    \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \;=\; 0.
203	  \]
204	  In particular, \(\dim_{\bar k} \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
205	\end{theorem}
206	
207	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. Embed \(\mathscr{F}\) in an injective
208	% object \(\mathscr{I}\) of \(\mathfrak{Ab}(X)\) and let \(\mathscr{G}\)
209	% be the quotient:
210	% \(0 \to \mathscr{F} \to \mathscr{I} \to \mathscr{G} \to 0\).
211	% Then \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque by hypothesis, \(\mathscr{I}\) is
212	% flasque by (2.4), and so \(\mathscr{G}\) is flasque by (II, Ex. 1.16c).
213	% Now since \(\mathscr{F}\) is flasque, we have an exact sequence (II,
214	% Ex. 1.16b)
215	% \(0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{F}) \to \Gamma(X, \mathscr{I}) \to
216	% \Gamma(X, \mathscr{G}) \to 0\).
217	% On the other hand, since \(\mathscr{I}\) is injective, we have
218	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{I}) = 0\) for \(i > 0\) (1.1Ae). Thus from the long
219	% exact sequence of cohomology, we get \(H^1(X, \mathscr{F}) = 0\) and
220	% \(H^i(X, \mathscr{F}) \cong H^{i-1}(X, \mathscr{G})\) for each
221	% \(i \geq 2\). But \(\mathscr{G}\) is also flasque, so by induction on
222	% \(i\) we get the result."
223	\begin{proof}
224	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf}
225	  We follow Hartshorne's induction. Embed \(\mathcal F\) into an
226	  injective object \(\mathcal I\) of the abelian category
227	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}\;(\Opens.\mathtt{grothendieckTopology}\;X)\;
228	  (\Module \bar k)\) — such an injective exists by the
229	  enough-injectives instance for sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules on opens
230	  (\cref{chap:Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK}). Form the quotient
231	  short exact sequence
232	  \[
233	    0 \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal I \to \mathcal G \to 0
234	  \]
235	  in the sheaf category.
236	
237	  \emph{Step 1: the quotient \(\mathcal G\) is flasque.} The hypothesis
238	  gives that \(\mathcal F\) is flasque. The injective object
239	  \(\mathcal I\) is flasque by the Hartshorne~III~Lemma~2.4 input:
240	  any injective \(\mathcal O_X\)-module is flasque, where the
241	  \(\mathcal O_X\) here is the constant sheaf of rings \(\bar k\) on
242	  \(X\), so the sheaf category over the constant sheaf of rings is the
243	  category of sheaves of \(\bar k\)-modules and the lemma applies. From
244	  the two flasqueness inputs the quotient \(\mathcal G\) inherits
245	  flasqueness by Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(c): \emph{if
246	  \(0 \to \mathcal F' \to \mathcal F \to \mathcal F'' \to 0\) is an
247	  exact sequence and \(\mathcal F', \mathcal F\) are flasque, then
248	  \(\mathcal F''\) is flasque}.
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: the global-sections functor is exact on the chosen
251	  short exact sequence.} Since the leftmost term \(\mathcal F\) is
252	  flasque, the long exact sequence of cohomology starts with a short
253	  exact sequence on global sections
254	  (Hartshorne~II~Exercise~1.16(b)):
255	  \[
256	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal F) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to
257	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal G) \to 0.
258	  \]
259	
260	  \emph{Step 3: the long exact sequence gives the vanishing.}
261	  The object \(\mathcal I\) is injective in the abelian sheaf category,
262	  hence its right-derived global-sections cohomology vanishes in
263	  positive degree:
264	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;i = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\). The long
265	  exact sequence of the short exact sequence above, applied to the
266	  derived functor \(\HModule\;\bar k\;{-}\;{\bullet}\) of \(\Gamma\),
267	  reads in degrees \(0\) and \(1\) as
268	  \[
269	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal I) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal G)
270	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1
271	    \to \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal I\;1 = 0.
272	  \]
273	  The leftmost map is surjective by Step~2, so the connecting morphism
274	  to \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\) is the zero map, and the kernel
275	  of \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 \to 0\) is all of
276	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1\); hence
277	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;1 = 0\).
278	
279	  \emph{Step 4: higher degrees by induction.} For \(i \ge 2\), the long
280	  exact sequence yields
281	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal F\;i \cong \HModule\;\bar k\;\mathcal G\;
282	  (i - 1)\); the inductive hypothesis on the flasque quotient
283	  \(\mathcal G\) gives the right-hand side is zero. This closes the
284	  vanishing in all positive degrees. The chapter consumes only the
285	  degree-\(1\) instance, but the lemma is stated and proved in full
286	  generality so it can support later sub-builds (e.g.\ Grothendieck
287	  vanishing on a one-dimensional scheme).
288	
289	  % NOTE: the proof structure mirrors Hartshorne~III~Proposition~2.5
290	  % verbatim. The Lean closure is gated on three substrate inputs:
291	  % (a) the enough-injectives instance for `Sheaf J (ModuleCat kbar)`
292	  % already provided by chapter
293	  % `Cohomology_StructureSheafModuleK.tex`; (b) the project-side
294	  % flasque-quotient lemma (Hartshorne~II~Ex.~1.16(c)) restated for
295	  % `ModuleCat kbar`-valued sheaves; (c) the long-exact-sequence
296	  % carrier for `Abelian.Ext.covariantSequence` at the `HModule kbar`
297	  % level (the same LES carrier consumed by
298	  % `eulerCharacteristic_shortExact_add` in
299	  % `RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`). All three are project-side ~50-100
300	  % LOC apiece.
301	\end{proof}

303	\section{Skyscraper sheaves are flasque}
304	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperFlasque}
305	
306	\begin{lemma}
307	
308	\leanok
309	[Skyscraper sheaf as pushforward from a closed point]
310	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}
311	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward_const}
312	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.1, Exercise~1.17 (read from
313	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 68).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Skyscraper Sheaves. Let \(X\) be a topological space,
315	  % let \(P\) be a point, and let \(A\) be an abelian group. Define a
316	  % sheaf \(i_P(A)\) on \(X\) as follows: \(i_P(A)(U) = A\) if \(P \in
317	  % U\), \(0\) otherwise. \ldots Show that this sheaf could also be
318	  % described as \(i_*(A)\), where \(A\) denotes the constant sheaf
319	  % \(A\) on the closed subspace \(\{P\}^-\), and \(i\colon \{P\}^- \to
320	  % X\) is the inclusion."
321	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.1, Exercise~1.17.}
322	  Let \(X\) be a topological space, let \(P \in X\) be a point with
323	  closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\), and let \(A\) be a \(\bar k\)-module.
324	  Write \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow X\) for the closed
325	  embedding. Then the skyscraper sheaf
326	  \(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;A\) on \(X\) is naturally isomorphic to
327	  the pushforward \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;A\bigr)\)
328	  of the constant sheaf with value \(A\) on \(\overline{\{P\}}\).
329	\end{lemma}
330	
331	\begin{proof}
332	  Both presheaves send an open \(U \subseteq X\) to \(A\) when
333	  \(P \in U\) (equivalently, when
334	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}}\) is non-empty, since the closed subset
335	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is irreducible and any open meeting it contains
336	  \(P\)), and to \(0\) when \(P \notin U\) (equivalently,
337	  \(U \cap \overline{\{P\}} = \varnothing\)). The restriction maps agree
338	  by inspection: in the \(P \in V \subseteq U\) case both are the
339	  identity on \(A\); in the \(P \notin V\) case both factor through
340	  \(0\). The natural identification on opens is a sheaf morphism, and
341	  on stalks it is the identity (the stalk at any \(Q \neq P\) outside
342	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is zero on both sides; the stalk at any point of
343	  \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(A\) on both sides). Hence the comparison is
344	  an isomorphism of sheaves.
345	\end{proof}
346	
347	\begin{lemma}
348	
349	\leanok
350	[Closure of a closed point is irreducible]
351	  \label{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}
352	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PrimeDivisor.closure_isIrreducible}
353	
354	  Let \(C\) be a Noetherian scheme (in particular the smooth proper
355	  curve \(C / \bar k\) of this chapter), and let \(P \in C\) be a closed
356	  point. Then the closure \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
357	  (trivially: a singleton is irreducible as a topological space).
358	
359	  \textit{Project-bespoke ancillary} (immediate set-theoretic fact;
360	  no external source pinned).
361	\end{lemma}
362	
363	\begin{proof}
364	
365	\leanok
366	  A singleton topological space is irreducible: its only non-empty open
367	  is the whole space, which trivially intersects every other non-empty
368	  open in a non-empty set (in fact in itself). For a closed point
369	  \(P \in C\) the closure \(\overline{\{P\}}\) is \(\{P\}\), which is
370	  homeomorphic to a singleton, hence irreducible.
371	\end{proof}
372	
373	\begin{lemma}
374	
375	\leanok
376	[Closed-point skyscraper sheaf is flasque]
377	  \label{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
378	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
379	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward,
380	        lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible,
381	        lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
382	
383	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
384	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then the
385	  closed-point skyscraper sheaf
386	  \[
387	    k(P) \;:=\; \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
388	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)
389	  \]
390	  is flasque as a sheaf of \(\bar k\)-modules on \(C\).
391	
392	  \textit{Project-bespoke packaging} (no separately named external
393	  source; the constituent inputs are quoted in their own lemma blocks
394	  above).
395	\end{lemma}
396	
397	\begin{proof}
398	  \leanok
399	  \uses{def:isFlasque_sheaf, lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward, lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible, lem:isFlasque_pushforward, lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}
400	  By
401	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, the skyscraper sheaf is
402	  isomorphic to the pushforward
403	  \(i_*\bigl((\mathrm{constantSheaf}\;{-}\;\_)\;
404	  (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr)\) along the inclusion
405	  \(i \colon \overline{\{P\}} \hookrightarrow C\). The base
406	  \(\overline{\{P\}} = \{P\}\) is irreducible
407	  (\cref{lem:closedPoint_closure_irreducible}), so the constant sheaf on
408	  it is flasque (\cref{lem:isFlasque_constant_irreducible}). Pushforward
409	  of a flasque sheaf is flasque
410	  (\cref{lem:isFlasque_pushforward}). Flasqueness transports along the
411	  isomorphism of \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_eq_pushforward}, so
412	  \(k(P)\) is flasque.
413	\end{proof}

415	\section{\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf vanishes}
416	\label{sec:H1Vanishing_skyscraperH1}
417	
418	\begin{theorem}
419	
420	\leanok
421	[\(H^1\) of a closed-point skyscraper sheaf has dimension zero]
422	  \label{lem:H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero_main}
423	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.H1_skyscraperSheaf_finrank_eq_zero}
424	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
425	
426	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
427	  \(\bar k\) and let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. Then
428	  \[
429	    \dim_{\bar k}\, H^1\bigl(C,\, \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;
430	      (\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;\bar k)\bigr) \;=\; 0,
431	  \]
432	  where the left-hand side is the \(\bar k\)-finrank of
433	  \(\HModule\;\bar k\;(\mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;
434	  \bar k\;\bar k))\;1\).
435	
436	  \textit{Project-bespoke assembly} of the two substrate inputs of this
437	  chapter (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque} and
438	  \cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). The chapter's external source
439	  is Hartshorne~III.2 Proposition~2.5 applied to the flasque skyscraper
440	  sheaf — both inputs are quoted verbatim in their own lemma blocks
441	  above.
442	\end{theorem}
443	
444	\begin{proof}
445	  \uses{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque, lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}
446	  \leanok
447	  The closed-point skyscraper sheaf
448	  \(k(P) = \mathtt{skyscraperSheaf}\;P\;(\Module.\mathtt{of}\;\bar k\;
449	  \bar k)\) is flasque (\cref{lem:skyscraperSheaf_isFlasque}). By the
450	  flasque-cohomology vanishing theorem
451	  (\cref{thm:H1_vanishing_flasque}) applied at \(i = 1\) on the
452	  underlying topological space \(C.\mathtt{left.toTopCat}\),
453	  \[
454	    \HModule\;\bar k\;(k(P))\;1 \;=\; 0.
455	  \]
456	  Taking \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\;\bar k\) of the zero module gives
457	  the claimed identity \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\).
458	\end{proof}

43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	\leanok
95	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
96	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
97	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
98	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
99	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map
100	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
101	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi\) is said to be \emph{defined} at a point
102	  % \(v\) of \(V\) if \(v \in U\) for some \((U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi\). The set \(U_1\) of
103	  % \(v\) at which \(\varphi\) is defined is open, and there is a regular map
104	  % \(\varphi_1 \colon U_1 \to W\) such that \((U_1, \varphi_1) \in \varphi\) --- clearly,
105	  % \(U_1 = \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in \varphi} U\) and we can define \(\varphi_1\) to be
106	  % the regular map such that \(\varphi_1 | U = \varphi_U\) for all $(U, \varphi_U) \in
107	  % \varphi$."
108	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S I.3, p.~16.}
109	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
110	  The \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\) is the open set
111	  \[
112	    \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;:=\; \bigcup_{(U, \varphi_U) \in f} U \;\subseteq\; X,
113	  \]
114	  i.e.\ the union of the dense opens of all representatives of \(f\). The
115	  \emph{indeterminacy locus} of \(f\) is the closed complement
116	  \[
117	    Z(f) \;:=\; X \smallsetminus \mathrm{Dom}(f) \;\subseteq\; X.
118	  \]
119	  The map \(f\) is regular (i.e.\ defined on all of \(X\)) if and only if \(Z(f) = \emptyset\).
120	
121	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
122	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} is a method on the
123	  Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure pinned at
124	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Birational.RationalMap}; it returns a
125	  \texttt{Set X} (the underlying carrier of the closed complement of
126	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.domain}, which is already exposed by Mathlib). The
127	  closedness of \(Z(f)\) is a separate \texttt{IsClosed} witness, packaged either as a
128	  field of \texttt{indeterminacyLocus} or as a standalone lemma
129	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus}, whichever is cleaner with
130	  Mathlib's existing API at commit \texttt{b80f227}.
131	\end{definition}
132	
133	\begin{definition}
134	
135	\leanok
136	  [Codimension-1-indeterminacy-free rational maps]
137	  \label{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}
138	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree}
139	  
140	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 --- the conclusion
141	  % "an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)"
142	  % is the standing predicate we abstract (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf,
143	  % PDF page 22).
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A rational map \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow W\) from a normal
145	  % variety \(V\) to a complete variety \(W\) is defined on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose
146	  % complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)."
147	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16 (the
148	  conclusion).}
149	  Let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map between varieties over \(\bar k\).
150	  We say \(f\) is \emph{codim-1-indeterminacy-free} (or has \emph{codimension-\(\geq 2\)
151	  indeterminacy}) if every irreducible component of the closed subset \(Z(f) \subseteq X\)
152	  has codimension \(\geq 2\) in \(X\). Equivalently, no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in
153	  \(Z(f)\); equivalently, every point \(\eta \in X\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\)
154	  lies in \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\).
155	
156	  \paragraph{Why the abstraction.} Milne's Theorem~3.1 (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}
157	  below) and Lemma~3.3 (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} below) both produce
158	  conclusions about the codimension of \(Z(f)\). Phrasing the codim-\(\geq 2\) condition
159	  as a named predicate keeps the cited statements concise and lets the consumer
160	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} read off the combination
161	  (\(Z(f)\) has codim \(\geq 2\) from \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} \emph{and} \(Z(f)\) is
162	  empty or pure codim 1 from \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} \(\Rightarrow\) \(Z(f)\)
163	  is empty) as a single line.
164	
165	  \paragraph{Lean encoding scope.} The Lean target
166	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} is a \texttt{Prop}-valued
167	  predicate, encoded via Mathlib's \texttt{Order.coheight} on the specialisation
168	  preorder of \texttt{X.carrier} as in
169	  \cref{def:prime_divisor}: \(f\) is codim-1-indeterminacy-free if and only if for every
170	  \(\eta \in X\) with \(\texttt{Order.coheight\ }\eta = 1\), $\eta \in
171	  \texttt{f.domain}\(. The encoding matches the standing \)\mathrm{Order.coheight}$
172	  convention pinned in \cref{def:prime_divisor}, so prime divisors of \(X\) and the
173	  hypothesis ``no codim-\(1\) indeterminacy'' use the same Mathlib idiom.
174	\end{definition}

176	\section{Smoothness yields a DVR at every codim-1 point}
177	\label{sec:smooth_dvr}
178	
179	The codim-\(1\) extension argument in Milne's proof of Theorem~3.1 hinges on the local
180	ring at the generic point of a prime divisor being a discrete valuation ring (so that
181	the valuative criterion of properness applies). On a normal variety this is the
182	defining property of regularity in codimension one; on a smooth variety it is automatic.
183	
184	The smooth-implies-regular-local-stalk implication itself is the Jacobian-criterion
185	direction of Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}; we package it as a separate sub-lemma so that
186	\cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} below reads cleanly as ``regular local of dimension
187	\(1\) is a DVR'', with the regularity prerequisite supplied by the sub-lemma.
188	
189	\begin{lemma}
190	  [Smooth over \(\bar k\) \(\Rightarrow\) regular local stalk (Stacks 00TT)]
191	  \label{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}
192	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT --- Jacobian criterion
193	  % (smooth-implies-regular direction) (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex,
194	  % L38593--38611; tag verified against the Stacks website tag/00TT page,
195	  % "Lemma 10.140.3" in section 10.140 "Smooth algebras over fields").
196	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $k$ be any field. Let $S$ be a finite type $k$-algebra.
197	  % Let $X = \Spec(S)$. Let $\mathfrak q \subset S$ be a prime corresponding to
198	  % $x \in X$. The following are equivalent:
199	  % \begin{enumerate}
200	  % \item The $k$-algebra $S$ is smooth at $\mathfrak q$ over $k$.
201	  % \item We have
202	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
203	  % \leq \dim_x X$.
204	  % \item We have
205	  % $\dim_{\kappa(\mathfrak q)} \Omega_{S/k} \otimes_S \kappa(\mathfrak q)
206	  % = \dim_x X$.
207	  % \end{enumerate}
208	  % Moreover, in this case the local ring $S_{\mathfrak q}$ is regular."
209	  % NOTE (iter-186 plan): the iter-185 writer left an empty
210	  % uses-annotation here intentionally to signal "no project-side label
211	  % prerequisites" — the Stacks 00TT prerequisites (smooth ring map,
212	  % regular local ring) live in Mathlib as typeclasses
213	  % (Algebra.Smooth, IsRegularLocalRing) but are not packaged as
214	  % blueprint-labeled definitions in this project; the project's
215	  % def:codim_one_indeterminacy and Auslander--Buchsbaum-side
216	  % cor:regular_cohen_macaulay are adjacent context, not strict
217	  % prerequisites. blueprint-doctor (iter-185) flagged the empty
218	  % argument as a CRITICAL plastex/depgraph build-breaker and removed
219	  % it; the iter-186 plan agent further reworded this NOTE because the
220	  % literal backtick-quoted token was still tripping the doctor regex.
221	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.}
222	  Let \(X\) be a variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) (geometrically
223	  integral, separated, locally of finite type) and assume the structure morphism
224	  \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) is \emph{smooth}. Then for every point \(z \in X\) the
225	  stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, z}\) is a regular local ring.
226	
227	  The hypothesis \([IsAlgClosed\,\bar k]\) is load-bearing: tag 00TT is stated
228	  over an arbitrary field, but the variant Mathlib will be able to discharge
229	  axiom-clean at commit \texttt{b80f227} routes through residue fields that are
230	  \(\bar k\)-rational (every closed point of \(X\) has residue field \(\bar k\) under
231	  algebraic closure, and the chain of localisations from a closed point reaches
232	  every \(z \in X\) by the standard chain-of-primes argument). Without algebraic
233	  closure one needs the geometric-regularity refinement of tag 00TT (Stacks tag
234	  \texttt{056T} in the project's \texttt{stacks-varieties.md} card) and an extra
235	  base-change-to-\(\bar k\) step.
236	\end{lemma}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00TT.} The cited lemma packages the
240	  Jacobian-criterion equivalence and concludes ``in this case the local ring
241	  \(S_{\mathfrak q}\) is regular''. Translated to the scheme level: a smooth
242	  finite-type morphism \(X \to \Spec(\bar k)\) restricted to an affine chart
243	  \(\Spec(S) \subseteq X\) gives an affine algebra \(\bar k \to S\) that is
244	  smooth at every prime \(\mathfrak q \subset S\); tag 00TT then yields

270	  (``Regular Rings'') develops the regular-local-ring theory needed for
271	  step~(iv); see \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} for the depth-/CM-side
272	  references the project already maintains.
273	\end{proof}

276	
277	\leanok

279	  \label{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}
280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing_dvr_of_codim_one}
281	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, p.~130 (the definition ``regular in codimension one'',
282	  % together with the immediately preceding remark that on a nonsingular variety every
283	  % local ring is regular) (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf,
284	  % PDF page 147).
285	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textbf{Definition.} We say a scheme \(X\) is \emph{regular in
286	  % codimension one} (or sometimes \emph{nonsingular in codimension one}) if every
287	  % local ring \(\mathcal O_x\) of \(X\) of dimension one is regular."
288	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The most important examples of such schemes are nonsingular
289	  % varieties over a field (I, \S5) and noetherian normal schemes. On a nonsingular
290	  % variety the local ring of every closed point is regular (I, 5.1), hence all the

293	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic

297	  the DVR consequence at a codim-1 point).}
298	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\) and let \(\eta \in X\) be a point with
299	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a
300	  discrete valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\).
301	\end{lemma}
302	
303	\begin{proof}
304	
305	  % NOTE (iter-185 writer): the `\leanok` marker on this proof block reflects
306	  % the Krull-dim half (Tier-1 axiom-clean closure achieved iter-184 via
307	  % `Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight` from the iter-183
308	  % `Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`, see
309	  % `\cref{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}`). The `IsRegularLocalRing` half is
310	  % tracked separately as the Stacks 00TT gap and is now packaged in
311	  % `\cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring}` above; that block carries no
312	  % `\leanok` because the Mathlib hooks (i)--(iv) in its proof sketch are not
313	  % all axiom-clean at commit `b80f227`. The composition (00TT regularity +
314	  % iter-183 Krull-dim bridge) inside the private Lean helper
315	  % `smooth_codim_one_maximalIdeal_isPrincipal_and_ne_bot`
316	  % (`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean:223`) keeps a single
317	  % typed `sorry` on the regularity half pending an iter-185+ prover round.
318	  By \cref{lem:smooth_to_regular_local_ring} (Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}, the
319	  Jacobian-criterion direction), the stalk \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular for
320	  every point \(x \in X\) --- in particular at every closed point (the case
321	  Hartshorne~I.5.1 states classically). Localising a regular local ring at a
322	  prime ideal yields a regular local ring; in particular, for \(\eta \in X\) with
323	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\), the local ring
324	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is regular of Krull dimension \(1\). A regular local ring of Krull dimension \(1\) is exactly a
325	  discrete valuation ring (Atiyah--Macdonald, Proposition 9.2; or Stacks tag
326	  \texttt{00PD}). Its fraction field is the function field \(K(X)\) of \(X\) by
327	  irreducibility of \(X\) (the unique generic point of \(X\) lies above \(\eta\), and the
328	  stalk at the generic point of an integral scheme is the function field).

344	\section{Milne Theorem 3.1: codimension-\(\geq 2\) extension}
345	\label{sec:milne_thm31}
346	
347	The first input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is the following: a rational map from a normal
348	variety to a \emph{complete} variety automatically extends across every codim-\(1\)
349	point.
350	
351	\begin{theorem}
352	
353	\leanok
354	  [Milne Theorem~3.1: codim-\(\geq 2\) extension to a complete target]
355	  \label{thm:codim_one_extension}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_of_codimOneFree_of_smooth}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16
359	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 22).
360	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Theorem 3.1.} \emph{A rational map $\varphi \colon V
361	  % \dashrightarrow W\( from a normal variety \)V\( to a complete variety \)W$ is defined
362	  % on an open subset \(U\) of \(V\) whose complement \(V \smallsetminus U\) has codimension
363	  % \(\geq 2\).}"
364	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.1, \S I.3, p.~16.}
365	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a complete variety over
366	  \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. Then the
367	  indeterminacy locus \(Z(f)\) (\cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}) has codimension
368	  \(\geq 2\) in \(X\); equivalently, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
369	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}).
370	
371	  Specialising further: if in addition \(f\) \emph{is} codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
372	  ``for free'' (i.e.\ no prime divisor of \(X\) is contained in \(Z(f)\)), then
373	  \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) = X\) and \(f\) extends to a regular morphism $\widetilde f \colon X
374	  \to Y\(. The extension is unique because \)X\( is reduced and \)\mathrm{Dom}(f)$ is dense
375	  in \(X\).
376	\end{theorem}
377	
378	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Assume first that \(V\) is a curve. Thus we are
379	% given a nonsingular curve \(V\) and a regular map \(\varphi \colon U \to W\) from an
380	% open subset of \(V\) which we want to extend to \(V\). Consider the maps
381	% [diagram: \(U \hookrightarrow V\), $U \xrightarrow{u \mapsto (u, \varphi(u))} Z
382	% \subseteq V \times W\(, \)U \xrightarrow{\varphi} W$, with projections from
383	% \(V \times W\) to \(V\) and to \(W\)]
384	% Let \(U'\) be the image of \(U\) in \(V \times W\), and let \(Z\) be its closure. The image
385	% of \(Z\) in \(V\) is closed (because \(W\) is complete), and contains \(U\) (the composite
386	% \(U \to V\) is the given inclusion), and so \(Z\) maps onto \(V\). The maps $U \to U'
387	% \to U\( are isomorphisms. Therefore, \)Z \to V$ is a surjective map from a complete
388	% curve onto a nonsingular complete curve that is an isomorphism on open subsets.
389	% Such a map must be an isomorphism (complete nonsingular curves are determined by
390	% their function fields). The restriction of the projection map \(V \times W \to W\)
391	% to \(Z\ (\simeq V)\) is the extension of \(\varphi\) to \(V\) we are seeking.
392	%   The general case can be reduced to the one-dimensional case (using schemes). Let
393	% \(U\) be the largest subset on which \(\varphi\) is defined, and suppose that $V
394	% \smallsetminus U\( has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor \)Z\( in \)V
395	% \smallsetminus U\(. Because \)V$ is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete
396	% valuation ring \(\mathcal O_Z\) with field of fractions \(k(V)\). The map \(\varphi\)
397	% defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(V)) \to W\), which the
398	% valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a
399	% morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_Z) \to W\). This implies that \(\varphi\)
400	% has a representative defined on an open subset that meets \(Z\) in a nonempty set,
401	% which is a contradiction."
402	\begin{proof}
403	  
404	  Following Milne's two-step proof.
405	
406	  \emph{Step 1: rule out codim-\(1\) components.} Suppose for contradiction that \(Z(f)\)
407	  contains a prime divisor \(W \subseteq X\) (i.e.\ a codim-\(1\) irreducible component).
408	  Let \(\eta \in W\) be its generic point, so \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta\}} = 1\). By
409	  \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}\) is a discrete
410	  valuation ring with fraction field the function field \(K(X)\). Any representative
411	  \((U, \varphi_U) \in f\) has \(U \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f) \neq \emptyset\) and so determines
412	  a \(\bar k\)-algebra map from the function field \(K(Y)\) of \(Y\) to \(K(X)\): explicitly,
413	  \(\varphi_U\) defines a morphism of schemes \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X)) \to Y\) (since
414	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(K(X))\) is the generic point of \(X\)). By the valuative criterion
415	  of properness applied to the complete variety \(Y\)
416	  (\emph{cf.}\ Hartshorne~II.4.7, cited verbatim in Milne~3.1's proof), this morphism
417	  extends to a morphism \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathcal O_{X, \eta}) \to Y\). Standard
418	  algebraic-geometry spreading then exhibits an open neighbourhood \(V\) of \(\eta\) in
419	  \(X\) on which \(f\) has a regular representative; in particular $\eta \in
420	  \mathrm{Dom}(f)\(, contradicting \)\eta \in W \subseteq Z(f)\(. Hence no codim-\)1$
421	  component of \(X\) lies in \(Z(f)\), i.e.\ \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\).
422	
423	  \emph{Step 2: extend across codim-\(\geq 2\) points (the "codim-1-indeterminacy-free
424	  \(\Rightarrow\) extension" half).} Suppose now that \(Z(f)\) has codimension \(\geq 2\)
425	  but is non-empty (we want to deduce \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) under the additional input
426	  that ``\(f\) is already codim-1-indeterminacy-free, by hypothesis''). Pick a closed
427	  point \(x \in Z(f)\) with \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{x\}} \geq 2\). By
428	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} applied to the regular local ring
429	  \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\), we have
430	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = \dim(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\). The sheaf
431	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on the punctured neighbourhood \(V \smallsetminus \{x\}\)
432	  (any small affine neighbourhood \(V \ni x\)) has \(H^0_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\)
433	  and \(H^1_{\{x\}}(V, \mathcal O_X) = 0\) (the depth-\(\geq 2\) characterisation of
434	  local-cohomology vanishing, Stacks tag \texttt{0AVF}; cf.\ Hartshorne~III.3.5 for
435	  the surface case). Pull back the coordinates of any affine chart of \(Y\) around the
436	  closure \(\overline{f(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \cap V)}\) along \(f\); the resulting sections of
437	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(V \cap \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) extend uniquely to sections on \(V\),
438	  yielding a regular representative of \(f\) on \(V\). Hence \(x \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\),
439	  contradicting \(x \in Z(f)\). Therefore \(Z(f) = \emptyset\) and \(f\) extends to a
440	  regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to Y\).
441	
442	  Uniqueness of \(\widetilde f\) is the standard reduced-and-separated agreement
443	  principle: two morphisms \(X \to Y\) that agree on the dense open \(\mathrm{Dom}(f)\)
444	  agree on all of \(X\).
445	\end{proof}
446	
447	\begin{remark}
448	  \label{rmk:codim1_role_of_ab}
449	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} Step~1 of the proof uses only normality
450	  + the valuative criterion of properness (Hartshorne~II.4.7) and is characteristic-free.
451	  Step~2 uses the depth-\(\geq 2\) extension property at a codim-\(\geq 2\) regular
452	  point, which is exactly what \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
453	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies (regular local of dimension \(d\)
454	  \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(d\)). For our application the source \(X\) is a
455	  smooth variety, so the regular-local-ring hypothesis is automatic; the Cohen--Macaulay
456	  consequence is what feeds the local-cohomology vanishing.
457	
458	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
459	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), the codim-\(\geq 2\) closed points of
460	  \(X\) are exactly the closed points of \(X\) (because \(\dim X = 2\)), and at each such
461	  point \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is regular of dimension \(2\), hence Cohen--Macaulay of
462	  depth \(2\). The smooth-surface case is the one A.4.c will invoke when extending the
463	  symmetric-product rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) along the Abel--Jacobi
464	  birational equivalence to \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\).
465	\end{remark}
466	
467	\section{Milne Lemma 3.3: indeterminacy locus of a map to a group variety is codim 1}
468	\label{sec:milne_lem33}
469	
470	The second input to Milne's Theorem~3.2 is structural: when the target \(Y\) is a group
471	variety, the indeterminacy locus is either empty or of \emph{pure} codimension \(1\).
472	Combined with \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s ``codimension \(\geq 2\)'' conclusion,
473	this forces the locus to be empty.
474	
475	\begin{lemma}
476	
477	\leanok
478	  [Milne Lemma~3.3: pure-codim-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus into a group
479	  variety]
480	  \label{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
481	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacy_pure_codim_one_into_grpScheme}
482	  
483	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17
484	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
485	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\textsc{Lemma 3.3.} \emph{Let \(\varphi \colon V \dashrightarrow G\)
486	  % be a rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either
487	  % \(\varphi\) is defined on all of \(V\) or the points where it is not defined form a
488	  % closed subset of pure codimension 1 in \(V\) (i.e., a finite union of prime
489	  % divisors).}"
490	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Lemma~3.3, \S I.3, p.~17.}
491	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(G\) be a group variety over
492	  \(\bar k\) (a smooth group scheme of finite type), and let $f \colon X \dashrightarrow
493	  G\( be a rational map. Then either \)f\( is defined on all of \)X$, or the indeterminacy
494	  locus \(Z(f) \subseteq X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) (i.e.\ a finite
495	  union of prime divisors).
496	
497	  Equivalently: it is \emph{never} the case that \(f\) has a non-empty indeterminacy
498	  locus all of whose irreducible components have codimension \(\geq 2\).
499	\end{lemma}
500	
501	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "\textsc{Proof.} Define a rational map
502	%   \(\Phi \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow G\), $\;(x, y) \mapsto \varphi(x) \cdot
503	%   \varphi(y)^{-1}$.
504	% More precisely, if \((U, \varphi_U)\) represents \(\varphi\), then \(\Phi\) is the rational
505	% map represented by
506	%   $U \times U \xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U} G \times G
507	%   \xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}} G
508	%   \xrightarrow{m} G$.
509	% Clearly \(\Phi\) is defined at a diagonal point \((x, x)\) if \(\varphi\) is defined at
510	% \(x\), and then \(\Phi(x, x) = e\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\), then
511	% it is defined on an open neighbourhood of \((x, x)\); in particular, there will be an
512	% open subset \(U\) of \(V\) such that \(\Phi\) is defined on \(\{x\} \times U\). After
513	% possible replacing \(U\) by a smaller open subset (not necessarily containing \(x\)),
514	% \(\varphi\) will be defined on \(U\). For \(u \in U\), the formula
515	%   \(\varphi(x) = \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u)\)
516	% defines \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Thus \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) if and only if \(\Phi\) is
517	% defined at \((x, x)\). The rational map \(\Phi\) defines a map
518	%   \(\Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \to k(V \times V)\).
519	% Since \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) if it is defined there, it follows that \(\Phi\)
520	% is defined at \((x, x)\) if and only if
521	%   \(\operatorname{Im}(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subset \mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)}\).
522	% Now \(V \times V\) is nonsingular, and so we have a good theory of divisors (AG,
523	% Chapter 12). For a nonzero rational function \(f\) on \(V \times V\), write
524	%   \(\operatorname{div}(f) = \operatorname{div}(f)_0 - \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\),
525	% with \(\operatorname{div}(f)_0\) and \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\) effective divisors
526	% --- note that \(\operatorname{div}(f)_\infty = \operatorname{div}(f^{-1})_0\). Then
527	%   $\mathcal O_{V \times V, (x, x)} = \{f \in k(V \times V) \mid
528	%   \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x)\} \cup \{0\}$.
529	% Suppose \(\phi\) is not defined at \(x\). Then for some $f \in \operatorname{Im}
530	% (\varphi^\ast)\(, \)(x, x) \in \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\(, and clearly \)\Phi$ is
531	% not defined at the points \((y, y) \in \Delta \cap \operatorname{div}(f)_\infty\).
532	% This is a subset of pure codimension one in \(\Delta\) (AG 9.2), and when we identify
533	% it with a subset of \(V\), it is a subset of \(V\) of codimension one passing through
534	% \(x\) on which \(\varphi\) is not defined."
535	\begin{proof}
536	  
537	  Following Milne's proof of Lemma~3.3. The argument has four sub-steps. Throughout we
538	  fix a representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\).
539	
540	  \emph{Sub-step 1: the difference rational map.} Define
541	  \[
542	    \Phi \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow G,
543	    \qquad
544	    \Phi(x, y) \;:=\; \varphi(x) \cdot \varphi(y)^{-1},
545	  \]
546	  via the composition
547	  \[
548	    U \times U
549	    \;\xrightarrow{\varphi_U \times \varphi_U}\; G \times G
550	    \;\xrightarrow{\mathrm{id} \times \mathrm{inv}}\; G \times G
551	    \;\xrightarrow{m}\; G,
552	  \]
553	  where \(\mathrm{inv} \colon G \to G\) is the group-variety inverse and
554	  \(m \colon G \times G \to G\) is the group law. The map \(\Phi\) is rational by
555	  composition: its domain is at least \(U \times U \subseteq X \times X\), which is dense
556	  because \(U\) is dense in \(X\).
557	
558	  \emph{Sub-step 2: \(\Phi\)-defined at \((x, x)\) \(\Leftrightarrow\) \(\varphi\)-defined at
559	  \(x\).} If \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\), the explicit formula \(\Phi(x, x) = e\) (the
560	  identity of \(G\)) shows \(\Phi\) is defined at \((x, x)\). Conversely, if \(\Phi\) is
561	  defined at \((x, x)\), openness of \(\mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)\) in \(X \times X\) produces an
562	  open set \(W \ni (x, x)\) on which \(\Phi\) is defined; by projection (taking the slice
563	  \(\{x\} \times W' \subseteq W\) for some open \(W' \subseteq X\), possibly shrinking
564	  \(W'\) so \(\varphi\) is defined on \(W'\) as well --- \(W'\) need not contain \(x\)), the
565	  identity
566	  \[
567	    \varphi(x) \;=\; \Phi(x, u) \cdot \varphi(u),
568	    \qquad u \in W',
569	  \]
570	  exhibits a regular representative of \(\varphi\) at \(x\). Hence $x \in
571	  \mathrm{Dom}(\varphi)\( if and only if \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{Dom}(\Phi)$.
572	
573	  \emph{Sub-step 3: rephrase via pullback of local rings.} The rational map \(\Phi\)
574	  pulls \(\mathcal O_{G, e}\) back to a \(\bar k\)-subalgebra of the function field
575	  \(K(X \times X)\):
576	  \[
577	    \Phi^\ast \colon \mathcal O_{G, e} \;\longrightarrow\; K(X \times X).
578	  \]
579	  Because \(\Phi\) sends \((x, x)\) to \(e\) wherever it is defined, \(\Phi\) is defined at
580	  \((x, x)\) if and only if $\Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e}) \subseteq \mathcal O_{X
581	  \times X, (x, x)}\( (i.e.\ every local function on \)G\( near \)e$ has no pole at
582	  \((x, x)\) after pullback).
583	
584	  \emph{Sub-step 4: pole locus is pure codimension \(1\).} The variety \(X \times X\) is
585	  nonsingular (smoothness is stable under products over \(\bar k\)), so the Weil-divisor
586	  apparatus of \cref{def:codim1_cycles} and the order map of
587	  \cref{def:order_at_point} apply on \(X \times X\). For any nonzero rational function
588	  \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) decompose its divisor as
589	  \[
590	    \mathrm{div}(f) \;=\; \mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty,
591	  \]
592	  with \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) effective and
593	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty = \mathrm{div}(f^{-1})_0\) (Milne's notation, matching the
594	  project's \(\div(f) = \sum \ord_Y(f) \cdot Y\) of
595	  \cref{def:principal_divisor}: \(\mathrm{div}(f)_0\) collects the prime divisors with
596	  \(\ord_Y(f) > 0\) and \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) those with \(\ord_Y(f) < 0\)). The local
597	  ring at \((x, x)\) can be characterised as
598	  \[
599	    \mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}
600	    \;=\; \bigl\{\, f \in K(X \times X) \;\big|\;
601	    \mathrm{div}(f)_\infty \text{ does not contain } (x, x) \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
602	  \]
603	  Suppose \(f \in K(X \times X)^\ast\) is not in \(\mathcal O_{X \times X, (x, x)}\);
604	  equivalently, \((x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty)\). The pole divisor
605	  \(\mathrm{div}(f)_\infty\) is a finite sum of prime divisors of \(X \times X\) (i.e.\ a
606	  closed subset of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X \times X\)). Its intersection with the
607	  diagonal \(\Delta_X \subseteq X \times X\) is a closed subset of pure codimension \(1\)
608	  in \(\Delta_X\) (this is Milne's reference to AG~9.2, the standard fact that a prime
609	  divisor of \(X \times X\) either contains \(\Delta_X\) or meets it in pure codimension
610	  \(1\); on a nonsingular variety this is an instance of Krull's principal-ideal theorem
611	  applied to the local equation of \(\Delta_X\) inside \(X \times X\)).
612	
613	  \emph{Conclusion.} Suppose \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow G\) is not defined on all of
614	  \(X\). Pick \(x \in Z(f)\). By Sub-steps~2--3, there is some $f_0 \in
615	  \Phi^\ast(\mathcal O_{G, e})\( with \)(x, x) \in \mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty)$.
616	  By Sub-step~4 the intersection $\mathrm{supp}(\mathrm{div}(f_0)_\infty) \cap
617	  \Delta_X\( is pure codimension \)1\( in \)\Delta_X\(, passing through \)(x, x)$.
618	  Identifying \(\Delta_X\) with \(X\) via the diagonal isomorphism, the corresponding
619	  closed subset of \(X\) has pure codimension \(1\) and passes through \(x\). By Sub-step~2,
620	  every point of this codim-\(1\) subset is a point where \(\Phi\) is undefined on the
621	  diagonal, hence where \(\varphi\) is undefined. Thus \(Z(\varphi)\) contains a pure
622	  codim-\(1\) closed subset passing through \(x\); since \(x \in Z(\varphi)\) was arbitrary,
623	  every irreducible component of \(Z(\varphi)\) has codimension exactly \(1\), i.e.\
624	  \(Z(\varphi)\) is of pure codimension \(1\).
625	\end{proof}
626	
627	\begin{remark}
628	  \label{rmk:milne_lem33_charfree}
629	  \textbf{Characteristic-free.} The above proof uses only: (i) the group law on \(G\)
630	  and its inverse (formal smoothness of \(G\) as a group object); (ii) the Weil-divisor
631	  apparatus on the nonsingular variety \(X \times X\) (Hartshorne~II.6, or
632	  \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} in the project's notation); (iii) the
633	  decomposition of a divisor on a nonsingular variety into effective pieces, and
634	  Krull's principal-ideal theorem on \(X \times X\). None of these has a characteristic
635	  restriction, so \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} holds over every
636	  algebraically closed base field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states the
637	  lemma in this generality at \S I.3, p.~17.)
638	\end{remark}
639	
640	\section{Weil-divisor obstruction to codim-1 extension}
641	\label{sec:weil_obstruction}
642	
643	The proof of \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s Step~1 (codim-\(1\) ruling-out) goes
644	through the valuative criterion. In the project's Weil-divisor language
645	(\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}) the same obstruction has a cleaner phrasing:
646	the extension fails at a prime divisor \(W\) if and only if some affine coordinate of
647	\(f\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\) has a strictly negative order
648	\(\ord_W(\cdot) < 0\). This is the form the Lean prover needs when consuming
649	\cref{def:order_at_point} and \cref{def:principal_divisor}.
650	
651	\begin{theorem}
652	
653	  [Weil-divisor characterisation of the codim-1 extension obstruction]
654	  \label{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
655	  
656	  % NOTE (iter-179 review, lvb-checker `codimone-iter179` corrective):
657	  % `\lean{...}` pin DETACHED (Path D2 option (b) per the lvb-checker). The
658	  % substantive Hartshorne-II.6 valuative reformulation (the iff with
659	  % ord_W >= 0) below is the *intended* statement; the iter-179 Lane D
660	  % `mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point` decl is only a 2-LOC
661	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain` reshuffle that does NOT prove the
662	  % ord_W content, so re-pinning `\lean{...}` to it would falsely claim
663	  % kernel-clean formalization of the substantive Hartshorne iff. The
664	  % weaker reshuffle proven this iter will be pinned to its own light
665	  % `\begin{lemma}` block (working title `lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle`)
666	  % by an iter-180+ blueprint-writer dispatch; the substantive Hartshorne
667	  % version stays pinned here but unlinked until a future
668	  % `extend_iff_pullback_order_nonneg` lemma carries
669	  % `Scheme.RationalMap.order` machinery (pullback `RationalMap →
670	  % function-field ring map`, not yet in Mathlib at b80f227). See
671	  % task_results/lean-vs-blueprint-checker-codimone-iter179.md +
672	  % proof-journal/sessions/session_179/recommendations.md REC-14.
673	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], II.6, pp.~130--131 (the valuation \(v_Y\) associated to a
674	  % prime divisor, and the characterisation of \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_Y}\) as the
675	  % subring of \(K(X)\) on which \(v_Y \geq 0\)) (read from
676	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF pages 147--148).
677	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "If \(Y\) is a prime divisor on \(X\), let \(\eta \in Y\) be its generic
678	  % point. Then the local ring \(\mathcal O_{\eta, X}\) of \(X\) at \(\eta\) is a discrete
679	  % valuation ring with quotient field \(K\), the function field of \(X\). We call the
680	  % corresponding discrete valuation \(v_Y\) the \emph{valuation of \(Y\)}."
681	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Now let \(f \in K^\ast\) be any nonzero rational function on \(X\).
682	  % Then \(v_Y(f)\) is an integer. If it is positive, we say \(f\) has a \emph{zero} along
683	  % \(Y\), of that order; if it is negative, we say \(f\) has a \emph{pole} along \(Y\), of
684	  % order \(-v_Y(f)\)."
685	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, II.6, pp.~130--131 (valuation \(v_Y\) and the order map).}
686	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over \(\bar k\), let \(Y\) be a variety over \(\bar k\),
687	  let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map, and let \(W \subseteq X\) be a
688	  prime divisor with generic point \(\eta_W \in W\) (so
689	  \(\mathrm{codim}_X \overline{\{\eta_W\}} = 1\)). Let \((U, \varphi_U)\) be any representative of
690	  \(f\) with \(U \cap (X \smallsetminus W) \neq \emptyset\). The following are equivalent:
691	  \begin{enumerate}
692	    \item \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) (i.e.\ \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\), equivalently
693	      \(W \not\subseteq Z(f)\)).
694	    \item For some (equivalently, every) affine open neighbourhood \(V \subseteq Y\) of
695	      the generic image of \(f\) near \(W\), every regular function $g \in
696	      \mathcal O_Y(V)\( pulls back to a rational function \)\varphi_U^\ast(g) \in K(X)$
697	      satisfying
698	      \[
699	        \ord_W\bigl(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\bigr) \;\geq\; 0.
700	      \]
701	  \end{enumerate}
702	
703	  Consequently: \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free
704	  (\cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}) if and only if, for every prime divisor
705	  \(W \subseteq X\) and every affine open \(V \subseteq Y\) as above, the pullback
706	  \(\varphi_U^\ast(g)\) has \(\ord_W \geq 0\) for all \(g \in \mathcal O_Y(V)\).
707	\end{theorem}
708	
709	\begin{proof}
710	  
711	  By \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, the local ring \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\) is a
712	  DVR with fraction field \(K(X)\) and uniformiser determined by \(W\); by
713	  \cref{def:order_at_point} its valuation is exactly \(\ord_W\). The local ring at
714	  \(\eta_W\) is therefore characterised inside \(K(X)\) as the subring
715	  \[
716	    \mathcal O_{X, \eta_W} \;=\; \bigl\{\, h \in K(X) \;\big|\; \ord_W(h) \geq 0
717	    \,\bigr\} \cup \{0\}.
718	  \]
719	  The rational map \(f\) is defined at \(\eta_W\) if and only if, for any affine open
720	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec} A \subseteq Y\) around the image \(\overline{f(\eta_W)}\),
721	  the pullback \(\varphi_U^\ast \colon A \to K(X)\) lands in
722	  \(\mathcal O_{X, \eta_W}\): this is the standard local characterisation of where a
723	  rational map is regular at a codim-\(1\) point of a smooth variety (it is the
724	  ``regular = no pole'' criterion). By the displayed identity, the latter is
725	  equivalent to \(\ord_W(\varphi_U^\ast(g)) \geq 0\) for every \(g \in A\), which is
726	  condition (2). The equivalence (1)~\(\Leftrightarrow\)~(2) is therefore established.
727	
728	  For the consequence, \(f\) is codim-\(1\)-indeterminacy-free iff no prime divisor \(W\) is
729	  contained in \(Z(f)\), which by the equivalence above is iff the \(\ord_W \geq 0\)
730	  condition holds for every prime divisor \(W\).
731	\end{proof}
732	
733	\begin{remark}
734	  \label{rmk:weil_obstruction_application}
735	  \textbf{Why this form is the prover-facing one.} In the consumer chapter
736	  \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension} the Lean prover combines
737	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} with \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}; in
738	  isolation, the abstract codim-\(\geq 2\) statement
739	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} suffices and \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}
740	  is not invoked. The Weil-divisor form is the bridge to the project's
741	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API, which is the form A.4.a's own internal sub-proofs
742	  use when establishing \emph{specific} extensions in genus-\(0\) classifying-map
743	  arguments and in the symmetric-product Albanese chapter
744	  \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}.
745	\end{remark}
746	
747	\begin{lemma}
748	
749	\leanok
750	  [Definedness at a prime divisor, repackaged as a through-the-point partial map]
751	  \label{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle}
752	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.mem_domain_iff_exists_partialMap_through_point}
753	  Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be varieties over \(\bar k\) and let
754	  \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) be a rational map. For a prime divisor
755	  \(W \subseteq X\) with generic point \(\eta_W = W.\mathrm{point}\), the
756	  following are equivalent:
757	  \begin{enumerate}
758	    \item \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) (\(f\) is defined at the generic point of
759	      \(W\)).
760	    \item There exists a partial-map representative \((U, \varphi_U)\) of \(f\) ---
761	      i.e.\ a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) on a dense open
762	      \(U \subseteq X\) whose induced rational map equals \(f\) --- whose domain
763	      \(U\) contains \(\eta_W\).
764	  \end{enumerate}
765	\end{lemma}
766	
767	\begin{proof}
768	
769	  The lemma is a definitional reshuffle of the Mathlib membership predicate
770	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.mem\_domain}, which already states that
771	  \(\eta_W \in \mathrm{Dom}(f)\) iff there is some partial-map representative of
772	  \(f\) defined at \(\eta_W\); the only content here is reordering the two
773	  conjuncts under the existential so the \(\varphi_U \mapsto f\) component lands
774	  first (matching the way later witnesses in the project construct partial-map
775	  representatives). The forward and backward implications are immediate from
776	  the unfolded definition.
777	\end{proof}
778	
779	\begin{remark}
780	  \label{rmk:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle_scope}
781	  \textbf{Relationship to \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}.}
782	  \cref{lem:mem_domain_partial_map_reshuffle} is the iter-179 Lane~D honest
783	  fallback for the substantive Hartshorne-II.6 ``regular = no pole''
784	  reformulation that \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} states in its full
785	  \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form. The reshuffle captures only the
786	  definitional half of the equivalence (existence of a partial-map
787	  representative through a prime-divisor generic point); upgrading it to the
788	  substantive \(\ord_W \geq 0\) form requires the
789	  \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap}-to-function-field pullback machinery, which is
790	  not shipped by Mathlib at commit \texttt{b80f227}. See the iter-179
791	  \texttt{\% NOTE} block on \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} for the
792	  history.
793	\end{remark}

795	\section{Lean encoding}
796	\label{sec:codim1_lean_encoding}
797	
798	The Lean target file is the new
799	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}. The declarations to
800	introduce, in order:
801	
802	\begin{enumerate}
803	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus} ---
804	    \cref{def:indeterminacy_locus}, a method on the Mathlib
805	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap} structure. Implementation: \texttt{X.carrier
806	    \textbackslash{} f.domain} as a \texttt{Set X}, paired with an
807	    \texttt{IsClosed} witness (either bundled or as a separate
808	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.isClosed\_indeterminacyLocus} lemma).
809	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.CodimOneFree} ---
810	    \cref{def:codim_one_indeterminacy}, a \texttt{Prop}-valued predicate. Encoding:
811	    \texttt{\(\forall\) \(\eta\) : X, Order.coheight \(\eta\) = 1 \(\to\) \(\eta\) \(\in\)
812	    f.domain} (matching the \texttt{Order.coheight} idiom of
813	    \cref{def:prime_divisor}).
814	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.localRing\_dvr\_of\_codim\_one} ---
815	    \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr}, a lemma (or instance) producing
816	    \texttt{IsDiscreteValuationRing (Scheme.LocalRing.at\ X \(\eta\))} under the

24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	\leanok
149	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
150	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
151	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
152	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
153	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
154	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
155	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
156	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
157	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
158	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
159	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
160	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
161	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
162	\end{proof}
