215	\end{remark}
216	\begin{lemma}
217	\leanok
218	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
219	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
221	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
222	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let \(X\) be a complete variety, \(Y\) and \(Z\)
223	  % any varieties, and \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) a morphism such that for some
224	  % \(y_0 \in Y\), \(f(X \times \{y_0\})\) is a single point \(z_0\) of \(Z\). Then there is a
225	  % morphism \(g \colon Y \to Z\) such that if \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is the
226	  % projection, \(f = g \circ p_2\)."
227	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
228	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
229	  Let \(X\) be a complete variety and \(Y\), \(Z\) any varieties over \(\bar k\). Let
230	  \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a point \(x_0 \in X\), and suppose given
231	  \(y_0 \in Y\), \(z_0 \in Z\) with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
232	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
233	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
234	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
235	  Then there is a non-empty open subset \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) on which \(f\) agrees, as a
236	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\), where
237	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
238	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)\( is the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
239	  \(U = X \times V\) with \(V := Y - G\), \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))\) for an affine open
240	  neighbourhood \(U_0\) of \(z_0\), and on \(U\) one has \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)\).
241	  \medskip
242	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of \(V\) is exactly
243	  the assertion \(y_0 \notin G\), and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
244	  \subseteq U_0\( keeps the slice over \)y_0\( inside the affine \)U_0\(, away from \)F = Z - U_0$. A
245	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for \(X = Y = Z\) and
246	  \(f = p_1\) the first projection, \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0\)
247	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in \(\{x = x_0\}\), which has empty
248	  interior in the irreducible \(X \times Y\)). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
249	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
250	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
251	  \medskip
252	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
253	  source.} The prose already takes \(\bar k\) algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
254	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
255	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field \(\kappa(y)\) to be \(\bar k\) itself, so that a
256	  proper integral slice has global sections \(\Gamma = \bar k\) and maps to a single \(\bar k\)-point of
257	  the affine \(U_0\); this requires \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\). Second, the globalisation of the
258	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
259	  closed points of the saturated open \(U\) to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ \(U\) to be a Jacobson space.
260	  Over the algebraically closed \(\bar k\) this holds precisely because \(X \times Y\) is \emph{locally of
261	  finite type} over \(\bar k\) (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
262	  property of \(\Spec \bar k\), and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
263	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\). Accordingly the formalization carries
264	  \emph{both} \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and
265	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\) on this lemma (in addition to
266	  \([\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]\)), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
267	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
268	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
269	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
270	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
271	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
272	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) --- the base-field variable is literally named \(\mathtt{kbar}\) ---
273	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over \(\bar k\), so the
274	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
275	\end{lemma}
276	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point \(x_0 \in X\), and define \(g \colon Y \to Z\) by
277	% \(g(y) = f(x_0, y)\). Since \(X \times Y\) is a variety, to show that \(f = g \circ p_2\), it is
278	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of \(X \times Y\). Let
279	% \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), \(F = Z - U\), and \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\);
280	% then \(G\) is closed in \(Y\) since \(X\) is complete and hence \(p_2\) is a closed map. Further
281	% \(y_0 \notin G\) since \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}\). Therefore \(Y - G = V\) is a non-empty
282	% open subset of \(Y\). For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
283	% \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
284	% any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
285	% assertion."
286	\begin{proof}
287	  \leanok
288	  Let \(U_0\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(z_0\) in \(Z\), put \(F = Z - U_0\), and set
289	  \(G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))\). Because \(X\) is complete, the projection \(p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y\) is a
290	  \emph{closed} map, so \(G\) (the image of the closed set \(f^{-1}(F)\)) is closed in \(Y\). The
291	  collapse hypothesis \(f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0\) gives
292	  \(f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing\), hence \(y_0 \notin G\); this is the only place
293	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for \(V := Y - G\) is non-empty precisely
294	  because \(y_0 \in V\). For each \(y \in V\) the proper variety \(X \times \{y\}\) is mapped by \(f\)
295	  into the affine \(U_0\), hence to a single point, necessarily \(f(x_0, y)\). So for all \(x \in X\),
296	  \(y \in V\), we have \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)\), i.e.\ \(f\) and
297	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on the non-empty open \(U := X \times V\). (In the Lean
298	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
299	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
300	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
301	  \medskip
302	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
303	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
304	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
305	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
306	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``\(X\) complete \(\Rightarrow p_2\) closed'' is
307	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
308	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on \(X\), base-changed across the canonical pullback square
309	  to make \(p_2\) universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
310	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites \((\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}\) to
311	  \(\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}\) exactly. This step is already
312	  proven.
313	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of \(V\)) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
314	  \(X \times \{y_0\}\) over the \(\bar k\)-rational point \(y_0\) is exactly the image of the slice
315	  section \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
316	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
317	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
318	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
319	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1\) and
320	  \(s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0\) come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
321	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
322	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
323	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
324	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]\).
325	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
326	  ``Proper connected slice into affine \(\Rightarrow\) single point, glued to a morphism equality on
327	  \(U\)'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
328	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) statement: the
329	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no \(H^0\) base change), and route~(c)
330	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
331	  stack is: for each closed point \(y \in V\) one has \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\) (alg-closed, finite type);
332	  the slice \(X_y\) is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
333	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over \(\bar k\)
334	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
335	  extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring
336	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so \(f\) and
337	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
338	  locally-of-finite-type \(U\) (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
339	  locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
340	  hypothesis \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)), so the per-slice
341	  agreement globalises to all of \(U\) via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
342	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
343	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
344	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
345	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
346	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
347	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
348	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
349	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
350	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
351	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
352	\end{proof}
353	\begin{lemma}
354	\leanok
355	[Bridge~2: slice-constancy on a saturated open mapping into an affine]
356	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
357	  
358	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
359	  % NOTE (iter-161): the iter-160 SIGNATURE GAP is RESOLVED. The Lean proof wires Step 2
360	  % (morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints, PROVEN axiom-clean) over Step 1
361	  % (rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine, the residual sorry). Step 2 needs the closed
362	  % points of U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson; over k̄ this follows from (X⊗Y) being locally of finite
363	  % type, so [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom] is now a stated hypothesis of this lemma (and across the
364	  % chain). With it, the `JacobsonSpace U` instance is a routine discharge (LocallyOfFiniteType.
365	  % jacobsonSpace + JacobsonSpace.of_isOpenEmbedding), no longer an as-typed gap. The only `sorry`
366	  % reachable from this lemma is now Step 1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy). The hypothesis
367	  % set is recorded in the statement and proof prose below (both [IsAlgClosed k̄] AND
368	  % [LocallyOfFiniteType (X⊗Y).hom]); the earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim
369	  % is retired.
370	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
371	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "For each \(y \in V\), the complete variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by
372	  % \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single point of \(U\). But this means that for
373	  % any \(x \in X\), \(y \in V\), \(f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)\), and this proves our
374	  % assertion." [Mumford, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma, p.~43.]
375	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
376	  ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete slice maps into the affine, hence to a single point'' step).}
377	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(X\), \(Y\), \(Z\) be objects of
378	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\) with \(X\) proper, \(X \times Y\) geometrically irreducible, reduced,
379	  and \emph{locally of finite type} over \(\bar k\) (the formalization carries
380	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\), which makes the saturated open \(U\)
381	  below a Jacobson space, so its closed points are dense --- the hypothesis Step~2 of the proof
382	  consumes), and \(Z\) separated. Let \(f \colon X \times Y \to Z\) be a morphism, fix a \(\bar k\)-point
383	  \(x_0 \colon \mathbf 1 \to X\), and write $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,
384	  (\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,(p_2)\( for the ``\)(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$''
385	  endomorphism. Suppose:
386	  \begin{itemize}
387	    \item \(U \subseteq X \times Y\) is a \(p_2\)-\emph{saturated} open, i.e.\ \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\)
388	      for a set \(V_{\mathrm{set}} \subseteq Y\) (so \(U\) contains the whole fibre \(X_y\) over each of
389	      its points);
390	    \item \(U_0 \subseteq Z\) is an \emph{affine} open;
391	    \item \(f(U) \subseteq U_0\), i.e.\ \(f\) maps \(U\) into the affine \(U_0\).
392	  \end{itemize}
393	  Then \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree on \(U\) as scheme morphisms:
394	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\).
395	\end{lemma}
396	\begin{proof}
397	  \leanok
398	  This is the formalization realization of Mumford's step ``for each \(y \in V\), the complete
399	  variety \(X \times \{y\}\) gets mapped by \(f\) into the affine variety \(U\), and hence to a single
400	  point of \(U\)''. We realise it by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route that is
401	  \emph{cohomology-free}; in particular the relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
402	  \(f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O\) framing is a confirmed Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no
403	  \(H^0\) base change) and is \emph{deliberately avoided}. The argument splits into two named
404	  sub-lemmas, one residual and one proven.
405	  \medskip
406	  \noindent\textbf{Step 1 (per-closed-slice constancy) --- \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}, the residual.}
407	  Fix a closed point \(x\) of \(U\), lying over a closed point \(y = p_2(x) \in V_{\mathrm{set}}\).
408	  Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and \(Y\) is of finite type over \(\bar k\), the residue field
409	  is \(\kappa(y) = \bar k\). Because \(U = p_2^{-1}(V_{\mathrm{set}})\) is saturated, \(U\) contains the
410	  \emph{entire} fibre \(X_y \cong X\) over \(y\); hence \(f\) maps the proper integral slice \(X_y\) into the
411	  affine \(U_0\). By \(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\) the global sections \(\Gamma(X_y)\) form a
412	  field, module-finite over \(\bar k\) by \(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\), and
413	  algebraic closedness collapses the finite extension to give \(\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k\). A morphism into
414	  the affine \(U_0\) is pinned by its ring map on global sections (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), so the
415	  restriction of \(f\) to \(X_y\) factors through a single \(\bar k\)-point of \(U_0\) --- necessarily the
416	  point \(f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x)\). Thus \(f\) and \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\)
417	  agree (after the residue-field probe) at every closed point of \(U\). This per-slice statement is the
418	  chain's single genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
419	  \medskip
420	  \noindent\textbf{Step 2 (globalisation over dense closed points) --- \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, proven.}
421	  The closed points are dense in the locally-of-finite-type \(\bar k\)-scheme \(U\)
422	  (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\) / the Jacobson-space property, supplied here by the new hypothesis
423	  \([\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]\)). To turn ``\(f\) and
424	  \(\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f\) agree at each closed point'' into a single morphism equality on \(U\),
425	  the proven globalisation \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} assembles the closed points
426	  into one dominant probe --- the coproduct
427	  \(\coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,U} \Spec \kappa(x) \to U\), whose range is dense --- and feeds
428	  it to the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity handle \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (the same
429	  family \(\mathtt{rigidity\_core}\) uses). This yields the morphism equality
430	  \(U.\iota \fatsemi f = U.\iota \fatsemi (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)\) on all of \(U\). The
431	  ``agrees on a dense set of closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-equality'' connective is the one piece
432	  Mathlib does not package directly (it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
433	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\)); that bespoke build is now done in
434	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}, and it is \emph{not} cohomology. \qedhere
435	\end{proof}
436	\begin{lemma}\leanok
437	[Step~2: morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a Jacobson source are equal]
438	  \label{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
439	  
440	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}
441	  % Project-bespoke assembly: NO external source. Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism
442	  % ext_of_isDominant; the "dense closed points => hom-extensionality" connective is built here.
443	  Let \(W\), \(Z\) be schemes with \(W\) \emph{reduced}, \(W\) a \emph{Jacobson space}, and \(Z\)
444	  \emph{separated}. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon W \to Z\) be morphisms. If, for every \emph{closed} point
445	  \(x \in W\), the two morphisms agree after restriction along the residue-field point
446	  \(\Spec \kappa(x) \to W\) (in Lean, $W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 =
447	  W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\(), then \)g_1 = g_2$.
448	\end{lemma}
449	\begin{proof}
450	\leanok
451	  This is the ``dense closed points \(\Rightarrow\) hom-extensionality'' connective that Mathlib does
452	  not package directly: it supplies only the single-dominant-morphism extensionality
453	  \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) (a dominant morphism into a separated target is an epimorphism for
454	  the purpose of right-cancellation). We assemble the per-closed-point hypotheses into one such
455	  dominant probe.
456	  Form the coproduct \(P := \coprod_{x \in \mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} \Spec \kappa(x)\) and the morphism
457	  \(\mathrm{probe} \colon P \to W\) defined by \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\) of the residue-field points
458	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\). Its topological range contains every closed point of \(W\):
459	  the inclusion \(\iota_x \colon \Spec \kappa(x) \to P\) composes with \(\mathrm{probe}\) to
460	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) (the defining property of \(\mathtt{Sigma.desc}\)), and the
461	  range of \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x\) is the singleton \(\{x\}\)
462	  (\(\mathtt{Scheme.range\_fromSpecResidueField}\)). Because \(W\) is a Jacobson space, the closed points
463	  are dense (\(\mathtt{closure\_closedPoints}\), i.e.\ \(\overline{\mathrm{closedPoints}\,W} = W\)), so
464	  the range of \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dense and \(\mathrm{probe}\) is \emph{dominant}.
465	  Componentwise, \(g_1\) and \(g_2\) agree after \(\mathrm{probe}\): precomposing with each coproduct
466	  inclusion \(\iota_x\) reduces (by \(\mathtt{Sigma.\iota\_desc}\)) to
467	  \(W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_1 = W.\mathtt{fromSpecResidueField}\,x \fatsemi g_2\),
468	  which is the hypothesis at the closed point \(x\). By the universal property of the coproduct
469	  (\(\mathtt{Sigma.hom\_ext}\)) this gives \(\mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_1 = \mathrm{probe} \fatsemi g_2\).
470	  Since \(\mathrm{probe}\) is dominant and \(Z\) is separated, \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isDominant}\) cancels it
471	  on the left to yield \(g_1 = g_2\).
472	\end{proof}
473	\begin{lemma}
474	\leanok
475	[Global-sections constancy: two \(\bar k\)-points of a proper integral source into an affine agree]
476	  \label{lem:eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
477	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral}
478	  % Project-bespoke assembly of Mathlib lemmas: NO external source. Realises, cohomology-free, the
479	  % classical "a global regular function on a proper integral variety over an algebraically closed
480	  % field is constant" --- precisely the affine-image step of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma proof quoted
481	  % above (the "complete slice into an affine maps to a single point" sentence, Ch.~II \S4, p.~43).
482	  Let \(\bar k\) be algebraically closed and let \(W\) be an integral scheme equipped with a structure
483	  morphism \(w \colon W \to \Spec \bar k\) that is universally closed (proper) and locally of finite
484	  type. Let \(g \colon W \to V\) be a morphism into an \emph{affine} scheme \(V\). Then any two
485	  \(\bar k\)-points of \(W\) --- i.e.\ any two sections \(a, b \colon \Spec \bar k \to W\) of \(w\) (so
486	  \(a \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\) and \(b \fatsemi w = \mathbf 1\)) --- satisfy \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\).
487	  \medskip
488	  \noindent\textbf{Every hypothesis is load-bearing} (confirmed by the iter-161 audit).
489	  \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) collapses the finite extension \(\Gamma(W)/\bar k\); integrality of
490	  \(W\) makes \(\Gamma(W)\) a domain (without it a two-point disjoint union is a counterexample);
491	  \(\mathtt{UniversallyClosed}\) is what upgrades \(\Gamma(W)\) to a \emph{field} (without it
492	  \(\mathbb A^1\), with \(\Gamma = \bar k[t]\) and two distinct \(\bar k\)-points, is a counterexample);
493	  \(\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\) gives finiteness of \(\Gamma(W)\) over \(\bar k\); and
494	  \([\mathtt{IsAffine}\ V]\) is what lets \(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\) pin the morphism by its ring map
495	  on global sections.
496	\end{lemma}
497	\begin{proof}
498	\leanok
499	  Because \(W\) is integral and \(w\) is universally closed, the global sections
500	  \(\Gamma(W, \mathcal O_W)\) form a field (\(\mathtt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}\)), and the structure
501	  ring map \(w^\sharp \colon \bar k \to \Gamma(W)\) (the map \(\mathtt{wk.appTop}\) after the canonical
502	  identification \(\Gamma(\Spec \bar k) \cong \bar k\)) is integral / module-finite
503	  (\(\mathtt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}\)). Since \(\bar k\) is algebraically closed and
504	  \(\Gamma(W)\) is a domain finite over it, that map is bijective
505	  (\(\mathtt{IsAlgClosed.ringHom\_bijective\_of\_isIntegral}\)); thus \(\Gamma(W) = \bar k\) and
506	  \(w^\sharp\) is an isomorphism on global sections --- in particular an epimorphism. Both sections
507	  \(a\), \(b\) left-invert \(w^\sharp\) on global sections (they are sections of \(w\)), so they induce the
508	  \emph{same} ring map \(a^\sharp = b^\sharp\) on \(\Gamma(W)\) (cancel the epimorphism \(w^\sharp\)). A
509	  morphism into the \emph{affine} \(V\) is pinned by its global-sections ring map
510	  (\(\mathtt{ext\_of\_isAffine}\)), whence \(a \fatsemi g = b \fatsemi g\). This realises, without
511	  cohomology, the classical ``proper connected variety into an affine is a single point'' step
512	  quoted from Mumford above.
513	\end{proof}

526	  Let \(X \times Y\) be an integral scheme (here it is geometrically irreducible and reduced over
527	  \(\bar k\)). Suppose \(X\) is a \emph{retract} of \(X \times Y\): there is a section
528	  \(s \colon X \to X \times Y\) of the first projection \(p_1\), i.e.\ \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\).

533	  Integrality is reducedness together with irreducibility, and the retract supplies each.
534	  \emph{Reduced.} The section identity \(s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1_X\) makes the pullback on global
535	  sections \(p_1^\sharp \colon \mathcal O_X(X) \to \mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) split
536	  injective (it is left-inverted by \(s^\sharp\)). A split-injective ring map into the reduced ring
537	  \(\mathcal O_{X \times Y}(X \times Y)\) embeds \(\mathcal O_X(X)\) into a reduced ring, so it too is
538	  reduced (\(\mathtt{isReduced\_of\_injective}\) on the split injection); hence \(X\) is reduced.
539	  \emph{Irreducible.} The first projection \(p_1 \colon X \times Y \to X\) is surjective because it
540	  admits the section \(s\) (every point of \(X\) is \(p_1(s(\cdot))\)). The continuous image of an
541	  irreducible space under a surjection is irreducible; as \(X \times Y\) is irreducible and \(p_1\) is a
542	  continuous surjection onto \(X\), the space of \(X\) is irreducible.
543	  Reduced and irreducible together give that \(X\) is integral. This is elementary --- no cohomology,
544	  no external citation --- and feeds the Step-1 geometric assembly below, where the proper slice
545	  \(X_y \cong X\) must be presented as proper \emph{integral}.
546	\end{proof}

594	This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the genus-\(0\) base case. The two
595	load-bearing ingredients of the \textbf{primary route} are the additive decomposition over a
596	product (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and the concrete $\mathbb
597	G_m\(-scaling action \)\sigma_\times\( on \)\mathbb P^1$ (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}); from these
598	the base case ``every morphism \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant'' falls out \emph{in one shot}
599	(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one axis of the
600	decomposition, giving \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense \(\mathbb G_m\). The
601	classical \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive homomorphism step
602	(\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}) and the triviality lemma \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A) = 0\)
603	(\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}) are retained as the Milne-faithful alternative but are
604	\emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path. None of this uses the theorem of the cube, and --- crucially
605	--- none of it uses Milne's Theorem~3.2 / Lemma~3.3 (the rational-map extension): because $f
606	\colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\( is \emph{already} a total morphism out of the complete \)\mathbb
607	P^1$, there is no defect map to extend over a complete surface (see
608	\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}) and
609	Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) are retained in this chapter only as
610	Route-A inputs (the Albanese universal property) and are explicitly \emph{off} the genus-\(0\)
611	path. The genus-\(0\) output is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9, ``every rational
612	(here: regular) map \(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) is constant''.
613	\begin{remark}
614	  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
615	  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-\(0\) path.} An earlier draft of
616	  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
617	  base case ``\(\mathbb P^1 \to A\) constant''. That was a misreading. The base case
618	  (Proposition~3.9, the \(d = 1\) case of Proposition~3.10) follows directly from the Rigidity
619	  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity,
620	  \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) composed with the total \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
621	  action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): the scaling fixed point \(0\) collapses one
622	  axis of the decomposition, yielding \(f(\lambda x) = f(x)\) and hence constancy on the dense
623	  \(\mathbb G_m\) (the primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling shortcut; see
624	  \cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}). This route does not even use the triviality of
625	  \(\mathrm{Hom}(\mathbb G_a, A)\) (\cref{lem:hom_Ga_to_av_trivial}), which is retained only for the
626	  Milne-faithful \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative. Neither the theorem of the cube nor Milne's Theorem~3.2 /
627	  Lemma~3.3 (rational-map extension) is used: since \(f \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A\) is already a
628	  total morphism out of the complete \(\mathbb P^1\), no defect map needs extending over a complete
629	  surface (this corrects the earlier claim that the surface extension was on the genus-\(0\) path;
630	  see \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}). The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) enters only for
631	  projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties, polarizations, and the

27	\section{Tangent space at the identity}
28	\label{sec:pic0_tangent_space}
29	
30	For a \(k\)-group scheme \(G\) locally of finite type, the tangent
31	space at the identity \(\tu T_0 G\) is the \(k\)-vector space of
32	\(k\)-derivations of its local ring at \(e\) into \(k\), or
33	equivalently (and functorially) the set
34	\(G(k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2))_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-points
35	whose underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). For the Picard scheme of a
36	geometrically integral projective \(k\)-scheme, this tangent space is
37	identified with the first sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf via
38	the truncated exponential sequence and the étale-sheaf comparison
39	theorem; on a smooth proper curve, this is the genus-many-dimensional
40	\(k\)-vector space \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\).
41	
42	\begin{theorem}[Tangent space at the identity: \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \cong H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)]
43	  \label{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}
44	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.tangentSpaceIso}
45	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
46	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup,
47	        def:genus}
48	
49	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~thm:tgtsp;
50	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~41 (read from
51	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3265-L3270).
52	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
53	  % "\begin{thm}\label{thm:tgtsp}
54	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
55	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Let \(\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\) denote the
56	  % tangent space at \(0\).  Then
57	  %    \(\)\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}=\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
58	  %  \end{thm}"
59	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
60	  Thm.~thm:tgtsp; cf.\ [Hartshorne], ``Algebraic Geometry'', GTM 52,
61	  IV.1.1 (genus def.\ \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)).}
62	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
63	  \(g = g(C)\) over a field \(k\), and let \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
64	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) be the identity component of the
65	  Picard scheme of \(C/k\). There is a canonical isomorphism of
66	  \(k\)-vector spaces
67	  \[
68	    \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \;\cong\; H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C).
69	  \]
70	  In particular, the \(k\)-dimension of \(\tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k}\) equals
71	  the genus \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\)
72	  (\cref{def:genus}).
73	\end{theorem}
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
76	% L3358-L3367 + L3404-L3407: the construction of the comparison map and
77	% its identification as an isomorphism. Kleiman's full proof spans
78	% L3271-L3408 and is too long for one verbatim block, so we reproduce
79	% the two paragraphs that contain the load-bearing exact sequence and
80	% the conclusion; see Notes for Plan Agent.)
81	% "To compute this kernel, set \(X_\ve:=X\ox_kk_\ve\), and form the truncated
82	% exponential sequence of sheaves of Abelian groups:
83	%    \(\)0\to \mc O_{\!X} \to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^* \to \mc O_{\!X}^* \to 1,\(\)
84	%  where the first map takes a local section \(b\) to \(1+b\ve\).  This
85	% sequence is split by the map \(\mc O_{\!X}^*\to \mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*\) defined by
86	% \(a\mapsto a+0\cdot\ve\).  Hence taking cohomology yields this split exact
87	% sequence of Abelian groups:
88	%    \(\)0\to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}) \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X_\ve}^*)
89	%     \to \tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}^*) \to 1.\(\)"
90	% "In Square (\ref{CD:tgtsp}), the two vertical maps are isomorphisms by
91	% Exercise~\ref{ex:Alr} since \(\IPic_{X/k}\) represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\) and
92	% \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, \(v\)
93	% too is an isomorphism, as desired."
94	\begin{proof}
95	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
96	        thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}
97	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~thm:tgtsp. By
98	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, the Picard scheme
99	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists and represents the étale-sheafified relative
100	  Picard functor \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\); the identity component
101	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an open subscheme of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
102	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}), so the tangent
103	  spaces at the identity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) and of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
104	  agree; we may therefore work with \(\Pic_{C/k}\) throughout.
105	
106	  \emph{Tangent space via dual numbers.} Let
107	  \(k_\varepsilon := k[\varepsilon]/(\varepsilon^2)\) be the ring of
108	  dual numbers. For any \(k\)-scheme \(P\) locally of finite type and
109	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in P(k)\), the Zariski tangent space
110	  \(\tu T_e P\) is in functorial bijection with the set
111	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\) of \(k_\varepsilon\)-valued points whose
112	  underlying \(k\)-point is \(e\). When \(P\) is a \(k\)-group scheme,
113	  the natural homomorphism \(k_\varepsilon \to k\) (\(\varepsilon
114	  \mapsto 0\)) induces a group homomorphism
115	  \(P(k_\varepsilon) \to P(k)\) whose kernel coincides with
116	  \(P(k_\varepsilon)_e\); under this identification the vector-space
117	  addition on \(\tu T_e P\) corresponds to the group multiplication on
118	  the kernel. Specialising to \(P = \Pic_{C/k}\),
119	  \[
120	    \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}
121	    \;=\; \ker\bigl(\Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) \to \Pic_{C/k}(k)\bigr).
122	  \]
123	
124	  \emph{Computing the kernel via the truncated exponential sequence.}
125	  Set \(C_\varepsilon := C \otimes_k k_\varepsilon\). On the étale
126	  site of \(C\) the truncated exponential sequence of sheaves of
127	  abelian groups
128	  \[
129	    0 \to \mathcal{O}_C \to \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}
130	        \to \mathcal{O}_C^{\times} \to 1,
131	  \]
132	  whose first map sends a local section \(b\) to \(1 + b\varepsilon\),
133	  is split by \(a \mapsto a + 0 \cdot \varepsilon\). Taking sheaf
134	  cohomology produces a split exact sequence
135	  \[
136	    0 \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)
137	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times})
138	      \to H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \to 1.
139	  \]
140	
141	  \emph{Identification with the tangent space.} Because \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
142	  represents the étale-sheafification of
143	  \(T \mapsto H^1(C_T, \mathcal{O}_{C_T}^{\times})\), there is a
144	  commutative diagram of abelian groups
145	  \[
146	    \begin{array}{ccc}
147	      H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_{C_\varepsilon}^{\times}) &
148	        \longrightarrow & H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C^{\times}) \\
149	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \\
150	      \Pic_{C/k}(k_\varepsilon) & \longrightarrow &
151	        \Pic_{C/k}(k),
152	    \end{array}
153	  \]
154	  whose vertical maps are isomorphisms after base change to the
155	  algebraic closure \(\bar k\) (Kleiman~\S 5, Exercise~ex:Alr;
156	  invocation legitimised by
157	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Taking horizontal
158	  kernels yields a \(k\)-linear comparison map
159	  \(v : H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) \to \tu T_0 \Pic_{C/k}\) which is an
160	  isomorphism after base change to \(\bar k\), hence already an
161	  isomorphism over \(k\) (the source and target are finite-dimensional
162	  \(k\)-vector spaces, and an isomorphism of \(k\)-vector spaces is
163	  detected after a faithfully flat extension).
164	
165	  \emph{Compatibility with the additive structure.} The diagram of the
166	  preceding paragraph is functorial in \(k\)-linear endomorphisms of
167	  \(k_\varepsilon\); in particular scalar multiplication by \(a \in
168	  k\) on the dual numbers corresponds, on
169	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\), to scalar multiplication by \(a\) in the
170	  natural \(k\)-module structure. Hence \(v\) is a homomorphism of
171	  \(k\)-vector spaces, as required.
172	
173	  Finally, by the genus definition (Hartshorne~IV.1.1,
174	  \cref{def:genus}), \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = g(C)\); thus
175	  \(\dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\).
176	\end{proof}
177	
178	\section{Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
179	\label{sec:pic0_smooth}
180	
181	By the tangent-space dimension bound for a Noetherian local ring,
182	\(\dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g\), with
183	equality if and only if \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular at \(0\). For a
184	smooth proper curve, this inequality becomes an equality: smoothness
185	at the identity propagates to smoothness everywhere by
186	translation, hence \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth \(k\)-group scheme of
187	dimension \(g\).
188	
189	\begin{theorem}[Smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
190	  \label{thm:pic0_smooth}
191	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.smooth}
192	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
193	        def:pic_zero_subscheme,
194	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
195	
196	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0;
197	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~44 (read from
198	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3421-L3427 +
199	  % L3442-L3446).
200	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
201	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:sm} Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field
202	  % \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\).  Then
203	  %    \(\)\dim \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X}).\(\)
204	  %  Equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(0;\) if so, then
205	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\)
206	  % everywhere.
207	  %  \end{cor}"
208	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
209	  % "\begin{cor}\label{cor:ch0}
210	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\). Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists
211	  % and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\et}\). If \(k\) is of characteristic \(0\), then
212	  % \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth of dimension \(\dim\tu H^1(\mc O_{\!X})\) everywhere.
213	  %  \end{cor}"
214	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
215	  Cor.~cor:sm + Cor.~cor:ch0; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties''
216	  (course notes, 2008), \S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e), p.~86 (dimension of \(J\)
217	  equals the genus).}
218	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
219	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
220	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is smooth over \(k\) of dimension
221	  \(g\): the structural morphism
222	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is smooth, and every closed point of
223	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) lies in a smooth open neighbourhood.
224	\end{theorem}
225	
226	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
227	% L3428-L3439: the proof of Cor.~cor:sm. We reproduce the full proof,
228	% which deduces smoothness everywhere from smoothness at \(0\) by
229	% translation.)
230	% "\begin{proof} Plainly we may assume \(k\) is algebraically closed. Then,
231	% given any closed point \(\lambda\in\IPic_{X/k}\), there is an automorphism
232	% of \(\IPic_{X/k}\) that carries 0 to \(\lambda\), namely, ``multiplication''
233	% by \(\lambda\).  So \(\IPic_{X/k}\) has the same dimension at \(\lambda\) as
234	% at 0, and \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is
235	% smooth at 0.
236	%
237	% By general principles, \(\dim_0 \IPic_{X/k}\le \dim\tu T_0\IPic_{X/k}\),
238	% and equality holds if and only if \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\).
239	% Moreover, \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is regular at \(0\) if and only if it is smooth
240	% at \(0\) since \(k\) is algebraically closed.  Therefore, the corollary
241	% results from Theorem~\ref{thm:tgtsp}.
242	%  \end{proof}"
243	\begin{proof}
244	  \uses{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso,
245	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
246	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
247	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:sm, with the curve-specific dimension
248	  input supplied by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}.
249	
250	  Since smoothness of a scheme over \(k\) descends along faithfully
251	  flat extensions, we may replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure
252	  \(\bar k\) (using \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
253	  to identify \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\)).
254	  Over \(\bar k\) every closed point of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
255	  \(\bar k\)-rational, and the group law translates the identity \(0\)
256	  onto an arbitrary closed point: the translation morphism
257	  \(t_\lambda : \Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(x \mapsto x +
258	  \lambda\), is an automorphism by
259	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}. Consequently
260	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) has the same local dimension at \(\lambda\) as at
261	  \(0\), and is smooth at \(\lambda\) if and only if it is smooth at
262	  \(0\).
263	
264	  By the standard tangent-space dimension bound, for any Noetherian
265	  local ring \((A, \mathfrak{m})\) with residue field \(k\) one has
266	  \(\dim A \le \dim_k (\mathfrak{m}/\mathfrak{m}^2)\), with equality
267	  if and only if \(A\) is regular. Over an algebraically closed field,
268	  regularity at a \(\bar k\)-rational point is equivalent to
269	  smoothness. Hence
270	  \(
271	    \dim_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} \le \dim_k \tu T_0 \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)
272	  \)
273	  by \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso}, with equality iff
274	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is regular (equivalently, smooth) at \(0\).
275	
276	  It remains to show that for \(C/k\) a smooth proper curve, equality
277	  holds (so smoothness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) follows). For a smooth
278	  proper curve there is no obstruction: as Kleiman~\S 5 Ex.~ex:jac
279	  notes, projectivity and flatness of \(C/k\) together with the
280	  smoothness of \(C\) over \(k\) imply that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a
281	  smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension \(p_a(C) =
282	  g(C)\). In characteristic zero this reduces to Cartier's theorem
283	  (Kleiman~\S 5 Cor.~cor:ch0): every \(k\)-group scheme locally of
284	  finite type over a field of characteristic zero is smooth; in
285	  positive characteristic the smoothness uses the curve-specific
286	  Hodge-theoretic vanishing
287	  \(H^2(C, \mathcal{O}_C) = 0\) (the source-target of the obstruction
288	  to lifting tangent vectors to first-order deformations), which holds
289	  for any curve since \(C\) has dimension \(1\). In either case
290	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is smooth at \(0\); by the translation argument
291	  above, it is smooth everywhere.
292	
293	  Thus \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth and
294	  \(\dim \Pic^0_{C/k} = g(C)\), completing the proof.
295	\end{proof}
296	
297	\section{Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
298	\label{sec:pic0_proper}
299	
300	A smooth proper geometrically integral curve is geometrically normal,
301	so Kleiman's \(\Pic^0\)-projectivity theorem applies and upgrades the
302	quasi-projective conclusion to projective: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
303	projective over \(k\), in particular proper.
304	
305	\begin{theorem}[Properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
306	  \label{thm:pic0_proper}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.proper}
308	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
309	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
310	
311	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Thm.~th:qpp\&p;
312	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
313	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2935-L2940).
314	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
315	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:qpp&p}
316	  %  Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(X/k\) is projective
317	  % and geometrically integral.  Then \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) exists and is
318	  % quasi-projective.  If also \(X/k\) is geometrically normal, then
319	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is projective.
320	  %  \end{thm}"
321	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5,
322	  Thm.~th:qpp\&p; cf.\ [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes,
323	  2008), \S I.6 (abelian varieties are projective, p.~27).}
324	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
325	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
326	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is projective over \(k\), in
327	  particular proper: the structural morphism
328	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k}) \to \Spec k\) is a projective morphism, hence
329	  satisfies the universal closedness and separatedness conditions of
330	  properness.
331	\end{theorem}
332	
333	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
334	% L2941-L2984: the proof of th:qpp&p uses the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
335	% structure theorem to reduce projectivity to ruling out non-constant
336	% maps from the multiplicative group; the curve case relies on
337	% geometric normality of \(C\).)
338	% "Suppose \(X\) is also geometrically normal.  Since \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is
339	% quasi-projective, to prove it is projective, it suffices to prove it is
340	% proper.  By Lemma~\ref{lem:agps}, forming \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) commutes
341	% with extending \(k\).  And by \cite[2.7.1(vii)]{EGAIV2}, a \(k\)-scheme is
342	% complete if (and only if) it is after extending \(k\).  So we may, and do,
343	% assume \(k\) is algebraically closed.
344	%
345	% Recall the structure theorem of Chevalley and Rosenlicht for algebraic
346	% groups, or reduced connected group schemes of finite type over \(k\); see
347	% \cite[Thm.~1.1, p.~3]{Co02}.  The theorem says that every algebraic
348	% group is an extension of an Abelian variety (or complete algebraic
349	% group) by a linear (or affine) algebraic group.  Recall also that every
350	% solvable linear algebraic \(k\)-group is triangularizable (the
351	% Lie--Kolchin theorem); so, if it's nontrivial, then it contains a copy
352	% of the multiplicative group or of the additive group; see \cite[(10.5)
353	% and (10.2)]{Bo69}.  Now,  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is commutative, so solvable.
354	% Hence it suffices to show that, if \(T\) denotes the
355	% affine line minus the origin, then every \(k\)-map \(t\:T\to
356	% (\IPicz_{X/k})_{\red}\) is constant."
357	\begin{proof}
358	  \uses{thm:fga_pic_representability,
359	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
360	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
361	  We follow Kleiman~\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, specialised to \(X = C\) a
362	  smooth proper geometrically integral curve.
363	
364	  \emph{Quasi-projectivity.} By
365	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) exists,
366	  represents \(\Pic_{(C/k)\et}\), and is locally of finite type; its
367	  identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite type by
368	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}.
369	  Kleiman's quasi-projectivity statement
370	  (\S 5 Thm.~th:qpp\&p, first conclusion) then gives that
371	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, since \(C/k\)
372	  is projective and geometrically integral.
373	
374	  \emph{Reduction to algebraically closed \(k\).} Properness descends
375	  through faithfully flat extensions of the base field
376	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 2.7.1(vii)), and formation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
377	  commutes with extension of \(k\)
378	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}). Hence it
379	  suffices to prove that \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k} =
380	  \Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\) is proper, and we may assume
381	  \(k = \bar k\) is algebraically closed throughout.
382	
383	  \emph{Reduction via Chevalley--Rosenlicht.} A quasi-projective
384	  scheme is projective if and only if it is proper, so it suffices to
385	  show that \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is proper over \(\bar k\). By the
386	  Chevalley--Rosenlicht structure theorem (Conrad, ``A modern proof of
387	  Chevalley's theorem on algebraic groups'', Thm.~1.1, p.~3), every
388	  reduced connected algebraic \(\bar k\)-group is an extension of an
389	  abelian variety by a linear (affine) algebraic group. The
390	  multiplicative group \(\mathbb{G}_m\) and the additive group
391	  \(\mathbb{G}_a\) generate every connected solvable linear group
392	  (Lie--Kolchin); since \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is commutative, hence
393	  solvable, properness will follow once we show that every
394	  \(\bar k\)-morphism \(t : \mathbb{G}_m \to (\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\red}\)
395	  is constant.
396	
397	  \emph{No non-constant map from \(\mathbb{G}_m\) (uses geometric
398	  normality of \(C\)).} The smooth proper curve \(C/\bar k\) is
399	  in particular geometrically normal. Suppose
400	  \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\), where \(T = \mathbb{A}^1
401	  \setminus \{0\}\) is the multiplicative group; by the comparison
402	  theorem (Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:cmp) \(t\) is represented by an
403	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times T\). Since
404	  \(C \times T\) is normal and integral, every invertible sheaf is the
405	  sheaf of a Cartier divisor: write
406	  \(\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{O}_{C \times T}(D)\) for some divisor \(D\)
407	  (Hartshorne II Ex.~6.15). Restricting \(\mathcal{L}\) to the generic
408	  fibre of the projection \(p : C \times T \to C\): the generic fibre
409	  is \(T\) localised at the generic point of \(C\), and an invertible
410	  sheaf on an open subscheme of \(\mathbb{A}^1\) is trivial. Hence
411	  there is a rational function \(\phi\) on \(C \times T\) such that
412	  \((\phi) + D\) restricts to the trivial divisor on the generic
413	  fibre. Pick any section \(s : C \to C \times T\) of \(p\) (e.g.\ at
414	  a closed point of \(T\)), and set \(E := s^*((\phi) + D)\); then
415	  \(E\) is a divisor on \(C\) with the property that \(p^* E\) and
416	  \((\phi) + D\) coincide as cycles, hence as divisors (Altman--Kleiman
417	  AK70, Prp.~3.10, p.~139, valid since \(C \times T\) is normal).
418	  Therefore \(\mathcal{L} \cong p^* \mathcal{O}_C(E)\), so the
419	  associated map \(t : T \to \Pic^0_{C/\bar k}\) factors through the
420	  generic fibre and is constant. This rules out non-constant maps
421	  \(\mathbb{G}_m \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\); the additive case
422	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is analogous (and follows from the
423	  multiplicative case via the standard embedding
424	  \(\mathbb{G}_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb{G}_m\) on solvable
425	  subgroups). Hence the linear part in the Chevalley--Rosenlicht
426	  decomposition of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is trivial, so \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is
427	  an abelian variety; in particular, it is proper. Combined with
428	  quasi-projectivity, this yields projectivity of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
429	  over \(\bar k\), hence over \(k\) by faithfully flat descent of
430	  properness.
431	\end{proof}
432	
433	\section{Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
434	\label{sec:pic0_geom_irred}
435	
436	Geometric irreducibility is delivered directly by the abstract
437	identity-component substrate
438	(\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}) applied
439	to the \(k\)-group scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\). We restate the
440	specialisation here for the assembly downstream.
441	
442	\begin{theorem}[Geometric irreducibility of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)]
443	  \label{thm:pic0_geom_irred}
444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.geometricallyIrreducible}
445	  \uses{def:pic_zero_subscheme,
446	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
447	
448	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0;
449	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~36 (read from
450	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2921-L2929).
451	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
452	  % "\begin{prp}\label{prp:pic0}
453	  %   Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\).  Assume \(\IPic_{X/k}\)
454	  % exists and represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\).  Then \(\IPic_{X/k}\) is
455	  % separated, and it is smooth if it has a geometrically reduced open
456	  % subscheme.  Furthermore, the connected component of the identity
457	  % \(\IPicz_{X/k}\) is an open and closed group subscheme of finite type; it
458	  % is geometrically irreducible; and forming it commutes with extending
459	  % \(k\).
460	  %  \end{prp}"
461	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Prp.~prp:pic0
462	  (geometric irreducibility of the connected component of the
463	  identity).}
464	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
465	  field \(k\). The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
466	  (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is geometrically irreducible: after
467	  base change to the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), the scheme
468	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) is irreducible.
469	\end{theorem}
470	
471	\begin{proof}
472	  \uses{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible,
473	        thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes,
474	        thm:fga_pic_representability}
475	  Apply the abstract identity-component substrate to the \(k\)-group
476	  scheme \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\), which is locally of finite type by
477	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. By
478	  \cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}, the
479	  identity component \((\Pic_{C/k})^0 = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is of finite
480	  type and geometrically irreducible over \(k\). The base-change
481	  compatibility of
482	  \cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes} identifies
483	  \((\Pic^0_{C/k})_{\bar k}\) with \(\Pic^0_{C_{\bar k}/\bar k}\),
484	  closing the proof.
485	\end{proof}

487	\section{Assembly: \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety}
488	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
489	
490	A \(k\)-group scheme is an \emph{abelian variety} iff its underlying
491	\(k\)-scheme is a complete (proper) connected group variety
492	(Milne~\S I.1, p.~8). Combining the smoothness, properness, and
493	geometric irreducibility statements of this chapter with the
494	identity-component group-scheme structure of
495	\cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent} packages \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an
496	abelian variety of dimension \(g\). This is the load-bearing A.3.vii
497	gate for Route~A.
498	
499	\begin{theorem}[\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety]
500	  \label{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}
501	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0.isAbelianVariety}
502	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
503	        thm:pic0_proper,
504	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
505	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}
506	
507	  % SOURCE: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
508	  % \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety) (read from
509	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF p.~14).
510	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
511	  % "A complete connected group variety is called an abelian variety. As we
512	  % shall see, they are projective, and (fortunately) commutative. Their
513	  % group laws will be written additively. Thus \(t_a\) is now denoted
514	  % \(x \mapsto x + a\) and \(e\) is usually denoted \(0\)."
515	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac;
516	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~51 (read from
517	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L3990-L3996).
518	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
519	  % "\begin{rmk}\label{rmk:Jac}
520	  %  Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric fibers are
521	  % connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
522	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
523	  % Exercise~REF."
524	  \textit{Source: [Milne], ``Abelian Varieties'' (course notes, 2008),
525	  \S I.1, p.~8 (definition of abelian variety); [Kleiman], ``The
526	  Picard scheme'', \S 5, Rmk.~rmk:Jac (the Jacobian
527	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) of a smooth proper family of curves is a
528	  projective abelian \(S\)-scheme).}
529	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
530	  genus \(g\) over a field \(k\). The identity component
531	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_zero_subscheme}) is an
532	  \emph{abelian variety over \(k\)} of dimension \(g\): the underlying
533	  \(k\)-scheme is smooth (\cref{thm:pic0_smooth}), proper
534	  (\cref{thm:pic0_proper}), and geometrically irreducible
535	  (\cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}), and it carries a \(k\)-group-scheme
536	  structure inherited from \(\Pic_{C/k}\) via the inclusion
537	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
538	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}). In
539	  particular, \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is automatically commutative (Milne~\S
540	  I.1, Cor.~1.4).
541	\end{theorem}
542	
543	\begin{proof}
544	  \uses{thm:pic0_smooth,
545	        thm:pic0_proper,
546	        thm:pic0_geom_irred,
547	        thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism,
548	        thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}
549	  We assemble Milne's defining axioms of an abelian variety from the
550	  four conjuncts established earlier in this chapter and in
551	  \cref{chap:Picard_IdentityComponent}.
552	
553	  \emph{(i) Group-variety structure.} By
554	  \cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism} applied to
555	  \(G = \Pic_{C/k}\) (a \(k\)-group scheme locally of finite type by
556	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}), the identity component
557	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from
558	  \(\Pic_{C/k}\) compatible with the clopen inclusion.
559	
560	  \emph{(ii) Smoothness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}, the structural
561	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is smooth (of relative
562	  dimension \(g\)). In particular \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth
563	  \(k\)-variety: it is reduced (Milne's standing convention bundles
564	  reducedness into ``variety''), geometrically reduced (by
565	  smoothness over \(k\)), and locally of finite type.
566	
567	  \emph{(iii) Properness.} By \cref{thm:pic0_proper}, the structural
568	  morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\) is proper (in fact
569	  projective). This is Milne's ``complete'' axiom.
570	
571	  \emph{(iv) Connectedness (geometric irreducibility).} By
572	  \cref{thm:pic0_geom_irred}, the underlying scheme
573	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is geometrically irreducible; in particular it is
574	  geometrically connected, hence connected.
575	
576	  Combining (i)--(iv): \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is a smooth, proper,
577	  geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme equipped with a
578	  \(k\)-group-scheme structure. By Milne~\S I.1, p.~8, ``a complete
579	  connected group variety is called an abelian variety''. Thus
580	  \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is an abelian variety over \(k\); commutativity is
581	  automatic (Milne~\S I.1, Cor.~1.4). Its dimension is the relative
582	  dimension of the smooth morphism \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec k\),
583	  which equals \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal{O}_C)\) by
584	  \cref{thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso} and \cref{thm:pic0_smooth}.
585	
586	  This is the content of Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac: for \(X/S\)
587	  projective and smooth with connected curve fibres of genus
588	  \(g > 0\) (specialise to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\)), the Jacobian
589	  \(J := \Pic^0_{X/S}\) exists and is a projective abelian
590	  \(S\)-scheme. The genus-zero case is degenerate
591	  (\(\Pic^0_{C/k} = 0\), Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)) but is
592	  consistent with the assembly: a point scheme \(\Spec k\) is
593	  trivially an abelian variety of dimension \(0\).
594	\end{proof}

653	\section{Out of scope}
654	\label{sec:pic0_isAbelianVariety_out_of_scope}
655	
656	This chapter packages \emph{only} the five A.3.iii--vii steps. The
657	following downstream constructions live in separate sibling chapters
658	and are explicitly out of scope here:
659	
660	\begin{itemize}
661	  \item \textbf{Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)}
662	    (A.4.d.ii) --- the universal property of \(f_P : C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\)
663	    is the content of \cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}, gated on this
664	    chapter via \cref{thm:pic0_isAbelianVariety}.
665	  \item \textbf{Autoduality \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \cong (\Pic^0_{C/k})^\vee\)}
666	    (Milne~\S III.6, Thm.~6.6; Kleiman~\S 5 Rmk.~rmk:Jac, Abel map) ---
667	    not needed for the Jacobian's existence as an abelian variety,
668	    hence out of scope.
669	  \item \textbf{Torsion component \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k}\)}
670	    (Kleiman~\S 6) --- for a smooth proper curve
671	    \(\Pic^\tau_{C/k} = \Pic^0_{C/k}\), so the finer torsion-component
672	    finiteness statements of Kleiman~\S 6 are not load-bearing on
673	    Route~A.
674	  \item \textbf{Genus-zero degenerate case.} When \(g(C) = 0\),
675	    \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the trivial group scheme \(\Spec k\)
676	    (Milne~\S III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e)); this is consistent with the
677	    abelian-variety assembly (a point is trivially an abelian variety
678	    of dimension \(0\)) and is handled uniformly here, but is not
679	    separately stated.
680	\end{itemize}

745	  homomorphism. Form
746	  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
747	  the difference of the two regular maps \(A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B\)
748	  and \(A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B\). Then
749	  \(\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0\) and \(\varphi\) vanishes on both axes,
750	  \(\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)\) (since \(\alpha(0) = 0\)). By the additive
751	  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with \(V = W = A\)), the axis-restrictions
752	  of \(\varphi\) being \(0\) force \(\varphi \equiv 0\); equivalently, \(\varphi\) collapses the complete
753	  axis \(A \times 0\) and vanishes on \(0 \times A\), so Rigidity gives \(\varphi = 0\). Hence
754	  \(\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')\), i.e.\ \(\alpha\) is a homomorphism.

756	\begin{lemma}
757	[A rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends (Milne Theorem 3.2)]
758	  \label{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}
759	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_to_av_extends}
760	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2 (with Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.3), \S I.3, doc p.~16--18 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 22--24)
761	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map α:V ⇢ A from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
762	  % variety is defined on the whole of V."
763	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Thm 3.1, used in the proof): "THEOREM 3.1. A rational map φ:V ⇢ W from a
764	  % normal variety V to a complete variety W is defined on an open subset U of V whose
765	  % complement V∖U has codimension ≥ 2."
766	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Lemma 3.3, used in the proof): "LEMMA 3.3. Let φ:V ⇢ G be a rational map from
767	  % a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either φ is defined on all of V or the points
768	  % where it is not defined form a closed subset of pure codimension 1 in V (i.e., a finite
769	  % union of prime divisors)."
770	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2 (with Theorem~3.1, Lemma~3.3).}
771	  \textbf{Route-A-only (iter-164).} This extension lemma is \emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical
772	  path; the 4th route (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}, \cref{def:gaTranslationP1}) closes the
773	  base case without it. It is retained here only as an input to Route~A (the positive-genus
774	  Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6) and carries the route's riskiest sub-build
775	  (\cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}).
776	  A rational map \(\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A\) from a nonsingular variety \(V\) over
777	  \(\bar k\) to an abelian variety \(A\) is defined on the whole of \(V\).
778	\end{lemma}
779	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma." [Milne, Thm 3.2, \S I.3,
780	% doc p.~17.]
781	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Thm 3.1, general case): "Let U be the largest subset on which φ is
782	% defined, and suppose that V∖U has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor Z in V∖U.
783	% Because V is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete valuation ring O_Z with field of
784	% fractions k(V). The map φ defines a morphism of schemes Spec(k(V)) → W, which the valuative
785	% criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a morphism Spec(O_Z) → W.
786	% This implies that φ has a representative defined on an open subset that meets Z in a nonempty
787	% set, which is a contradiction." [Milne, Thm 3.1, \S I.3, doc p.~17.]
788	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Lemma 3.3, the difference-map): "Define a rational map Φ:V×V ⇢ G,
789	% (x,y) ↦ φ(x)·φ(y)⁻¹. ... Thus φ is defined at x if and only if Φ is defined at (x,x). ...
790	% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
791	% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,
792	% and when we identify it with a subset of V, it is a subset of V of codimension one passing
793	% through x on which φ is not defined." [Milne, Lemma 3.3, \S I.3, doc p.~17--18.]
794	\begin{proof}
795	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
796	  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
797	  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
798	  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-\(1\) point), a rational map from the
799	  normal \(V\) to the complete \(A\) is defined outside a closed set of codimension \(\geq 2\). By
800	  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
801	  empty or pure of codimension \(1\): forming the difference map
802	  \(\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A\), one shows
803	  \(\varphi\) is defined at \(x\) iff \(\Phi\) is defined at the diagonal point \((x, x)\), and an
804	  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-\(1\) component of the non-domain. The two
805	  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence \(\alpha\) is everywhere
806	  defined.
807	\end{proof}
808	\begin{remark}
809	  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
810	  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-\(1\) step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
811	  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-\(2\) is within
812	  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
813	  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
814	  discrete valuation ring \(\mathcal O_{V, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\). But Lemma~3.3,
815	  which rules out codimension-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
816	  Milne via Weil-divisor theory (\(\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1\), prime divisors,
817	  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
818	  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
819	  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) separately, that the
820	  morphism \(\Spec k(V) \to A\) extends over \(\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}\) using only that \(A\) is
821	  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at \(Z\) without
822	  globally building \(\mathrm{div}(f)\)) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely.
823	  \textbf{This surface extension is \emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) critical path (corrected, iter-164).}
824	  An earlier draft routed the genus-\(0\) base case through this extension, by collapsing the
825	  additive-defect map \(\psi(x, y) = f(x + y) - f(x) - f(y)\) on the \emph{affine} surface
826	  \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) and then extending \(\psi\) over the complete surface
827	  \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) --- which genuinely \emph{would} need Theorem~3.2's surface case
828	  (Milne Theorem~3.4 / Lemma~3.3). That detour is unnecessary. The 4th route
829	  (\cref{rmk:base_case_fourth_route}) replaces the two-variable affine defect map by the
830	  \emph{total} translation action \(\sigma \colon \mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb G_a \to \mathbb P^1\)
831	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}): \(h := \sigma \fatsemi f\) is a total morphism on $\mathbb P^1
832	  \times \mathbb G_a\( (no extension needed), and the complete-source factor is \)\mathbb P^1$
833	  itself, so the additive decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} applies directly.
834	  Consequently \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Theorem~3.2), the codim-\(1\) indeterminacy
835	  sub-build (Lemma~3.3), and the Weil-divisor gap discussed above are all confined to
836	  \textbf{Route~A} (the positive-genus Albanese universal property, Milne \S III.6); they are
837	  retained in this chapter solely as Route-A inputs and may be deferred until Route~A consumes
838	  them. The genus-\(0\) close no longer depends on any of them.
839	\end{remark}
840	\section{The genus-\(0\) base objects and the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling action (the primary route)}
841	\begin{definition}
842	[Concrete genus-\(0\) base objects: \(\mathbb P^1\), \(\mathbb G_a\), \(\mathbb G_m\) over \(\bar k\)]
843	  \label{def:genus0_base_objects}
844	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.ProjectiveLineBar}
845	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of standard objects; the mathematics is textbook (Hartshorne
846	  % I.2--3 for the projective line; the group laws on \(\mathbb A^1\) are elementary). No external
847	  % % SOURCE QUOTE is needed for the Lean encoding itself.
848	  Work over \(\Spec \bar k\) with \(\bar k\) algebraically closed. The primary \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling
849	  route (and its \(\mathbb G_a\)-additive alternative) needs only three concrete objects of
850	  \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\):
851	  \begin{itemize}
852	    \item \(\mathbb P^1 = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), the projective line --- a smooth proper
853	      geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(0\) (Mathlib: projective space, or \(\mathrm{Proj}\)
854	      of the graded polynomial ring; \lean name \texttt{ProjectiveLineBar}). It carries
855	      the distinguished \(\bar k\)-points \(0\), \(1\), \(\infty\) and the two standard affine charts
856	      \(\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\) (coordinate \(x\)) and
857	      \(\mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0\}\) (coordinate \(u = 1/x\)).
858	    \item \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1_{\bar k}\), the affine line as the \emph{additive} group object
859	      (Mathlib: \texttt{AffineSpace}\,\(\mathbb A(1; \Spec \bar k)\) with the \(\mathtt{GrpObj}\)
860	      additive structure \((x, y) \mapsto x + y\), identity \(0\); \lean name \texttt{Ga}).
861	      As a scheme \(\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}\), and the open
862	      immersion \(\mathbb G_a \hookrightarrow \mathbb P^1\) has dense (cofinite) image.
863	    \item \(\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{0, \infty\}\), the
864	      \emph{multiplicative} group object (group law \((x, y) \mapsto xy\), identity \(1\); \lean name
865	      \texttt{Gm}). Its open immersion into \(\mathbb P^1\) also has dense image.
866	  \end{itemize}
867	  All three are of finite type over \(\bar k\); \(\mathbb P^1\) is proper, while \(\mathbb G_a\) and
868	  \(\mathbb G_m\) are affine (hence not complete). These are the only ``genus-\(0\)'' geometric inputs
869	  the base case needs.
870	\end{definition}

964	\subsection*{Chart cover and chart-ring iso (formalisation infrastructure)}
965	The next three declarations are iter-168 formalisation infrastructure that the chartwise
966	construction of the \(\mathbb G_m\)-scaling action \(\sigma_\times\) (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1})
967	and its downstream consumers (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}) ultimately rest on.
968	They realise, at the Lean level, the standard ``affine chart of \(\mathbb P^1\) is $\mathbb
969	A^1\('' picture: a two-chart affine open cover of \)\overline{\mathbb P^1}\( by \)D_+(X_0)$ and
970	\(D_+(X_1)\); the identification of each chart ring with the one-variable polynomial ring
971	\(\bar k[u]\); and the resulting reducedness of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) as a scheme. None
972	of them is on the genus-\(0\) critical path conceptually, but each is consumed by the Lean
973	formalisation downstream.
974	\begin{definition}
975	[Two-chart affine open cover of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\)]
976	  \label{def:projlinebar_affine_cover}
977	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.projectiveLineBarAffineCover}
978	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of the textbook fact that \(\mathbb P^1\) is covered by its
979	  % two standard affine charts \(D_+(X_0)\) and \(D_+(X_1)\); the construction is built on top of
980	  % Mathlib's \(\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}\). No external % SOURCE QUOTE
981	  % needed for the Lean encoding itself.
982	  The two-chart affine open cover of \(\overline{\mathbb P^1}\) by the standard \(D_+(X_0)\) and
983	  \(D_+(X_1)\), built by specialising Mathlib's
984	  \(\mathrm{Proj.affineOpenCoverOfIrrelevantLESpan}\) to the index set
985	  \(\iota = \{0, 1\} = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\) with family \(f = (X_0, X_1)\) and weights
986	  \(m = (1, 1)\). The non-trivial input is that the irrelevant ideal of the bi-graded
987	  \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) is contained in \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\{X_0, X_1\}\), which holds by
988	  writing any irrelevant element as a sum of monomials of strictly positive total degree
989	  (so each monomial is divisible by \(X_0\) or \(X_1\)). This cover packages the two affine
990	  charts into the data that Mathlib's \(\mathrm{IsReduced.of\_openCover}\) and
991	  \(\mathrm{Scheme.Cover.glueMorphisms}\) APIs consume, and is the chartwise glue for the
992	  iter-169 attack on the body of \(\sigma_\times = \mathtt{gmScalingP1}\)
993	  (\cref{def:gaTranslationP1}).
994	\end{definition}

998	  \label{def:proj_chart_ring_iso}

1004	  degree-zero part \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) of the localization of the standard
1005	  \(\mathbb N\)-graded polynomial ring \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) at \(X_i\) --- is isomorphic as a
1006	  \(\bar k\)-algebra to the one-variable polynomial ring \(\bar k[u]\), where the affine

1039	  \circ \mathrm{inverse} = \mathrm{id}_{\bar k[u]}$ is already proven axiom-clean.

1041	  ``cancel surjective'' proof: from surjectivity of the inverse
1042	  \(\bar k[u] \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\)

1049	\begin{lemma}
1050	\leanok
1051	[Surjectivity of the inverse chart-ring map \(\bar k[u] \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\)]
1052	  \label{lem:mvPoly_to_homogeneousLocalization_away_surjective}
1053	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway_surjective}
1054	  % Archon-original Lean encoding of an elementary corollary of a Mathlib lemma. The
1055	  % external authority is the Mathlib statement
1056	  % \texttt{HomogeneousLocalization.Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}
1057	  % (\texttt{Mathlib/RingTheory/GradedAlgebra/HomogeneousLocalization.lean:1064}); we do
1058	  % not include a verbatim \% SOURCE QUOTE because no Mathlib source quote is currently
1059	  % retained under \texttt{references/} for this lemma (Mathlib source files are not
1060	  % mirrored in the project's reference cache). The mathematics is standard.
1061	  For each \(i \in \{0, 1\}\), the ring map
1062	  \[ \mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i \colon
1063	     \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k \longrightarrow
1064	     \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i \]
1065	  --- the inverse direction of the chart-ring iso of \cref{def:proj_chart_ring_iso},
1066	  built via \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial.eval}_2\mathrm{Hom}\) as the ring map sending the
1067	  generator \(u \in \mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\) to the homogeneous-localisation
1068	  element \(X_{1-i}/X_i \in \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) and the constants
1069	  \(r \in \bar k\) along the composite $\bar k \to \mathcal A_0 \to \mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal
1070	  A\,X_i\( --- is surjective onto \)\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i$.
1071	  \medskip
1072	  \textit{Proof.} The image of \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\)
1073	  is the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\) generated by the
1074	  single localised element \(X_{1-i}/X_i\), since \(\mathrm{MvPolynomial}\,\{*\}\,\bar k\)
1075	  is generated as a \(\bar k\)-algebra by its single generator \(u\) and the base-ring map
1076	  \(\bar k \to \mathcal A_0\) is bijective (via the canonical identification of degree-zero
1077	  scalars in the \(\bar k\)-algebra \(\bar k[X_0, X_1]\) with \(\bar k\)). Mathlib's lemma
1078	  \(\mathrm{HomogeneousLocalization.Away.adjoin\_mk\_prod\_pow\_eq\_top}\) says that for any
1079	  multi-index \(v \colon \iota' \to A\) of homogeneous elements of degree \(dv\) generating
1080	  \(\mathcal A\) up to degree \(d\), the \(\mathcal A_0\)-subalgebra of
1081	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,(\prod v_i^{dv_i})\) generated by the family of ratios
1082	  \(\{v_i^{dv_i}/\prod v_j^{dv_j}\}\) is the whole ring \(\top\). Specialising to
1083	  \(d = 1\), \(\iota' = \mathrm{Fin}\,2\), \(v = (X_0, X_1)\), and exponent vector
1084	  \(dv = (1, 1)\), the relevant generator \(\prod v_i^{dv_i} = X_0 \cdot X_1\) collapses
1085	  along the localisation at \(X_i\) (the \(X_i\)-component is already a unit, so only the
1086	  \(X_{1-i}/X_i\) ratio remains as a non-trivial generator), and the conclusion is that
1087	  \(\mathrm{Algebra.adjoin}\,\mathcal A_0\,\{X_{1-i}/X_i\} = \top\) inside
1088	  \(\mathrm{Away}\,\mathcal A\,X_i\). This is exactly the image of
1089	  \(\mathrm{mvPolyToHomogeneousLocalizationAway}_i\), so the map is surjective. \qed
1090	\end{lemma}

1700	  \noindent\textbf{(IV) Substantive ring-level residual.} All three pickup paths reduce,
1701	  after their structural setup, to the same ring-level identity. On the basic-open
1702	  intersection \(D_+(X_0 \cdot X_1) \subseteq \overline{\mathbb P^1}\) both chart
1703	  coordinates are units (the chart-\(0\) coordinate \(t = X_1/X_0\) and the chart-\(1\)
1704	  coordinate \(u = X_0/X_1\) are reciprocals: \(t \cdot u = 1\)), and the agreement
1705	  reduces to the ring-level identity
1706	  \[ \lambda \cdot u \ =\ \frac{1}{t} \cdot \lambda \quad
1707	     \text{in } \mathrm{Localization.Away}\,t \otimes_{\bar k} \mathtt{GmRing}\,\bar k, \]
1708	  i.e.\ to the unit-of-the-localisation identity \(t \cdot u = 1\)
