388	  \end{itemize}
389	  The dominant block is (A.2). It can be started in parallel with the
390	  genus-\(0\) stack because it is file-disjoint and its prerequisites are
391	  Mathlib-side rather than project-side.
392	  \paragraph{Route B --- Symmetric powers and Stein factorisation (historical alternative; not pursued).}
393	  \textbf{Iter-144 disposition.} Route B is a historical alternative \emph{not pursued} by the project. It is preserved here for scholarly context only. Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA --- is the committed M3 route per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition. The decomposition, gating Mathlib pieces, and midpoint LOC figure (\(\sim 9000\) LOC) recorded below are the iter-123 audit values (\texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the chapter does not re-cost Route B under current Mathlib snapshots.
394	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
395	  \begin{itemize}
396	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The \(g\)-fold symmetric power.} Form the product \(C^g\) and let \(S_g\) act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient \(C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g\) exists as a \(k\)-scheme, smooth and proper over \(k\) of dimension \(g\). Smoothness follows because the action of \(S_g\) on the smooth \(k\)-scheme \(C^g\) is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of \(C^g\) via the geometric-quotient property.
397	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(k)\). The map
398	    \[
399	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
400	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)
401	    \]
402	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the \(Q_i\) and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, \(\sigma\) is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree \(g\) on \(C\) has \(h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1\), so the fibre \(\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])\) is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces \(\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)\) over the locus where \(h^0 > 1\).
403	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because \(\sigma\) is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre \(\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k\) is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
404	    \[
405	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
406	    \]
407	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of \(\sigma\) are connected, this intermediate scheme equals \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore \(\Pic^g_{C/k}\) itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension \(g\). Translating by \(-g P_0\) yields \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\), and we take \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
408	  \end{itemize}
409	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route B.} Each sub-step is blocked by a different missing piece.
410	  \begin{itemize}
411	    \item Sub-step B.1 requires symmetric powers of schemes (the construction \(\Sym^g\) for a \(k\)-scheme of finite type) and finite-group-scheme quotients of schemes (the quotient \(C^g / S_g\)). Mathlib has finite-group quotients of types and of various algebraic structures, but not yet a usable scheme-level quotient by a finite group action with the smooth-target property.
412	    \item Sub-step B.2 requires Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, including the relative version for families of curves --- the cohomology-and-base-change machinery for proper morphisms.
413	    \item Sub-step B.3 requires the Stein factorisation theorem for proper morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes (\(f \colon X \to Y\) proper \(\implies\) \(f_* \mathcal O_X\) is a coherent \(\mathcal O_Y\)-algebra). Mathlib has partial coherence-theoretic infrastructure but does not yet have the cohomology of \(\mathcal O\)-modules of finite type along proper morphisms in the form needed.
414	  \end{itemize}
415	  \paragraph{Genus-\(0\) sub-case (absorbed into the same witness).}
416	  When \(g(C) = 0\) the witness's underlying scheme is \(J = \Spec k\), and the universal property reduces to a rigidity statement.
417	  \begin{itemize}
418	    \item[\textbf{(C.1)}] \emph{Brauer--Severi identification.} A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve \(C\) over \(k\) with \(g(C) = 0\) and a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\) is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_k\). This is the Brauer--Severi triviality criterion: a Brauer--Severi variety of dimension \(1\) is \(\mathbb P^1\) over its base if and only if it has a rational point. The genus-\(0\) identification on the curve side uses \(h^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2\) (Riemann--Roch) to produce a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_k\), which is then an isomorphism by degree-and-dimension.
419	    \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1\) to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure \(\bar k\), since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\) and the genus-\(0\) identification \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) is only available after passing to \(\bar k\) (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexamples to a \(k\)-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over \(k\) --- equality of the given morphism with the constant morphism as \(k\)-schemes --- then follows by \emph{descent}: a morphism equality that holds after base change to \(\bar k\) descends to \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) (Sub-step~C.2.f).
420	    \begin{itemize}
421	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.a)}] \emph{Statement (over \(\bar k\)).} Let \(A\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\), with identity element \(\eta_A \in A(\bar k)\). Let
422	      \[
423	        f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \longrightarrow A
424	      \]
425	      be a morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes such that \(f(p) = \eta_A\) for some closed point \(p \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}(\bar k)\). Then \(f\) factors as the composite \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A\); equivalently, \(f\) is the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\).
426	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let \(c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) denote the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\) --- the unique \(\bar k\)-morphism factoring through the closed-point section \(\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A\). The conclusion \(f = c\) follows from Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} applied to \(g_1 := f\), \(g_2 := c\), \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), \(Y := A\), once we produce a non-empty open \(U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) on which \(U.\iota \circ f = U.\iota \circ c\) as scheme morphisms. (After the iter-125 refactor, the cited theorem is stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated; the source-side group-object decoration that the earlier formulation carried has been dropped. \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) satisfies the refactored hypotheses trivially --- it is smooth, hence reduced; it is geometrically irreducible over \(\bar k\); and \(A\) is proper hence separated over \(\bar k\).) The reduction therefore amounts to: produce a non-empty open \(U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) on which \(f\) and \(c\) agree as scheme morphisms; we will in fact take \(U = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\).
427	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism \(f\) goes from a proper geometrically integral \(\bar k\)-scheme of dimension \(1\) to a smooth proper \(\bar k\)-scheme of dimension \(g := \dim_{\bar k} A\). The set-theoretic image \(f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A\) is an irreducible closed subset of \(A\) (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most \(1\). If \(g = 0\) then \(A = \Spec \bar k\) and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique \(\bar k\)-morphism to \(\Spec \bar k\) is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume \(g \geq 1\), in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of \(A\) --- an irreducible rational curve in \(A\).
428	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: morphisms from \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) to an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly \(1\) --- is ruled out by the classical fact that, for an abelian variety \(A\) of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), every morphism \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant. The project ranks three proof routes for this fact; route~(c) is \textbf{committed}, with (b) and (a) as fallbacks.
429	      \begin{itemize}
430	        \item[\textbf{(c) [committed; characteristic-free]}] \emph{Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free).} Over \(\bar k\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-\(0\) curve is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}), and every morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) to an abelian variety is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); a fortiori the morphism \(f\) is constant. The constancy of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is \emph{not} supplied by the theorem of the cube. The base case is the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) group-law incompatibility (Milne Proposition~3.9, \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}): the additive-defect map \(\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)\) on \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\) is extended to the complete surface \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) (rational-map extension \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}, Milne Thm~3.2/3.4) and then collapsed by the \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford Form~I --- an elementary closed-map argument), forcing \(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) to be an additive homomorphism; the same argument on \(\mathbb G_m\) produces a multiplicative relation incompatible with it unless \(f\) is constant. The additivity Corollary~1.5 (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) packages the same collapse over a product. Combining with the genus-\(0\) classification yields the headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain uses \emph{neither} the theorem of the cube, \emph{nor} \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre duality, \emph{nor} the Picard representability engine, and is valid in arbitrary characteristic; it is developed in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}. It is the committed route for the genus-\(0\) keystone; the over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\)) is the fallback-(a) artifact, not consumed here.
431	        \item[\textbf{(b) [fallback]}] \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along \(f\) induces a homomorphism of group schemes \(f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})\), where \(A^{\vee}\) is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on \(A\)). The Picard group of \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) has trivial degree-zero component, \(\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0\), because every line bundle on \(\mathbb P^1\) of degree \(0\) is isomorphic to \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}\) (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence \(f^{\vee} = 0\). By autoduality of abelian varieties (\(A^{\vee\vee} = A\) canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of \(f^{\vee}\) implies that \(f\) itself is constant. This route is free once Route~A's \(\Pic^0\) engine exists, but it \emph{couples} the genus-\(0\) rigidity to the Route~A representability monster, which the committed route~(c) deliberately avoids.
432	        \item[\textbf{(a) [fallback; off the critical path; carries a characteristic-\(p\) gap]}] \emph{Via the cotangent bundle (the differential \(df = 0\) route).} The cotangent sheaf \(\Omega_{A/\bar k}\) of an abelian variety is a trivial \(\mathcal O_A\)-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along \(f\) yields a morphism of locally free sheaves \(f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\), whose global sections form a \(\bar k\)-linear map
433	        \[
434	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
435	        \]
436	        Triviality of \(\Omega_{A/\bar k}\) means the left-hand side is a non-zero \(\bar k\)-vector space (of dimension \(g\)); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential \(df\) to vanish identically on \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\). Smoothness of \(A\) over \(\bar k\) together with the vanishing of \(df\) forces \(f\) to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism. This is the \emph{differential} route: it requires the Serre-duality-flavoured vanishing \(H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0\) together with the cotangent-triviality of \(\Omega_A\), is retained in the tree via the closed chart-algebra envelope (which supplies only the converse implication \(df = 0 \Rightarrow f\) constant), and carries a characteristic-\(p\) gap. It is \emph{not} the committed route.
437	      \end{itemize}
438	      Each route yields: \(f\) is constant. The pointed condition \(f(p) = \eta_A\) then identifies the constant value, so \(f = c\) on underlying topological points.
439	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality \(f = c\) on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open \(U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and \(A\) is separated over \(\bar k\) (proper implies separated), so a \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, \(f|_U = c|_U\) as scheme morphisms for the open \(U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives \(f = c\) as scheme morphisms.
440	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Descent of morphism equality from \(\bar k\) to \(k\).} The rigidity conclusion of Sub-step~C.2.d is proved over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\), where the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}) carries the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and, being characteristic-free, no \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\). To obtain the corresponding equality of morphisms over the base field \(k\), we descend along the base change \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\).
441	      Concretely, let \(f, c \colon C \to A\) be two \(k\)-morphisms (in the application \(f\) is the given map and \(c\) the constant map at \(\eta_A\)). Base-change along \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) produces \(f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k} \colon C_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) together with the projections \(p \colon C_{\bar k} = C \times_k \bar k \to C\) and \(q \colon A_{\bar k} \to A\), fitting the base-change squares \(q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p\) and \(q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p\). Suppose \(f_{\bar k} = c_{\bar k}\) (this is what \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} delivers over \(\bar k\)). Then \(f \circ p = q \circ f_{\bar k} = q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p\), so \(f\) and \(c\) agree after pre-composition with \(p\).
442	      It remains to right-cancel \(p\). The morphism \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) is faithfully flat (a field extension is a faithfully flat ring map) and surjective; both properties are stable under base change, so the projection \(p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C\) is faithfully flat and surjective. By \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}, \(p\) is therefore an epimorphism of schemes, and right-cancelling it in \(f \circ p = c \circ p\) gives \(f = c\) over \(k\). \emph{This closing epi-cancellation is the only genuinely \(\sim 2\)-line part.} The descent as a whole is \textbf{not} a \(\sim 2\)-line step but a multi-iteration base-change sub-build: neither this project nor Mathlib provides a base-change functor \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k) \to \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\), so even producing \(f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k}\) and the base-change squares \(q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p\) above first requires (a) that functor; (b) transfer of every relevant instance along it (\(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\,1\), \(\mathtt{IsProper}\), \(\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\), \(\mathtt{GrpObj}\)); (c) stability of \(\genus = 0\) under the base change \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) for a geometrically irreducible curve; and (d) the base-change-square commutativity identities. All four must be in place before the closing epi-cancellation applies; only that final cancellation is short, while the surrounding base-change infrastructure is the bulk of the cost.
443	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement.} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-\(0\) curve over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) to a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over \(\bar k\) is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project records the committed, characteristic-free form as the named declaration \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis); the older over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose base field still carries both \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\), is retained only as the fallback-(a) artifact. The \textbf{committed} proof route is route~(c) of C.2.d: the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free) (\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, headline \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} feeding \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}), which is characteristic-free and uses neither the theorem of the cube, nor Serre duality, nor the Picard engine. The differential decomposition into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)--(iii) --- (i) cotangent triviality of a group scheme over \(\bar k\) via the functorial shear iso; (ii) scheme-level \(\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow\) \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass plus the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}; (iii) characteristic-\(p\) handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius \(F_X\) --- is the \textbf{fallback} route~(a), off the critical path but retained in the tree (each piece an upstream Mathlib gap-fill in its own right; see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}). When \(g = \dim A = 0\) the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (after the iter-125 refactor, this is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib. The descent step C.2.f --- from \(\bar k\) back to the base field \(k\) --- has a genuinely short \emph{final} move (the epi-cancellation via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}, already in Mathlib in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat}), but the descent as a whole is \emph{not} a \(\sim 2\)-line step: it is a multi-iteration base-change sub-build, because neither this project nor Mathlib provides a base-change functor \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k) \to \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)\), and the instance transfer, genus-stability, and base-change-square identities (C.2.f items (a)--(d)) must all be built before the closing epi-cancellation applies.
444	    \end{itemize}
445	    \item[\textbf{(C.3)}] \emph{The trivial witness.} Take \(J := \Spec k\), the terminal \(k\)-scheme. The group-object structure on \(\Spec k\) is trivial; it is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of relative dimension \(0 = g(C)\). The unique morphism \(\iota_P \colon C \to \Spec k\) sends \(P\) to the (unique) point. By Sub-step C.2 every \(f \colon C \to A\) with \(f(P) = \eta_A\) is constant at \(\eta_A\), hence factors uniquely through the unique morphism \(g \colon \Spec k \to A\) sending the point to \(\eta_A\); uniqueness of \(g\) follows because \(\Spec k \to A\) is determined by its value on the unique \(k\)-rational point of \(\Spec k\).
446	  \end{itemize}
447	  \emph{Mathlib status for the genus-\(0\) sub-case.}
448	  \begin{itemize}
449	    \item Sub-step C.1 requires the genus-\(0\) identification on the curve side, which in turn requires Serre duality / the basic Riemann--Roch infrastructure that this project has explicitly trimmed (see the project's Phase~C exit policy). Mathlib has partial \(\mathbb P^1\)-theory but the curve-side genus-\(0\) identification is missing.
450	    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~REF of Chapter~REF), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged characteristic-free as \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}; the over-\(\bar k\) \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the committed route~(c) chain. The descent of morphism equality from \(\bar k\) back to the base field \(k\) (Sub-step~C.2.f) has a short \emph{final} epi-cancellation (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}) but is, as a whole, a multi-iteration base-change sub-build rather than a \(\sim 2\)-line step (see the re-costing at C.2.f).
451	    \item Sub-step C.3 is unconditional once C.1 and C.2 are in place.
452	  \end{itemize}
453	  \paragraph{Mathlib infrastructure summary.}
454	  Per the iter-144 M3 disposition (STRATEGY.md \S~M3), the project commits to Route A for the positive-genus arm and treats Route B as a historical alternative not pursued. The Mathlib build-outs gating the two surviving arms are therefore:
455	  \begin{itemize}
456	    \item[\textbf{(\(\alpha\))}] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure plus FGA representability (COMMITTED M3 infrastructure --- iter-144 disposition).} This is the committed Mathlib build-out that unlocks Route A, which in turn discharges the positive-genus arm of the witness existence (\cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). It is \textbf{mandatory and unconditional for the positive-genus witness \emph{object}}: that object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety (\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)), and no other route in this chapter produces it. In particular the critical path here is \emph{independent of the genus-\(0\) route choice} --- the genus-\(0\) arm is decoupled onto the trivial object \(\Spec k\) (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}) and the characteristic-free rigidity statement \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, neither of which touches the Picard engine. Route~A is the single highest-cost build-out, since Hilbert/Quot schemes are upstream of much of modern algebraic geometry; but once present, the rest of Route A (identity component, smoothness, properness, universal property --- the last being the Albanese property of \(\Pic^0\), Milne \emph{Abelian Varieties} Proposition~6.1) follows by classical arguments already widely formalised in adjacent settings.
457	    \item[\textbf{(\(\gamma\))}] \emph{The rigidity statement for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-\(0\) curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\).} The genus-\(0\) arm needs \emph{only} this rigidity \emph{statement}, \textbf{decoupled} from both the cotangent-vanishing pile and the Picard representability of (\(\alpha\)): its witness object is the trivial \(\Spec k\), and the statement is the project's named, characteristic-free declaration \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}), carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis (the older \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, still carrying both \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \([\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]\), is the retained fallback-(a) artifact). The \textbf{committed} proof route is the characteristic-free Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free) of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (route~(c): headline \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}, Milne Proposition~3.10 specialised to \(\mathbb P^1\)), which uses neither the theorem of the cube, nor Serre duality, nor representability. The differential decomposition into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} --- cotangent triviality of a group scheme over \(\bar k\) via the functorial shear iso; scheme-level \(\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow\) \(f\) factors through \(\Spec \bar k\) via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass; characteristic-\(p\) handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius \(F_X\) --- is the \textbf{fallback} route~(a), off the critical path but retained in the tree. The genus-\(0\) arm of the witness existence is packaged as \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}), which over a general base field \(k\) \emph{descends} the over-\(\bar k\) rigidity conclusion to \(k\): for each \(k\)-rational marked point \(P \in C(k)\) it base-changes the data \((C, A, f, P)\) along \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve, so \(C_{\bar k}\) is again a genus-\(0\) curve), applies \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} over \(\bar k\) to conclude \(f_{\bar k}\) is constant, then descends the morphism equality back to \(k\) along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) (Sub-step~C.2.f, whose closing move is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). The \(C(k) = \emptyset\) branch is vacuously true (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\)). Independently of (\(\alpha\)), establishing (\(\gamma\)) together with the descent step (C.2.f --- a multi-iteration base-change sub-build, not a \(\sim 2\)-line step; see the re-costing there) lets the genus-\(0\) part of the witness be discharged unconditionally via \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}; the higher-genus part is packaged as \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}, iter-134 scaffold) whose body closure is gated on Route A (\(\alpha\)). Per the iter-135 body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, the witness existence is now a case-split \(\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0\) delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} respectively, converting the formerly inline \texttt{sorry} into honest delegation to the two named scaffolds.
458	    \item[\textbf{(\(\beta\), historical only --- not pursued).}] \emph{Symmetric powers of schemes plus finite-group-scheme quotients plus Stein factorisation for proper morphisms.} Documented as a historical alternative not pursued by the project (Route B above); see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route B for context. This entry is retained for scholarly continuity only; under the iter-144 disposition, (\(\beta\)) is \emph{not} a build-out the project is gating any of its deliverables on, and (\(\beta\)) does not appear on any active critical path.
459	  \end{itemize}
460	  In sum: Mathlib currently contains neither of the two active infrastructure builds ((\(\alpha\)) and (\(\gamma\))), and the existence statement is recorded here as the project's single explicit foundational hypothesis. It backs all downstream consequences --- the four protected instances on \(\Jac(C)\) (Theorems~REF--REF), and the Abel--Jacobi map and its universal property of Chapter~REF --- and is discharged once (\(\alpha\)) and (\(\gamma\)) both land. The historical entry (\(\beta\)) is preserved for scholarly context only and is not part of the project's discharge path.
461	  \paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.} The Lean body of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is, per iter-135 refactor, a case-split \(\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0\) delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (genus-\(0\) arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing iter-151+ under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- see \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} body closure status, revised iter-145) and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (positive-genus arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window). The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).
462	\end{proof}
463	\subsection{The genus-\(0\) arm of the witness existence}
464	\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}
465	The genus-\(0\) sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-\(0\) branch can be discharged \emph{independently of Route~A}. Its witness object is the trivial scheme \(\Spec k\); the only remaining mathematical content is the rigidity statement \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}, the characteristic-free keystone of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (route~(c)), \emph{not} the differential / Serre-duality route and \emph{not} the Picard representability engine. (The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact, not the keystone the genus-\(0\) witness consumes.)
466	\subsection{Route (c): rigidity via Milne \S I.3 (cube-free; committed genus-\(0\) proof route)}
467	\label{sec:av_rigidity_route_c}
468	The committed genus-\(0\) route~(c) --- the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack --- is now developed in its own chapter, \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, which sits \emph{upstream} of this chapter in the import graph: it covers \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean}, which imports \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Genus} and is imported by \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian}. This relocation is precisely what breaks the former \texttt{RigidityKbar}\,\(\to\)\,\texttt{Rigidity}\,\(\to\)\,\texttt{Jacobian} import cycle, so that \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} can consume the keystone directly. The genus-\(0\) arm consumes that chapter's headline \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} --- the characteristic-free replacement (\emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis) for the over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, which remains in tree as the fallback-(a) artifact and still carries \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\).
469	The chain of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} is \textbf{cube-free}: the theorem of the cube is \emph{not} on the genus-\(0\) path (it enters Milne's theory only later, for projectivity, the seesaw principle, dual abelian varieties, and the Poincar\'e sheaf). It has four links, all resting on Milne \S I.3. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- cohomology-free --- and supplies the collapse of a complete-factor map. From it, the \emph{additive decomposition} Corollary~1.5 (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}) and \emph{Corollary~1.2} that a pointed regular map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism follow directly. The single-curve base case --- that every morphism \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A\) is constant --- is supplied \emph{not} by the cube but by the \(\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m\) group-law incompatibility (Milne Proposition~3.9, \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}): the additive-defect map \(\psi(x,y) = f(x+y) - f(x) - f(y)\), defined a priori only on the affine surface \(\mathbb G_a \times \mathbb G_a\), is extended to the complete surface \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) by the rational-map-extension Theorem~3.2/3.4 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and then collapsed by the Rigidity Lemma, forcing \(f|_{\mathbb G_a}\) to be an additive homomorphism; the parallel relation \(f(xy) = f(x) + f(y) + c\) on \(\mathbb G_m\) is incompatible with it unless \(f\) is constant. This gives \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}; composing with the genus-\(0\) classification \(C \cong \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) over \(\bar k\) (\cref{thm:genus_zero_curve_iso_p1}) yields \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain uses \emph{neither} the theorem of the cube, \emph{nor} \(H^0(C, \Omega_C) = 0\) / Serre duality, \emph{nor} the FGA \(\Pic\) representability engine, so the genus-\(0\) arm is fully \textbf{decoupled} from Route~A; the differential / Serre-duality argument survives only as the off-critical-path fallback~(a) of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}. The route's \textbf{riskiest sub-build} is the surface rational-map-extension step (Milne Lemma~3.3, pure-codimension-\(1\) indeterminacy on \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\), for which Mathlib has no Weil-divisor theory; see \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} \cref{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}), which is genuinely on the genus-\(0\) critical path and cannot be deferred to Route~A.
470	\begin{theorem}\leanok
471	[Genus-\(0\) Albanese witness]
472	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
473	  
474	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
475	  Let \(C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with \(\genus(C) = 0\). Then there exists a \(\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C\) whose underlying scheme is \(\Spec k\) (the terminal object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\)), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension \(0\), properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose \(\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\) field encodes the Albanese universal property for every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\).
476	\end{theorem}
477	\begin{proof}
478	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
479	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take \(J := \mathbf{1}\), the terminal object of \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), which on underlying schemes is \(\Spec k\) viewed as a \(k\)-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
480	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object \(\mathbf{1}\) of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to \(\mathbf{1}\), and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism \(\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}\) is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:134}--\texttt{140}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on \(\Spec k\) from the fact that, on the geometric fibre \(\Spec K\) over any field extension \(k \to K\), the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and \(\Spec K\) is irreducible.
481	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension \(\genus\,C\).} The smoothness witness \(\mathtt{smoothGenus}\) is required at relative dimension \(\genus\,C\). The hypothesis \(\genus\,C = 0\) rewrites the target relative dimension to \(0\), after which the goal reduces to \(\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})\). This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension \(0\).
482	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every \(k\)-rational marked point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\), we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple \((C, P, J)\).
483	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map \(\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}\) (the structure morphism of \(C\) as a \(k\)-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition \(P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}\) holds automatically: both sides are morphisms \(\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\), and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
484	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme \(A\) over \(k\) with identity element \(\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) and a morphism \(f \colon C \to A\) with \(P \circ f = \eta_A\), we must exhibit a unique morphism \(g \colon J \to A\) such that \(f = \alpha \circ g\). \emph{Existence}: take \(g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\). Then \(\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\), which equals \(f\) precisely when \(f\) is the constant morphism at \(\eta_A\). We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\). Base-change the data along \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\): the curve \(C_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus \(0\) (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme \(A_{\bar k}\) is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point \(P_{\bar k}\) is a \(\bar k\)-rational point, and \(f_{\bar k}\) still kills it: \(f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\). By \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (the project's characteristic-free genus-\(0\) rigidity keystone of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\) and \emph{no} \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\) hypothesis; it lives in the upstream \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean}, imported by \texttt{Jacobian.lean}, so consuming it here introduces no import cycle), \(f_{\bar k}\) equals the constant morphism \(\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}\) over \(\bar k\). Finally we descend this morphism equality back to \(k\): the projection \(p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C\) is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\), hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling \(p\) against the base-change-square identities yields \(f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A\) as \(k\)-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: if \(g_1, g_2 \colon \mathbf{1} \to A\) both satisfy \(f = \alpha \circ g_i\) with \(\alpha = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C\), then \(\alpha \circ g_1 = \alpha \circ g_2\), and we right-cancel \(\alpha\). This is the \emph{same} epi-cancellation as in the Existence paragraph: the underlying scheme morphism of \(\alpha\) is \(C.\mathtt{hom} \colon C \to \Spec k\) (\texttt{Over.toUnit\_left}), which is faithfully flat and surjective (a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve is faithfully flat and surjective over its base field), hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; therefore \(\alpha\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) by \texttt{Over.epi\_of\_epi\_left}, and \texttt{cancel\_epi} gives \(g_1 = g_2\). Uniqueness does \emph{not} follow from terminality of \(\mathbf{1}\): morphisms \emph{out} of a terminal object are global points and are not unique in general (terminality gives uniqueness only of morphisms \emph{into} \(\mathbf{1}\)).
485	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the \(C(k) = \emptyset\) branch.} When no \(k\)-rational point \(P : \mathbf{1} \to C\) exists (Brauer--Severi conics over \(\mathbb Q\) are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier \(\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J\) is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's \(\forall\) over an empty type is trivially true. The construction handles this branch implicitly without any explicit case split.
486	  \paragraph{Body closure status.} The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness} (in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean}) is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is gated on the body of the upstream keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (M2.a; in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean}, imported by \texttt{Jacobian.lean}), whose \textbf{committed} proof route is route~(c) --- the characteristic-free Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free) of \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, which is decoupled from the theorem of the cube, Route~A representability, and Serre duality. (The over-\(\bar k\) declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact, not the keystone consumed here.) The differential decomposition into pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}) is the retained \emph{fallback} route~(a), off the critical path. (The chart-algebra envelope of the fallback supplies only the converse implication \(df = 0 \Rightarrow\) constant, and carries a characteristic-\(p\) gap; the committed route~(c) avoids that gap entirely.)
487	\end{proof}

519	\begin{theorem}
520	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) (Milne III Prop 6.1)]
521	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
522	  
523	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic.albaneseUP}
524	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104 (PDF p.~110) (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110)
525	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).
526	  % The map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any
527	  % map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
528	  % to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
529	  % \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
530	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Prop 6.4, the symmetric variant invoked through Cor 1.5;
531	  % read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 111): "PROPOSITION
532	  % 6.4. Let \(A\) be an abelian variety over \(k\). For any map
533	  % \(\varphi\colon C\times C\to A\) such that \(\varphi(\Delta)=0\), there is a
534	  % unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that \(\psi\circ F=\varphi\)."
535	  % SOURCE QUOTE (Remark 6.5, the Albanese characterization; read from
536	  % references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 111): "REMARK 6.5. The
537	  % proposition says that \((A,F)\) is the Albanese variety of \(C\) in the
538	  % sense of Lang 1959, II 3, p45. Clearly the pairs \((J,f^P)\) and \((J,F)\)
539	  % are characterized by the universal properties in (6.1) and (6.4)."
540	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, III \S 6, Proposition 6.1 (p.~104), with Proposition 6.4 and Remark 6.5 (p.~105).}
541	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus \(g \geq 1\) over \(k\), fix a \(k\)-rational point \(P \in C(k)\), write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) for the identity component of its Picard scheme, and let \(f^P \colon C \to J\), \(Q \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]\), be the Abel--Jacobi morphism. For any morphism \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) from \(C\) to an abelian variety \(A\) over \(k\) with \(\varphi(P) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique homomorphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) of group schemes such that \(\varphi = \psi \circ f^P\). Equivalently, \(J\) together with \(f^P\) exhibits \(J\) as the Albanese object of the pointed curve \((C, P)\) in the sense of \cref{def:IsAlbanese}.
542	\end{theorem}
543	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Consider the map
544	% \((P_1,\dots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A\). Clearly this is
545	% symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\). It therefore defines a
546	% rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2) shows to be a regular map.
547	% It is clear from the construction that \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that
548	% \(f^P\) is the composite of \(Q\mapsto Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\) with
549	% \(f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J\)). In particular, \(\psi\) maps \(0\) to \(0\), and
550	% (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore a homomorphism. If \(\psi'\) is a second
551	% homomorphism such that \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\)
552	% agree on \(f^P(C)+\dots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
553	% [Milne, Prop 6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104.]
554	\begin{proof}
555	  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, III \S 6, Proposition 6.1.}
556	  Symmetrise the \(g\)-fold sum: the morphism \((Q_1, \dots, Q_g) \mapsto \sum_i \varphi(Q_i) \colon C^g \to A\) is symmetric in the factors and so factors through the \(g\)-fold symmetric product \(C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g\). Composing with the inverse of the Abel--Jacobi map \(f^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to J\), which is birational by Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch (its generic fibre is a single point), one obtains a \emph{rational} map \(\psi \colon J \dashrightarrow A\). By Milne Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined --- the composite of Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-\(2\) via the valuative criterion with Lemma~3.3's pure-codimension-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety) the rational map \(\psi\) extends uniquely to a regular morphism \(J \to A\). The identity \(\psi \circ f^P = \varphi\) holds by construction once \(f^P\) is identified as the composite \(Q \mapsto Q + (g - 1)P \colon C \to C^{(g)}\) followed by \(f^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to J\). The pointed condition \(\psi(0_J) = \eta_A\) then promotes \(\psi\) to a group-scheme homomorphism by Milne Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}: a regular map between abelian varieties sending the identity to the identity is automatically a homomorphism). Uniqueness: any two such homomorphisms agree on \(f^P(C)\) and therefore on the sumset \(f^P(C) + \dots + f^P(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which fills \(J\) because \(f^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to J\) is surjective (this is the dimension count \(\dim C^{(g)} = g = \dim J\) together with properness of \(C^{(g)}\)). The companion statement Proposition~6.4 for \(\varphi \colon C \times C \to A\) vanishing on the diagonal \(\Delta\) reduces to Proposition~6.1 via the additive decomposition Corollary~1.5 (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}): the additivity decomposition \(\varphi = \varphi_1 \circ p_1 + \varphi_2 \circ p_2\) with \(\varphi_i \colon C \to A\), \(\varphi_i(P) = 0\), combined with \(\varphi|_\Delta = 0\), forces \(\varphi_1 = -\varphi_2\), after which Proposition~6.1 supplies the unique \(\psi\).
557	  \medskip
558	  \emph{Dependency audit (iter-172, Outcome (b)).} The chain of dependencies of this proof, classified by their formalisation status in the project's tree, is:
559	  \begin{itemize}
560	    \item \emph{In-tree, axiom-clean (no new work):} Corollary~1.5 (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.hom\_additive\_decomp\_of\_rigidity}); Corollary~1.2 (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.av\_regularMap\_isHom\_of\_zero}); the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) underlying both.
561	    \item \emph{Reserved Lean target, body not yet closed --- Mathlib gap on the codimension-\(1\) step:} Milne Theorem~3.2 (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}, \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}). 568	\subsection{The positive-genus arm of the witness existence}
569	\label{sec:positiveGenusWitness}
570	The positive-genus sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is packaged separately from the genus-\(0\) arm so that the two arms can be closed independently. Whereas the genus-\(0\) arm \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} carries the trivial object \(\Spec k\) and routes through the (representability-free) characteristic-free rigidity keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of the upstream \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (with the over-\(\bar k\) \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} retained only as the fallback-(a) artifact), the positive-genus arm requires the full Albanese construction via Route A (Picard scheme via FGA --- Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability) of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}. Route A is \textbf{mandatory and unconditional} for the positive-genus witness object: that object must be a genuine \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety (\(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)), and this requirement is \emph{independent of the genus-\(0\) route choice} --- the two arms are fully decoupled. It is the committed M3 route per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3. Route B (symmetric powers + Stein factorisation) is a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
571	\begin{theorem}\leanok
572	[Positive-genus Albanese witness]
573	  \label{def:positiveGenusWitness}
574	  
575	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}
576	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Jac (the Jacobian: \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\) of a smooth projective family of genus-\(g>0\) curves exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
577	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume \(X/S\) is projective and smooth, its geometric
578	  % fibers are connected curves of genus \(g>0\), and \(S\) is Noetherian.  Set
579	  % \(J:=\IPicz_{X/S}\); it exists and is a projective Abelian \(S\)-scheme by
580	  % Exercise~REF.  Set \(\wh J:=\IPicz_{J/S}\); it exists, is a
581	  % projective Abelian scheme, and is ``dual'' to \(J\) by
582	  % Remark~REF."
583	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Jacobian, rmk:Jac).}
584	  Let \(C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) with \(\genus(C) \ge 1\). Then there exists a \(\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C\) whose underlying scheme is the Albanese variety of \(C\) (classically the identity component of the Picard scheme \(\mathrm{Pic}^0_{C/k}\), or equivalently the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism on \(\mathrm{Sym}^g(C)\)). The full \(\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\) data (group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension \(g\), properness, geometric irreducibility, and Albanese universal property uniformly over \(k\)-rational marked points) is supplied by the Route A construction (per \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route A; Route B is documented as a historical alternative not pursued).
585	\end{theorem}
586	\begin{proof}
587	  This is the M3 arm of the witness existence. M3 is \textbf{committed to Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA} (per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition; per-sub-phase budget recorded in the \emph{Route A --- per-sub-phase LOC and iter budget} paragraph above, \(\sim 6000\)--\(7500{+}\) LOC total, \(\sim 48\)--\(78\) iters after the iter-172 audit re-estimate of A.4 --- the bypass via Milne autoduality FAILS, so A.4 inherits the Theorem~3.2 + Lemma~3.3 + Auslander--Buchsbaum sub-build at \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC / \(\sim 22\)--\(35\) iters; the earlier iter-170 refresh estimate \(\sim 4400\)--\(6200\) LOC / \(\sim 33\)--\(54\) iters under-counted A.4, and the iter-123 audit \texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md} midpoint was \(\sim 6500\) LOC; the audit-corrected budget breaks out the two dominant blocks as (A.2) at \(\sim 2200\)--\(3000\) LOC and (A.4) at \(\sim 2500{+}\) LOC). The autonomous loop writes this Mathlib material directly in-tree; upstream PR extraction is an OPTIONAL downstream lift, not a project deliverable. The committed route produces the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} in positive genus via the following Mathlib-prerequisite path:
588	  \begin{itemize}
589	    \item \emph{Route A: Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability of \(\Pic_{C/k}\).} See \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route A for the four-step decomposition (A.1 relative Picard functor; A.2 representability; A.3 identity component; A.4 Abel--Jacobi universal morphism). The smallest entry point into Route A is the \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor (A.1 prerequisite; \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC) per the iter-123 audit.
590	  \end{itemize}
591	  Route B (symmetric powers \(C^{(g)} := C^g/S_g\) + Stein factorisation, \(\sim 9000\) LOC midpoint per the iter-123 audit) is documented in \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route B as a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
592	  M3 sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window; whether to start Route A scaffolding (smallest entry: \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor at \(\sim 700\)--\(1100\) LOC) in parallel with M2 or strictly after M2 closes is a planner-level scheduling call. Existing iter-150 mathlib-analogist scheduling trigger: dispatched if cumulative M2.body-pile LOC exceeds \(925\) LOC without piece (i.b) closing, OR if M2.a body closure has not landed by iter-160.
593	  The scaffold \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} (landed iter-134) is load-bearing iter-135 onward for the genus-stratified body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (iter-135 refactor): the body case-splits \(\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0\) and delegates to \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} on the \(\neg(\genus\,C = 0)\) arm via \texttt{Nat.pos\_of\_ne\_zero}. The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is contingent on Route A of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} being prosecuted in full.
594	\end{proof}
595	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
596	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
597	\begin{enumerate}
598	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of \(C\) is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~REF, carrying the four properties of Theorems~REF--REF (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-\(0\) case is included: the witness object is the trivial \(\Spec k\), and the witness's universal property carries only the rigidity \emph{statement} for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-\(0\) curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme --- \textbf{decoupled} from the Picard representability of Route~A. This rigidity is established over the algebraic closure \(\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k\) via the characteristic-free keystone \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} of the upstream \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (carrying \([\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\), no \([\mathtt{CharZero}]\)), whose proof route is the Milne \S I.3 rigidity chain (cube-free) of that chapter (route~(c)); the over-\(\bar k\) \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} is the retained fallback-(a) artifact (the differential / Serre-duality route, shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)). The conclusion over a general base field \(k\) is then obtained by \emph{descending} the over-\(\bar k\) morphism equality along the faithfully-flat surjection \(\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k\) (whose closing epi-cancellation is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; the surrounding base change is a multi-iteration sub-build, see Sub-step~C.2.f). The genus-\(0\) arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object \(\Spec k\), the universal morphism in the \(C(k) \neq \emptyset\) branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,\(C\), and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{prop:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} over \(\bar k\) followed by descent to \(k\).
599	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~REF introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(C \to \Jac(C)\) and shows that it is universal among morphisms from \(C\) to abelian varieties.
600	\end{enumerate}

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of \(\Jac(C)\). It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let \(k\) be a field. Let \(X\) be a reduced geometrically irreducible \(k\)-scheme and let \(Y\) be a separated \(k\)-scheme. Let \(g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y\) be two morphisms of \(k\)-schemes, and let \(U \subseteq X\) be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where \(U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X\) is the canonical open immersion), then \(g_1 = g_2\) as morphisms of \(k\)-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where \(Y\) is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism \(\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}\) formed from the group law on \(Y\). The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be \(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\), which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side \([\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) to \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\).
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{\(Y \to \Spec k\) is separated.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]\) provides this directly (no projection from \(\mathtt{IsProper}\) needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form \(\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)\) that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{\(X\) is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis \([\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]\) over the field-spectrum base \(\Spec k\) (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields \(\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}\) via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion \(U.\iota\) is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis \([\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]\), the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality \(g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}\) from the agreement-on-\(U\) hypothesis. The promotion to \(g_1 = g_2\) in \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\) uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of \(X.\mathrm{left}\).
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion \(U.\iota\) from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to \(\mathrm{Over}\)-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on \(X\) and \(Y\) are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-\(\bar k\) argument (for \(f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}\) hitting the identity at a \(\bar k\)-point) to this theorem with \(X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) and \(Y := A_{\bar k}\). The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~REF (the Albanese property of \(\Jac(C)\)): given two morphisms \(g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A\) with \(g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P\), scheme-level agreement on the image of \(\alpha_P\) (a non-empty open after passing to \(C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)\)) forces \(g_1 = g_2\).
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of REF requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the \(\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)\)-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
