467	\subsection*{The main \(\chi\)-identity}
468	
469	\begin{theorem}
470	
471	
472	\leanok
473	[Euler-characteristic identity for \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)]
474	  \label{thm:euler_char_eq_deg_plus_one_minus_genus}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_eq_degree_plus_one_minus_genus}
476	  
477	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Theorem~1.3 (the reduction inside the
478	  % proof: ``Thus we have to show that for any \(D\), $\chi(\mathscr L(D))
479	  % = \deg D + 1 - g$'') (read from
480	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 312).
481	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Theorem 1.3 (Riemann--Roch). \emph{Let \(D\) be a
482	  % divisor on a curve \(X\) of genus \(g\). Then}
483	  % \(l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g\)."
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we have to show that for any \(D\),
485	  % \(\chi(\mathscr L(D)) = \deg D + 1 - g\),
486	  % where for any coherent sheaf \(\mathscr F\) on \(X\), \(\chi(\mathscr F)\)
487	  % is the Euler characteristic
488	  % \(\chi(\mathscr F) = \dim H^0(X, \mathscr F) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr F)\)."
489	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Theorem~1.3, p.~295.}
490	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
491	  \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\). Then
492	  \[
493	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g.
494	  \]
495	\end{theorem}
496	
497	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "First we consider the case \(D = 0\). Then our
498	% formula says
499	% \(\dim H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) - \dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = 1 - g\),
500	% because \(H^0(X, \mathscr O_X) = k\) for any projective variety
501	% (I, 3.4), and \(\dim H^1(X, \mathscr O_X) = g\) by (1.1).
502	% Next, let \(D\) be any divisor, and let \(P\) be any point. We will show
503	% that the formula is true for \(D\) if and only if it is true for
504	% \(D + P\). Since any divisor can be reached from \(0\) in a finite number
505	% of steps by adding or subtracting a point each time, this will show
506	% the result holds for all \(D\).
507	% We consider \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(X\). Its structure sheaf is
508	% the skyscraper sheaf \(k\) sitting at the point \(P\), which we denote by
509	% \(k(P)\), and its ideal sheaf is \(\mathscr L(-P)\) by (II, 6.18).
510	% Therefore we have an exact sequence
511	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(-P) \to \mathscr O_X \to k(P) \to 0\).
512	% Tensoring with \(\mathscr L(D + P)\) we get
513	% \(0 \to \mathscr L(D) \to \mathscr L(D + P) \to k(P) \to 0\),
514	% (since \(\mathscr L(P)\) is locally free of rank 1, tensoring by it
515	% does not affect the sheaf \(k(P)\)). Now the Euler characteristic is
516	% additive on short exact sequences (III, Ex. 5.1), and $\chi(k(P))
517	% = 1$, so we have
518	% \(\chi(\mathscr L(D + P)) = \chi(\mathscr L(D)) + 1\).
519	% On the other hand, \(\deg D + P = \deg D + 1\), so our formula is true
520	% for \(D\) if and only if it is true for \(D + P\), as required."
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \leanok
523	  \uses{def:eulerChar_curve, def:l_invariant,
524	    lem:euler_char_shortExact_add, lem:euler_char_iso,
525	    lem:euler_char_skyscraperSheaf}
526	
527	  The proof is by induction on the structure of \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as the free
528	  abelian group on closed points (REF,
529	  REF). Both sides of the asserted
530	  equation are additive in \(D\) (the right-hand side by linearity of
531	  \(\deg\), REF; the left-hand side by the
532	  inductive step proved below), so it suffices to verify the formula
533	  for \(D = 0\) and to show that it transports across the elementary
534	  modification \(D \mapsto D + [P]\) at any closed point \(P \in C\).
535	
536	  \emph{Base case \(D = 0\).} The line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(0)\) is the
537	  structure sheaf \(\mathcal O_C\), and the formula
538	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C) = 1 - g\) reads
539	  \[
540	    \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
541	    - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)
542	    \;=\; 1 - g.
543	  \]
544	  The second term is the definition of the genus,
545	  \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = g\) (REF). The
546	  first term is \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = 1\): \(C\) is a
547	  proper geometrically irreducible curve over the algebraically
548	  closed field \(\bar k\), so its connected components agree with its
549	  geometrically connected components, and $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C)
550	  \cong \bar k\( as \)\bar k$-vector spaces (the Hartshorne~I.3.4
551	  statement that ``\(H^0(X, \mathcal O_X) = k\) for any projective
552	  variety'' over an algebraically closed field; the Mathlib home is
553	  the project's \(H^0\)-bridge in
554	  \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK.tex}). Substituting these
555	  two values gives \(1 - g = 1 - g\), as required.
556	
557	  \emph{Inductive step (Hartshorne's ``\(D \leftrightsquigarrow D + P\)''
558	  step).} Let \(P \in C\) be a closed point. We follow Hartshorne's
559	  argument: view \(P\) as a closed subscheme of \(C\); its structure
560	  sheaf is the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P) \cong \bar k\) supported at \(P\),
561	  and its sheaf of ideals is \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\)
562	  (the locally principal ideal sheaf cutting out \(P\) in \(C\)). Hence
563	  the short exact sequence
564	  \[
565	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(-[P]) \to \mathcal O_C \to k(P) \to 0
566	  \]
567	  in \(\mathbf{Coh}(C)\) (Hartshorne~II.6.18). Tensoring with the
568	  invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) (a locally free
569	  \(\mathcal O_C\)-module of rank \(1\), so tensoring preserves exactness
570	  and leaves the skyscraper sheaf \(k(P)\) unchanged) yields
571	  \[
572	    0 \to \mathcal O_C(D)
573	    \to \mathcal O_C(D + [P])
574	    \to k(P) \to 0.
575	  \]
576	  (Strictly: tensoring \(\mathcal O_C(-[P]) \subset \mathcal O_C\) with
577	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) produces a sheaf isomorphic — but not
578	  syntactically equal — to \(\mathcal O_C(D)\), and the skyscraper
579	  factor \(k(P)\) is preserved up to isomorphism because
580	  \(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])\) is locally free of rank \(1\). We transport
581	  along this isomorphism via iso-invariance of \(\chi\),
582	  REF.)
583	  Additivity of \(\chi\) on the short exact sequence
584	  (REF, the project-side
585	  factoring of the ``Now the Euler characteristic is additive on short
586	  exact sequences (III, Ex.~5.1)'' input of Hartshorne~IV.1.3) gives
587	  \[
588	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P]))
589	    \;=\;
590	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + \chi(k(P)).
591	  \]
592	  The skyscraper-sheaf input
593	  \(\chi(k(P)) = 1\) is the standalone substrate lemma
594	  REF (factored from the
595	  same Hartshorne~IV.1.3 line); its proof splits into the
596	  H\(^0\) identification \(\dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, k(P)) = 1\) and the
597	  H\(^1\) vanishing \(\dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, k(P)) = 0\), pursued
598	  independently by the prover (see the lemma's two-paragraph proof
599	  body). Therefore
600	  \[
601	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D + [P])) \;=\; \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) + 1.
602	  \]
603	  On the other hand, \(\deg([P]) = 1\) (every closed point contributes
604	  \(1\) to the bare-sum degree of REF), so by
605	  REF
606	  \[
607	    \deg(D + [P]) + 1 - g \;=\; \deg(D) + 1 - g + 1.
608	  \]
609	  Comparing the two displayed identities, the formula
610	  \(\chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) holds for \(D\) if and only
611	  if it holds for \(D + [P]\). Since the closed-point divisors \([P]\)
612	  generate \(\mathrm{Div}(C)\) as a free abelian group, the formula propagates
613	  from \(D = 0\) to every \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) by induction on the (signed)
614	  number of generators in the expression of \(D\).
615	
616	  \emph{Lean reference note on the three substrate lemmas.} The
617	  inductive step of this proof consumes three substantive substrate
618	  lemmas, each pinned to its own Lean declaration in
619	  \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean}: the additivity
620	  REF (\(\chi\) on a short
621	  exact sequence, gated on the project-side
622	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured Ext-LES carrier + Grothendieck
623	  vanishing + six-term rank-counting), the iso-invariance
624	  REF (\(\chi\) on
625	  isomorphism classes, axiom-clean against Mathlib's
626	  \(\Ext\)-postcomposition API; closed iter-186), and the skyscraper
627	  identity REF
628	  (\(\chi(k(P)) = 1\), splitting into an H\(^0\) half closed against
629	  the project's existing \(H^0\)-bridge and an H\(^1\) half gated on
630	  Mathlib flasque-cohomology — see the lemma's annotated proof body).
631	  Mathlib \texttt{b80f227} provides the Euler-characteristic API
632	  \texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.EulerCharacteristic}
633	  (\texttt{HomologicalComplex.eulerChar},

635	  specific additivity statement

637	  \(\Module \bar k\)-flavoured sheaf-cohomology level the project
638	  consumes; the additivity lemma is the project-side bridge.
639	\end{proof}

641	\section{The Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero}
642	
643	\begin{theorem}
644	
645	
646	\leanok
647	[Riemann--Roch formula in genus zero]
648	  \label{thm:riemannRoch_genus_zero}
649	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.l_eq_degree_plus_one_of_genus_zero}
650	  
651	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], IV.1, Example~1.3.5 (genus-\(0\) specialisation
652	  % of Riemann--Roch; the curve-is-rational consequence) (read from
653	  % references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf, PDF page 314).
654	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Example 1.3.5. Recall that a curve is \emph{rational}
655	  % if it is birational to \(\mathbf P^1\) (I, Ex. 6.1). Since curves in
656	  % this chapter are complete and nonsingular by definition, a curve
657	  % \(X\) is rational if and only if \(X \cong \mathbf P^1\) (I, 6.12). Now
658	  % using (1.3) we can show that \(X\) is rational if and only if \(g = 0\).
659	  % We already know that \(p_a(\mathbf P^1) = 0\) (I, Ex. 7.2), so
660	  % conversely suppose given a curve \(X\) of genus 0. Let \(P, Q\) be two
661	  % distinct points of \(X\) and apply Riemann--Roch to the divisor
662	  % \(D = P - Q\). Since \(\deg K - D = -2\), using (1.3.3) above, we have
663	  % \(l(K - D) = 0\), and so we find \(l(D) = 1\). But \(D\) is a divisor of
664	  % degree 0, so by (1.2) we have \(D \sim 0\), in other words $P \sim
665	  % Q\(. But this implies \)X$ is rational (II, 6.10.1)."
666	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, IV.1, Example~1.3.5, p.~297.}
667	  Let \(C\) be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over
668	  \(\bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), and let \(D \in \mathrm{Div}(C)\) with
669	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\). Then
670	  \[
671	    \ell(D) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
672	  \]
673	\end{theorem}
674	
675	\begin{proof}
676	  \leanok
677	  
678	  Specialising
679	  REF to the genus-\(0\)
680	  hypothesis \(g = 0\) yields
681	  \[
682	    \chi(\mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
683	  \]
684	  Unfolding \(\chi\) via REF and the definition
685	  \(\ell(D) = \dim_{\bar k} H^0(C, \mathcal O_C(D))\)
686	  (REF), this rewrites as
687	  \[
688	    \ell(D) - \dim_{\bar k} H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D))
689	    \;=\; \deg(D) + 1.
690	  \]
691	  Under the standing genus-\(0\) hypothesis and the assumption
692	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\), the higher-cohomology term vanishes:
693	  \[
694	    H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) \;=\; 0
695	    \qquad \text{for } \deg(D) \geq 0.
696	  \]
697	  Substituting yields \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\), as required.
698	
699	  \emph{The \(H^1\)-vanishing input.} On a smooth proper geometrically
700	  irreducible curve \(C / \bar k\) with \(g(C) = 0\), the vanishing
701	  \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) is the Hartshorne
702	  formulation Lemma~1.2 read covariantly: \(\ell(D) \neq 0\) forces
703	  \(\deg(D) \geq 0\); equivalently, for a curve where every
704	  degree-\(0\)-or-higher invertible sheaf is generated by global
705	  sections after a single point-twist, \(H^1\) of the twist must vanish
706	  by the long exact sequence of the skyscraper extension at any
707	  closed point. In the chain of sub-build chapters, this \(H^1\)
708	  vanishing is the natural content of \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
709	  \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added), where the line
710	  bundle \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) and its cohomology are constructed; once
711	  \texttt{RR.3} is closed, the present theorem inherits the
712	  vanishing as an instance, and the proof closes verbatim as
713	  displayed. Pending \texttt{RR.3}, the Lean signature
714	  \texttt{l\_eq\_degree\_plus\_one\_of\_genus\_zero} threads the
715	  \(H^1\)-vanishing hypothesis explicitly as a named premise; the
716	  iteration that closes \texttt{RR.3} then drops the premise in a
717	  follow-up signature simplification.
718	
719	  \emph{Sub-build note for \texttt{RR.4}.} The downstream consumer of
720	  this theorem is the genus-\(0\) classification
721	  REF of
722	  \texttt{AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex}: with \(\ell(D) = \deg(D) + 1\)
723	  and the degree-zero principal-divisor identity
724	  (REF), Hartshorne's Example~1.3.5 chain
725	  produces a degree-\(1\) morphism \(C \to \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}\) via
726	  \texttt{Proj.fromOfGlobalSections} applied to the two-dimensional
727	  \(H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P]))\), which is then upgraded to an
728	  isomorphism in \texttt{RR.4}.
729	\end{proof}
730	
731	\section{Out of scope}
732	
733	The following items are deliberately \emph{not} part of \texttt{RR.2}
734	and are sequenced into the sibling sub-build chapters:
735	
736	\begin{itemize}
737	  \item The general-\(g\) Riemann--Roch theorem
738	    \(\ell(D) - \ell(K - D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) (Hartshorne~IV.1.3 in
739	    its full form). This requires the canonical divisor \(K\), the
740	    canonical sheaf \(\Omega^1_{C / \bar k} = \omega_C\), and
741	    Serre duality $H^1(C, \mathcal L) \cong H^0(C, \omega_C \otimes
742	    \mathcal L^{-1})^*$. Mathlib has none of these for schemes, and
743	    the genus-\(0\) critical path does not require them; the general
744	    statement is queued for a follow-up chapter (post-RR-bridge).
745	  \item The construction of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O_C(D)\)
746	    from a Weil divisor \(D\), the linear-equivalence isomorphism
747	    \(\mathcal O_C(D) \cong \mathcal O_C(D')\) when \(D \sim D'\), and
748	    the computation of $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C([P])) \cong \bar k \cdot
749	    1 \oplus \bar k \cdot t$ (\emph{a non-constant rational function
750	    \(t\) exists}) live in \texttt{RR.3} (sibling chapter
751	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_OcOfD.tex}, to be added). Inside the proof
752	    of REF the dependency on
753	    \(\mathcal O_C(D)\) is reduced to its \(H^0\) functor and the short
754	    exact sequence of the closed point.
755	  \item The vanishing \(H^1(C, \mathcal O_C(D)) = 0\) for
756	    \(\deg(D) \geq 0\) on a genus-\(0\) curve is consumed as a named
757	    premise by REF; its closure is
758	    part of \texttt{RR.3} (where the cohomology of
759	    \(\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(d)\) is computed) and is recovered for
760	    a general smooth proper genus-\(0\) curve via the isomorphism
761	    \(C \cong \mathbb P^1\) of \texttt{RR.4} or directly via the
762	    base-change argument inside \texttt{RR.3}.
763	  \item The classification ``a smooth proper geometrically irreducible
764	    curve over \(\bar k\) with \(g = 0\) is isomorphic to
765	    \(\mathbb P^1\)'' (Hartshorne Example~IV.1.3.5 proper) is the
766	    headline of \texttt{RR.4} (sibling chapter
767	    \texttt{RiemannRoch\_RationalIsoP1.tex}, to be added). The
768	    present chapter supplies one of its two inputs (the $\ell(D) =
769	    \deg(D) + 1\( identity); the other input is the degree-\)0$
770	    principal-divisor identity REF from
771	    \texttt{RR.1}.
772	  \item Higher-cohomology bookkeeping (\(\chi\) as an alternating sum
773	    over all \(i \geq 0\), the Grothendieck vanishing
774	    \(H^i(C, \mathcal F) = 0\) for \(i > \dim C = 1\)) is not separately
775	    formalised here; the chapter consumes only the two-term form
776	    \(\chi(\mathcal F) = \dim H^0 - \dim H^1\) which is the standing
777	    curve specialisation.
778	\end{itemize}

7	% relative-spectrum functor \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) : \mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)^{op}
8	% \to \mathrm{Sch}/X used by the relative Picard functor on a product C \times_k T.
9	% Mathlib has the absolute Spec functor and affine gluing (Scheme.GlueData,

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60	
61	% NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-174 carrier note. The iter-179
62	% Block A refactor (analogies/relative-spec-encoding.md) upgrades the carrier
63	% to a 3-field structure
64	%     `structure QcohAlgebra (X : Scheme.{u}) where`
65	%     `  sheaf        : TopCat.Sheaf CommRingCat.{u} X.toPresheafedSpace`
66	%     `  unit         : X.sheaf ⟶ sheaf`
67	%     `  coequifibered : NatTrans.Coequifibered`
68	%     `    (Functor.whiskerLeft (AffineZariskiSite.toOpensFunctor X).op unit.hom)`
69	% The new third field IS the iter-175+ overlay the prior note flagged: Mathlib
70	% commit `b80f227`'s Stacks-01LL `NatTrans.Coequifibered` predicate is strictly
71	% weaker than the full `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` claimed by Definition
72	% 1.1's prose but is equivalent for the geometric purpose (constructing the
73	% relative-spectrum scheme) via the dense-subsite equivalence
74	% `AffineZariskiSite.sheafEquiv`. This is the predicate Mathlib's
75	% `AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData` consumes, so the iter-179
76	% `RelativeSpec`/`structureMorphism` bodies (now real Mathlib values) close
77	% against it.

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}

159	\begin{theorem}\leanok
160	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
161	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
162	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
163	  
164	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
165	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than

170	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
171	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).

176	  %  \(f\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)
177	  %  is a morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras. Note that this is the
178	  %  same as giving a \(f^{-1}\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra homomorphism
179	  %  \(\varphi : f^{-1}\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\), see
180	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
181	  %  This is also the same as giving an \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra map
182	  %  \(\varphi : \mathcal{A} \to f_*\mathcal{O}_T\), see
183	  %  Sheaves, Lemma REF.
184	  %  We will use all three ways of thinking about \(\varphi\),
185	  %  without further mention. [...]
186	  %  In this way we have defined a functor
187	  %  \begin{eqnarray}
188	  %  \label{equation-spec}
189	  %  F : \Sch^{opp} & \longrightarrow & \textit{Sets} \\
190	  %  T & \longmapsto & F(T) = \{\text{pairs }(f, \varphi) \text{ as above}\}
191	  %  \nonumber
192	  %  \end{eqnarray}
193	  %  [...]
194	  %  The functor \(F\) is representable by a scheme."
195	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-spec + section-spec
196	  functor description); [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(b).}
197	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra.
198	  Then \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) represents the functor
199	  \(F : \Sch^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\),
200	  $T \mapsto F(T) := \{(f, \varphi) : f : T \to X \text{ a morphism},\;
201	  \varphi : f^* \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T \text{ an } \mathcal{O}_T\text{-algebra map}\}.$
202	  Equivalently, for any \(X\)-scheme \(g : T \to X\),
203	  there is a natural bijection
204	  \[
205	    \Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \;\;\cong\;\;
206	    \Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_* \mathcal{O}_T)
207	  \]
208	  (using the adjunction between \(g_*\) and \(g^*\) to convert between
209	  \(\mathcal{A} \to g_*\mathcal{O}_T\) and \(g^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)).
210	  The bijection is natural in \(T\).
211	\end{theorem}

221	  \leanok
222	  % NOTE (iter-179 review): supersedes the iter-176 placeholder-discharge note.
223	  % The iter-179 Block A refactor replaces the iter-176 placeholder bodies with
224	  % the Mathlib values ``(AffineZariskiSite.relativeGluingData _).glued`` /
225	  % ``.toBase``; iter-179 Lane B closes the Lean ``UniversalProperty`` proof
226	  % via ``isAffineHom_of_forall_exists_isAffineOpen`` +

230	  % strictly weaker ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than the
231	  % Yoneda-bijection form pinned above (iter-174+ commitment; still pending
232	  % iter-180+). Consumers may now rely on the affineness conclusion, but the
233	  % Yoneda-bijection witness is still deferred.
234	  Following Stacks tag 01LQ (proof of lemma-spec): the functor \(F\) is a Zariski sheaf
235	  because morphisms of schemes and \(\mathcal{O}\)-algebra maps both glue under open covers.
236	  Choose an affine open cover \(X = \bigcup U_i\). The subfunctor
237	  \(F_i \subseteq F\) of pairs \((f, \varphi)\) with \(f(T) \subseteq U_i\) is representable by
238	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U_i))\) via the affine-base case
239	  (Theorem~REF) together with the
240	  base-change identification \(F_i \cong F_{U_i,\,\mathcal{A}|_{U_i}}\)
241	  (Theorem~REF). Each \(F_i \hookrightarrow F\) is an open
242	  subfunctor (pull back \(U_i\) along \(f\)). The \(F_i\) jointly cover \(F\) since the \(U_i\)

248	\section{Affine base}

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}

79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	\begin{lemma}\leanok
191	[Naturality respects the group structure]
192	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
194	  
195	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
196	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
197	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
198	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
199	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
200	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
201	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
202	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
203	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
204	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
205	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
206	  %  [\dots]
207	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
208	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
209	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
210	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
211	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
212	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
213	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
214	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
215	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
216	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
217	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
218	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
219	  homomorphisms.
220	\end{lemma}
221	
222	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
223	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
224	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
225	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
226	\begin{proof}
227	  
228	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
229	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
230	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
231	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
232	  structure sheaf, hence the map
233	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
234	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
235	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
236	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
237	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
238	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
239	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
240	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
241	\end{proof}
242	
243	\begin{theorem}\leanok
244	[\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is a group-valued presheaf]
245	  \label{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}
246	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
247	  
248	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
249	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (relative Picard functor as a functor into
250	  % abelian groups)
251	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
252	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
253	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
254	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
255	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
256	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
257	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
258	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.}
259	  The assignment \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_sharp},
260	  together with the structure maps \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) of
261	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, defines a presheaf of abelian groups
262	  \[
263	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\;
264	                                     \AddCommGroup
265	  \]
266	  on the category of \(k\)-schemes.
267	\end{theorem}
268	
269	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with the prior theorems, Kleiman \S 2 does not supply
270	% a separate proof body --- the presheaf-of-abelian-groups property is part of
271	% the definition df:aPf + df:Pfs. The project-side proof assembles the lemmas
272	% above; no external proof is being translated.
273	\begin{proof}
274	  
275	  The object map is well defined by
276	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
277	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
278	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by
279	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}, and satisfies the identity and
280	  composition laws by the same lemma. These data assemble into a functor
281	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\), i.e.\ a presheaf of abelian groups on
282	  \((\Sch/k)\).
283	\end{proof}

285	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
286	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
287	
288	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
289	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
290	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
291	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
292	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
293	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
294	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
295	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
296	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
297	
298	\begin{definition}\leanok
299	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
300	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
301	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
302	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
303	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
304	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
305	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
306	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
307	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
308	  % below is also corrected.
309	  
310	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
311	  % Definition df:Pfs --- the second half of df:Pfs names the \'etale sheaf
312	  % associated to the relative Picard functor
313	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318
314	  %  and the surrounding paragraph L1320-L1328).
315	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
316	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
317	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
318	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
319	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
320	  % topologies  by
321	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
322	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
323	  %  \end{dfn}
324	  %  [\dots]
325	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
326	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
327	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
328	  %  the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
329	  %  check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
330	  %  associated to the absolute Picard functor \(\Pic_X\), as well as to the
331	  %  relative Picard functor \(\Pic_{X/S}\)."
332	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
333	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
334	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
335	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
336	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
337	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
338	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):
339	  \[
340	    \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \;\;:=\;\;
341	       \bigl(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\bigr)^{\sim_{\et}}.
342	  \]
343	  Equivalently, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is the unique (up to canonical
344	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)
345	  equipped with a presheaf morphism $\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to
346	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
347	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
348	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
349	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
350	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
351	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
352	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
353	\end{definition}
354	
355	\begin{theorem}[\'Etale sheafification preserves the abelian-group structure]
356	  \label{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure}
357	  
358	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
359	  % Def.~df:Pfs --- the \'etale-sheafified functor \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named
360	  % alongside the abelian-group-valued \(\Pic_{X/S}\) in the same definition,
361	  % i.e.\ the sheafification lives in the same target category
362	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
363	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
364	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
365	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
366	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
367	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
368	  % topologies  by
369	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
370	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
371	  %  \end{dfn}"
372	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
373	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) is named together with the
374	  abelian-group-valued relative Picard functor, meaning the sheaf is taken in
375	  the category of abelian groups).}
376	  The \'etale sheafification
377	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification} is a
378	  sheaf with values in the category of abelian groups: the canonical morphism
379	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
380	  presheaves, and for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\) the value
381	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is naturally an abelian group whose structure
382	  maps make \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) a presheaf
383	  \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\).
384	\end{theorem}
385	
386	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a self-contained proof of
387	% this auxiliary statement --- it is the standard fact that the sheafification
388	% of a presheaf of abelian groups in a Grothendieck topology is again a
389	% presheaf of abelian groups (equivalently, the forgetful functor
390	% \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves filtered colimits, and the
391	% plus-construction is built from such colimits). The Lean side appeals to
392	% Mathlib's \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) machinery.
393	\begin{proof}
394	  
395	  The \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\) is a Grothendieck topology, and the
396	  forgetful functor \(\AddCommGroup \to \Set\) preserves and reflects the
397	  filtered colimits used by the plus-construction. Therefore the
398	  sheafification of a presheaf \(\mathcal{F} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\)
399	  in the underlying \'etale site agrees, after the forgetful functor to sets,
400	  with the sheafification of the underlying set-valued presheaf
401	  \(U \circ \mathcal{F}\); equivalently, the sheaf
402	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} = (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^{\sim_\et}\) is canonically a
403	  sheaf of abelian groups, the canonical morphism
404	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is a homomorphism of group
405	  presheaves, and the restriction maps are group homomorphisms. This is a
406	  standard consequence of the plus-construction with values in an algebraic
407	  category; on the Lean side it is the Mathlib pattern
408	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}\) applied with target category
409	  \(\AddCommGroup\).
410	\end{proof}

412	\section{Lean encoding}
413	\label{sec:relpic_lean_encoding}
414	
415	The Lean target file is the new
416	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelPicFunctor.lean}, sibling to
417	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}
418	(\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}) and
419	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
420	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
421	Lean targets as follows.
422	\begin{itemize}
423	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
424	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}) is the abelian-group instance on the
425	    quotient set built in
426	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
427	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). Mathlib's
428	    \texttt{QuotientAddGroup} machinery on a normal subgroup of an abelian
429	    group is the backbone; the project-side work is to certify that
430	    \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism (one extra
431	    \texttt{LinearMap.map\_mul}--style line on tensor product preservation).
432	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
433	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}) is the group-valued presheaf
434	    \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\). The Lean form is a functor
435	    \((\Sch/k)^{op} \to \AddCommGroup\) built from the data of
436	    \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} +
437	    \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
438	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
439	    (\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}) packages the structure-map
440	    homomorphism property: the set map \(g^\sharp\) of
441	    \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a group homomorphism. On the Lean side
442	    this is a one-step strengthening of the existing set-valued naturality
443	    proof.
444	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.presheaf}
445	    (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) wraps the previous three lemmas as a
446	    functor instance into \(\AddCommGroup\).
447	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
448	    % NOTE (iter-176 review): renamed from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme``
449	    % to avoid a collision with the representing-scheme declaration of the same
450	    % name in ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability``.
451	    (\cref{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}) is the \'etale sheafification
452	    of \texttt{PicSharp}. The Lean form is
453	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrothendieckTopology.Sheafify} applied to the
454	    \'etale topology on \(\Sch/k\) with target category \(\AddCommGroup\).
455	    Mathlib provides the abelian-group-valued sheafification API
456	    (\texttt{CategoryTheory.Sheafification}) for any concrete sheafifiable
457	    presheaf and any target with the required filtered-colimit + finite-limit
458	    structure; \cref{thm:rel_pic_etale_sheaf_group_structure} reduces the
459	    statement to that pattern. \textbf{Verification flag.} The exact Mathlib
460	    declaration name and the input-shape of the \'etale-Grothendieck-topology
461	    instance on \(\Sch/k\) are not pinned in this chapter; the prover should
462	    consult the current Mathlib snapshot (or open a follow-up if the API is
463	    not yet wired to \(\AddCommGroup\)). If the abelian-group-valued
464	    sheafification is absent from Mathlib at the pinned commit, the
465	    project-side sub-build is to combine the set-valued sheafification with
466	    Mathlib's \texttt{AddCommGroup} forgetful functor.
467	\end{itemize}
468	
469	The relationship with \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability} is captured by
470	the slogan: the FGA Picard scheme of A.2.c represents
471	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (Kleiman \S 4, th:main: ``\dots represents
472	\(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)''), \emph{not} \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) directly. The \'etale
473	sheafification step of this chapter is precisely what makes the
474	representability theorem in A.2.c well-posed.
475

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the
7	% join of the two preceding sub-rows: A.4.a, the codim-1 indeterminacy /
8	% codim-\(\geq 2\) extension package (\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}),
9	% and A.4.b, the Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary
10	% ``regular local \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay''
11	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}). The single output of this
12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean
24	% name \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap\_to\_av\_extends}); a Plan-Agent
25	% reconciliation pass should retarget that reservation at the theorem proved
26	% here (see "Notes for Plan Agent" in the writer report).
27	%
28	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean}
29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  
54	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
55	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
56	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
57	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
58	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
59	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
60	
61	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
62	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
63	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
64	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
65	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
66	\end{theorem}
67	
68	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
69	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
70	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
71	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
72	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
75	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
76	\begin{proof}
77	  
78	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.
79	  Theorem~3.1 (the codim-\(\geq 2\) extension theorem for a rational map from a normal
80	  variety to a complete variety; valuative criterion of properness) is
81	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} of the present project; Lemma~3.3 (the
82	  pure-codimension-\(1\) structure of the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a
83	  group variety) is \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} of the same chapter.
84	
85	  Concretely: let \(U \subseteq X\) be a maximal open set of definition of \(f\), and let
86	  \(Z := X \setminus U\) be its complement.
87	  \begin{enumerate}
88	    \item By \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} (Milne~3.1, the valuative-criterion extension
89	      to codim \(\geq 2\)), applied with the normal variety \(X\) and the complete variety
90	      \(A\), we have \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
91	    \item By \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} (Milne~3.3, the pure-codim-\(1\)
92	      indeterminacy structure for maps into the group variety \(A\)), the closed subset
93	      \(Z\) is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\) in \(X\).
94	    \item Both conditions together force \(Z = \emptyset\): a non-empty pure-codimension-\(1\)
95	      closed subset has codimension exactly \(1\), contradicting \(\mathrm{codim}_X(Z) \geq 2\).
96	  \end{enumerate}
97	  Therefore \(U = X\) and \(f\) admits a regular representative on all of \(X\), which is the
98	  required regular morphism \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\). Uniqueness is the standard
99	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle (see the paragraph preceding the theorem).
100	\end{proof}
101	
102	\begin{remark}
103	  \label{rmk:thm32_role_of_ab}
104	  \textbf{Where Auslander--Buchsbaum enters.} The combination above relies on
105	  \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}, which is itself proved in
106	  \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} in two stages: a codim-\(1\) step
107	  (valuative criterion of properness, applied at the DVR
108	  \(\mathcal O_{X, Z}\) of each codimension-\(1\) point \(Z\) --- works because \(X\) is normal
109	  and \(A\) is complete) and a codim-\(\geq 2\) step (depth-\(\geq 2\) at codim-\(\geq 2\) points,
110	  giving the local-cohomology / Hartshorne~III.8 sheaf-extension property). The
111	  depth-\(\geq 2\) input on a smooth (regular) variety is exactly what
112	  \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} supplies:
113	  for a regular local ring \((\mathcal O_{X, x}, \mathfrak m_x)\) of Krull dimension \(d\), the
114	  Auslander--Buchsbaum formula \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} (applied to \(M = R\)
115	  trivially, or to the depth-via-regular-sequence characterisation directly) gives
116	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) = d\), so in particular
117	  \(\mathrm{depth}(\mathcal O_{X, x}) \geq 2\) whenever \(\mathrm{codim}_X(\{x\}) \geq 2\).
118	  Concretely, when \(X\) is a smooth surface (e.g.\ \(\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1\) or
119	  \(C \times C\) for a smooth projective curve \(C\)), every closed point \(x\) is regular of
120	  dimension \(2\), so \(\mathcal O_{X, x}\) is Cohen--Macaulay of depth \(2\); sections of
121	  \(\mathcal O_X\) on \(U = X \setminus \{x\}\) extend uniquely across \(x\), and the same is
122	  true for affine-locally pulled-back sections of \(\mathcal O_A\) along \(f\).
123	
124	  Auslander--Buchsbaum therefore does not appear by name in the proof above; it appears
125	  inside \cref{thm:codim_one_extension}'s codim-\(\geq 2\) half. The dependency
126	  \(\) is recorded on
127	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} to keep the leanblueprint dependency graph
128	  faithful: A.4.c truly does sit on top of A.4.b through A.4.a.
129	\end{remark}
130	
131	\section{Why the proof is char-free}
132	\label{sec:thm32_char_free}
133	
134	Each of the two inputs is char-free:
135	\begin{itemize}
136	  \item \emph{Theorem~3.1 / codim-\(\geq 2\) extension} (\cref{thm:codim_one_extension}):
137	    the proof uses only the valuative criterion of properness and depth-\(\geq 2\) extension
138	    of sections on regular local rings; both are characteristic-agnostic.
139	  \item \emph{Lemma~3.3 / pure-codim-\(1\) indeterminacy for maps into a group variety}
140	    (\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}): Milne's proof forms the difference map
141	    \(\Phi(x, y) := \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon X \times X \dashrightarrow A\) and
142	    examines its zero/pole divisor along the diagonal --- a Weil-divisor argument that
143	    works for any algebraically closed base field. The argument uses only the group law
144	    on \(A\) (its smoothness as a group object), the regularity of \(X\) (so divisors and
145	    DVRs at codim-\(1\) points are available), and the existence of a divisor of zeros and
146	    poles for a nonzero rational function on a nonsingular variety. None of these inputs
147	    has a characteristic restriction.
148	\end{itemize}
149	Consequently \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} holds over every algebraically closed
150	field, in arbitrary characteristic. (Milne states this proof verbatim in characteristic-free
151	generality at \S I.3, pp.~16--17.)
152	
153	\section{Application: the symmetric-product Albanese map (gating A.4.d)}
154	\label{sec:thm32_application_to_a4d}
155	
156	The downstream consumer is A.4.d (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}):
157	the Albanese universal property of the Jacobian \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\). Milne's proof of
158	Proposition~6.1 of \S III.6 (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~104) constructs the unique
159	morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) factoring a given pointed regular morphism
160	\(\varphi \colon C \to A\) through the canonical \(f_P \colon C \to J\) as follows. The
161	symmetric power \(C^{(g)}\) admits a regular morphism \(\varphi^{(g)} \colon C^{(g)} \to A\)
162	defined by
163	\[
164	  \varphi^{(g)}\bigl(P_1 + \dots + P_g\bigr) \;=\; \varphi(P_1) + \dots + \varphi(P_g),
165	\]
166	and a birational morphism \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow J\) (the Abel--Jacobi map). One then
167	seeks a morphism \(J \to A\) making the obvious diagram commute. The construction goes via
168	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) followed by descent to \(J\); the rational map
169	\(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) obtained at the intermediate step is exactly the kind that
170	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes to a regular morphism, because \(C^{(g)}\) is
171	a nonsingular variety (Milne~III.3) and \(A\) is an abelian variety. Without
172	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} this step has no replacement: Milne's autoduality
173	shortcut (\(J \cong J^\vee\)) routes through the theorem of the cube, which the project
174	excised at iter-163; see the analysis in \cref{sec:Jacobian_routeA4_albaneseUP} of
175	\cref{chap:Jacobian}.
176	
177	\section{Lean encoding}
178	\label{sec:thm32_lean_encoding}
179

38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
70	
71	\begin{lemma}\leanok
72	[Line bundle / Quot correspondence via the Abel map]
73	  \label{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}
74	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.abelMap}
75	  
76	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3, Def.~dfn:Abel (the Abel
77	  % map), and Thm.~th:repDiv (representability of relative effective
78	  % divisors by an open subscheme of \(\IHilb_{X/S}\)); arXiv:math/0504020,
79	  % pp.~25, 23 (read from
80	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1837-L1841 + L2135-L2145).
81	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
82	  % "\begin{thm}\label{th:repDiv}
83	  %   Assume \(X/S\) is projective and flat.  Then \(\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}\) is
84	  %  representable by an open subscheme \(\IDiv_{X/S}\) of the Hilbert scheme
85	  %  \(\IHilb_{X/S}\).
86	  %   \end{thm}"
87	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
88	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{dfn:Abel}
89	  %   The {\it Abel map\/} is the natural map of functors
90	  %      \(\)A_{X/S}(T)\:\mathrm{Div}_{X/S}(T)\to \Pic_{X/S}(T)\(\)
91	  %   defined by sending a relative effective divisor \(D\) on \(X_T/T\) to the
92	  %  sheaf \(\mc O_{\!X_T}(D)\).  The target \(\Pic_{X/S}\) may be replaced by any
93	  %  of its associated sheaves."
94	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 3,
95	  Thm.~th:repDiv + Def.~dfn:Abel (cf.\ [Nitsure], ``Construction of Hilbert
96	  and Quot Schemes'', \S 1, where \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve, and let
98	  \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme. There is a natural map of presheaves on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\)
99	  \[
100	    A_{C/k} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et},
101	    \qquad D \;\longmapsto\; [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)],
102	  \]
103	  where \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of relative effective Cartier divisors
104	  on \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), and the target is the
105	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of
106	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}. The functor \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) is representable
107	  by an open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \subseteq \Hilb_{C/k}\) of the Hilbert scheme
108	  of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (with \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) the special case
109	  \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\) of the Quot functor). The Abel map is therefore
110	  representable by a morphism of \(k\)-schemes $A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to
111	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
115	% L1843-L1866, proof of th:repDiv)
116	% "Set \(H:=\IHilb_{X/S}\), and let \(W\subset X\x H\) be the universal
117	% (closed) subscheme, and \(q\:W\to H\) the projection.  Let \(V\) denote the
118	% set of points \(w\in W\) at which \(W\) is an effective divisor. Plainly \(V\)
119	% is open in \(W\).  Set \(Z:=q(W-V)\).  Then \(Z\) is closed because \(q\) is
120	% proper.  Set \(U:=H-Z\).  Then \(U\) is open, and \(q^{-1}U\) is an effective
121	% divisor in \(X\x U\).  In fact, since \(q\) is flat, \(q^{-1}U\) is a relative
122	% effective divisor in \(X\x U/U\).
123	% [\dots]"
124	\begin{proof}
125	  
126	  By \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, the Hilbert functor $\Hilb_{C/k} =
127	  \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\( is representable by a \)k$-scheme; this uses
128	  \(C/k\) projective and flat (the smooth proper curve hypothesis is
129	  stronger). Let \(W \subseteq C \times_k \Hilb_{C/k}\) be the universal
130	  closed subscheme and \(q : W \to \Hilb_{C/k}\) the projection. The locus
131	  \(V \subseteq W\) where \(W\) is locally an effective Cartier divisor (i.e.\
132	  cut out by one regular element) is open. The complement \(W \setminus V\)
133	  is closed in \(W\), and the image $Z := q(W \setminus V) \subseteq
134	  \Hilb_{C/k}\( is closed because \)q\( is proper. Hence \)\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} :=
135	  \Hilb_{C/k} \setminus Z\( is open in \)\Hilb_{C/k}$ and represents the
136	  subfunctor of relative effective divisors: on \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) the
137	  restriction of \(W\) is flat and cut out by one regular element fiberwise,
138	  so by the standard relative-divisor criterion (Kleiman~\S 3
139	  Lem.~lm:ctn) it is a relative effective divisor on $C \times_k
140	  \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} / \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}$.
141	
142	  The Abel map sends a relative effective divisor \(D \subseteq C \times_k T\)
143	  to the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D) := \mathcal{I}_D^{-1}\)
144	  (the dual of the ideal sheaf, which is invertible by the divisor
145	  hypothesis). The assignment is functorial in \(T\): pullback of an ideal
146	  sheaf along \(\mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\)
147	  takes the ideal of \(D\) to the ideal of \(D_{T'}\) (since \(D\) is flat over
148	  \(T\), the schematic inverse image \(D_{T'}\) is a relative effective
149	  divisor on \(C \times_k T'/T'\), again by Kleiman~\S 3 Lem.~lm:ctn), hence
150	  takes \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T'}(D_{T'})\).
151	  Composing with the canonical projection
152	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) of
153	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} and then with the
154	  \'etale sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} yields the
155	  natural map \(A_{C/k}(T) : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
156	\end{proof}
