158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}
172	  
173	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main,
174	  % and Cor.~cor:algsch (specialisation to \(S = \Spec k\), \(X\) complete);
175	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~26, 35 (read from
176	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2155-L2166 + L2686-L2690).
177	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
178	  % "\begin{thm}[Main]\label{th:main}
179	  %   Assume \(f\:X\to S\) is projective Zariski locally over \(S\), and is flat
180	  %  with integral geometric fibers.
181	  %
182	  %  \tu{(1)} Then \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists, is separated and locally of finite
183	  %  type over \(S\), and represents \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\).
184	  %
185	  %  \tu{(2)} If also \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective, then
186	  %  \(\IPic_{X/S}\) is a disjoint union of open
187	  %   subschemes, each an increasing union of open quasi-projective
188	  %  \(S\)-schemes.
189	  %   \end{thm}"
190	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
191	  % "\begin{sbscor}\label{cor:algsch}
192	  %    Assume \(S\) is the spectrum of a field \(k\), and \(X\) is complete.  Then
193	  %  \(\IPic_{X/k}\) exists, represents \(\Pic_{(X/k)\fppf}\), and is a disjoint
194	  %  union of open quasi-projective subschemes.
195	  %   \end{sbscor}"
196	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Main Thm.~th:main
197	  + Cor.~cor:algsch.}
198	  Let \(k\) be a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
199	  over \(k\). Then the \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor
200	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} is
201	  representable by a \(k\)-scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\), separated and locally of
202	  finite type over \(k\), which is a disjoint union of open
203	  quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes, each an increasing union of open
204	  quasi-projective subschemes. The scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is called the
205	  \emph{Picard scheme} of \(C/k\).
206	\end{theorem}
207	
208	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
209	% L2168-L2366, proof of th:main; long and is broken into the four-step
210	% sketch below per the project's structural restatement convention. The
211	% verbatim quote of the opening reduction step is:)
212	% "By~\cite[(0, 4.5.5), p.~106]{EGAG}, it is a local matter on \(S\) to
213	% represent a Zariski sheaf on the category of \(S\)-schemes.  Moreover, it
214	% is also a local matter on \(S\) to prove that an \(S\)-scheme is separated
215	% and locally of finite type.  Hence, in order to prove (1), we may assume
216	% \(S\) is Noetherian and \(X/S\) is projective.
217	% [\dots]
218	% Plainly an \(S\)-scheme is separated if it is a disjoint union of
219	% separated open subschemes, or if it is an increasing union of
220	% separated open subschemes.  Hence (1) follows from (2)."
221	% (subsequent steps quoted inline in the project-notation proof below).
222	\begin{proof}
223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}
286	
287	\subsection{The smooth-proper quotient lemma}
288	\label{subsec:fga_pic_qt}
289	
290	The structural lemma underlying step 4 above is recorded separately
291	because it is consumed verbatim in the assembly proof: it is the
292	abstract content allowing the descent from \(Z = \mathrm{Div}^{\phi_0}_0\) to the
293	representing Picard piece.
294	
295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}
298	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt;
300	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~31 (read from
301	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2368-L2375).
302	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
303	  % "\begin{lem}\label{lm:qt}
304	  %   Let \(\alpha\:Z\to P\) be a map of \'etale sheaves, and set \(R:=Z\x_PZ\).
305	  %  Assume \(\alpha\) is a surjection, \(Z\) is representable by a
306	  %  quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, \(R\) is representable by an \(S\)-scheme, and
307	  %  the first projection \(R\to Z\) is representable by a smooth and proper
308	  %  map.  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme, and
309	  %  \(\alpha\) is representable by a smooth map."
310	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Lem.~lm:qt.}
311	  Let \(\alpha : Z \to P\) be a morphism of \'etale sheaves on \((\Sch/k)\).
312	  Set \(R := Z \times_P Z\). Suppose:
313	  \begin{enumerate}
314	    \item \(\alpha\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves;
315	    \item \(Z\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme;
316	    \item \(R\) is representable by a \(k\)-scheme;
317	    \item the first projection \(R \to Z\) is representable by a smooth
318	      and proper map.
319	  \end{enumerate}
320	  Then \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme, and \(\alpha\)
321	  is representable by a smooth map.
322	\end{lemma}
323	
324	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
325	% L2377-L2417, proof of lm:qt; reproduced verbatim because it is the
326	% structural heart of step 4.)
327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is
329	% quasi-projective, it is separated; whence, the first projection
330	% \(Z\x_SZ\to Z\) is too.  But the first projection \(R\to Z\) is proper, and
331	% factors naturally through a map \(h\:R\to Z\x_SZ\).  Hence \(h\) is proper.
332	% But \(h\) is a monomorphism; that is, \(h\) is injective on \(T\)-points.
333	% Therefore, \(h\) is a closed embedding by \cite[8.11.5]{EGAIV3}.
334	%
335	% Plainly, for each \(S\)-scheme \(T\), the subset $R(T)\subset
336	% Z(T)\x_{S(T)}Z(T)$ is the graph of an equivalence relation.  Also, the
337	% map of schemes \(R\to Z\) is flat and proper, and \(Z\) is a
338	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme.  It follows that there exist a
339	% quasi-projective \(S\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat and projective map
340	% \(Z\to Q\) such that \(R=Z\x_QZ\).  (In other words, a flat and proper
341	% equivalence relation on a quasi-projective scheme is effective.)
342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are
344	% smooth.  But these fibers are, up to extension of the ground field, the
345	% same as those of \(R\to Z\).  And \(R\to Z\) is smooth by hypothesis.  Thus
346	% \(Z\to Q\) is smooth.
347	%
348	% It remains to see that \(Q\) represents \(P\).  First, observe that \(Z\to Q\)
349	% is (represents) a surjection of \'etale sheaves.
350	% [\dots]
351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps
353	% \(R\rightrightarrows Z\) by Exercise~REF below, which is an
354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same
355	% exercise, \(P\) too is the coequalizer of this pair of maps.  But, in any
356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)
357	% represents \(P\)."
358	\begin{proof}
359	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
360	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
361	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
362	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
363	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
364	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
365	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
366	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
367	  closed embedding.
368	
369	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
370	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
371	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
372	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
373	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
374	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
375	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
376	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
377	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
378	
379	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
380	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
381	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
382	
383	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
384	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
385	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
386	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
387	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
388	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
389	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
390	\end{proof}
391	
392	\section{Group-scheme structure}
393	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
394	
395	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
396	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
397	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
398	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
399	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
400	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.
401	
402	\begin{theorem}\leanok
403	[\(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group scheme]
404	  \label{thm:pic_is_group_scheme}
405	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.groupSchemeStructure}
406	  
407	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs (the relative
408	  % Picard functor takes values in abelian groups) and \S 4 surrounding
409	  % text (group-scheme structure on \(\IPic_{X/S}\) via tensor product);
410	  % arXiv:math/0504020, pp.~17, 26 (read from
411	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318 + L2057-L2069).
412	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
413	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
414	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
415	  %      \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
416	  %   Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
417	  %  topologies  by
418	  %      \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
419	  %      \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
420	  %   \end{dfn}"
421	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
422	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
423	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
424	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
425	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
426	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
427	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
428	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
429	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
430	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
431	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
432	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
433	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
434	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
435	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
436	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
437	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
438	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
439	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
440	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
441	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
442	  scheme locally of finite type.
443	\end{theorem}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
446	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
447	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
448	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
449	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
450	% records the standard transfer.
451	\begin{proof}
452	  
453	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
454	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
455	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
456	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
457	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
458	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
459	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
460	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
461	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
462	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
463	  in the value category).
464	
465	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
466	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
467	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
468	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
469	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
470	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
471	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
472	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
473	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
474	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
475	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
476	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
477	\end{proof}

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined

12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79	
80	\paragraph{Chapter structure.} \cref{sec:coheight_topology} proves the
81	topology-only lemma that coheight is preserved by open immersions
82	(\cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}). \cref{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
83	identifies coheight on a \(\Spec\) with the height of the underlying prime ideal
84	(\cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}). \cref{sec:coheight_bridge}
85	assembles the bridge \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}. The closing
86	\cref{sec:coheight_le_one_instance} packages the codim-\(1\) specialisation
87	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} that downstream files consume.
88	
89	\section{Coheight is preserved by open immersions}
90	\label{sec:coheight_topology}
91	
92	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
93	% L246--257 (the recipe extracts the lemma from a generic
94	% \texttt{Specializes.mem\_open} chain-lifting argument). The lemma is a
95	% generic topological fact about the specialisation preorder; no external
96	% reference shipped at \texttt{b80f227} states it under this exact
97	% formulation.
98	
99	\begin{lemma}
100	
101	\leanok
102	  [Coheight on opens equals coheight in the ambient space]
103	  \label{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
104	  \lean{Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}
105	  Let \(X\) be a topological space carrying the specialisation preorder, and
106	  let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subset. For any \(z \in U\),
107	  \[
108	    \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr),
109	  \]
110	  where on the right \(U\) carries the subspace topology and $\langle z, \,
111	  z \in U\rangle\( denotes \)z\( viewed as a point of \)U$.
112	\end{lemma}
113	
114	\begin{proof}
115	
116	\leanok
117	  Both sides are by definition the supremum, over natural numbers \(n\), of the
118	  set of \(n\) for which there exists a strictly increasing chain
119	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the relevant
120	  specialisation preorder (with \(\prec\) denoting strict specialisation). It
121	  therefore suffices to give an order-preserving bijection between the
122	  strict-generisation chains starting at \(z\) in \(X\) and those starting at the
123	  corresponding point of \(U\).
124	
125	  \emph{Chains in \(U\) embed into \(X\).} The inclusion \(U \hookrightarrow X\) is
126	  continuous, so it preserves specialisation: if \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(U\)
127	  then \(y \rightsquigarrow x\) in \(X\), and distinct points of \(U\) remain
128	  distinct in \(X\). Hence every strict-generisation chain in \(U\) starting at
129	  \(z\) lifts to one in \(X\) of the same length.
130	
131	  \emph{Chains in \(X\) starting at \(z\) all lie in \(U\).} Conversely, suppose
132	  \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) is a strict-generisation chain
133	  in \(X\) with \(a_0 = z \in U\). For each \(i \geq 0\), the relation $a_i \succeq
134	  z\( (in the specialisation preorder) is \)z \rightsquigarrow a_i$, i.e.\
135	  \(a_i\) is in the closure of \(\{z\}\) \emph{viewed from above}; in our
136	  convention the strict order \(a \prec b\) records \(b \rightsquigarrow a\)
137	  (the generic point is the strict maximum), so \(a_i \succeq z\) in \(X\) means
138	  \(a_i \rightsquigarrow z\) in \(X\). By the Mathlib lemma
139	  \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} (any specialisation
140	  \(a \rightsquigarrow z\) with \(z\) in an open set \(U\) forces \(a \in U\), since
141	  \(U\) is open and contains \(z\), hence contains every point whose closure
142	  contains \(z\) --- equivalently every \(a\) with \(a \rightsquigarrow z\)),
143	  every \(a_i\) lies in \(U\). Thus the chain descends to a strict-generisation
144	  chain $\langle z\rangle = \langle a_0\rangle \prec \langle a_1\rangle \prec
145	  \cdots \prec \langle a_n\rangle\( in \)U$ of the same length.
146	
147	  These two operations are mutually inverse on the set of strict-generisation
148	  chains starting at \(z\) (resp.\ at \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\)) and
149	  preserve length, so the suprema agree.
150	\end{proof}
151	
152	\section{Coheight on Spec equals height in PrimeSpectrum}
153	\label{sec:coheight_spec_duality}
154	
155	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
156	% L259--268. The convention reconciliation step traces to Mathlib's
157	% \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} in \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace} L438--447
158	% and to \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} +
159	% \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} in \texttt{Mathlib.Order.KrullDimension}.
160	
161	\begin{lemma}
162	
163	\leanok
164	  [Coheight on \(\Spec R\) equals height in \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)]
165	  \label{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
166	  \lean{Order.coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
167	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(p \in \Spec R\) (viewed as a point of
168	  the scheme spectrum, with the specialisation preorder). Then
169	  \[
170	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(p) \;=\; \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(p),
171	  \]
172	  where the right-hand side is the supremum of the lengths of strict
173	  prime-ideal chains \(\mathfrak{p}_0 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{p}\)
174	  ending at the prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) corresponding to \(p\).
175	\end{lemma}
176	
177	\begin{proof}
178	
179	\leanok
180	  The specialisation preorder on \(\Spec R\) is the dual of the natural
181	  inclusion preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). Concretely, for
182	  \(p, q \in \Spec R\) with underlying primes \(\mathfrak{p}, \mathfrak{q}\),
183	  the relation \(p \leq q\) in the specialisation preorder (which is
184	  \(q \rightsquigarrow p\) in topological notation) corresponds to the prime
185	  containment \(\mathfrak{q} \subseteq \mathfrak{p}\). This is the content of
186	  the Mathlib lemma \texttt{spec\_le\_iff} (located in
187	  \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.AffineSpace}, lines 438--447 at commit
188	  \texttt{b80f227}, with the explicit remark that the preorder on \(\Spec R\)
189	  equals the order-dual of the preorder on \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R\)).
190	
191	  Under this order anti-isomorphism, coheight of \(p\) in \(\Spec R\)
192	  (= supremum of strict-generisation chain lengths starting at \(p\),
193	  i.e.\ ascending chains \(p \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in the
194	  specialisation preorder) translates to height of \(\mathfrak{p}\) in
195	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\) (= supremum of strict descending prime-ideal
196	  chains $\mathfrak{p} \supsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \supsetneq \cdots \supsetneq
197	  \mathfrak{q}_n$, equivalently strict ascending chains
198	  $\mathfrak{q}_n \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq \mathfrak{q}_1 \subsetneq
199	  \mathfrak{p}$). The transport is mechanised by the Mathlib lemmas
200	  \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} (coheight is invariant under order
201	  isomorphisms) and \texttt{Order.height\_toDual} (height in the order-dual
202	  equals coheight in the original order), applied to the order-dual
203	  identification \(\Spec R \simeq_o (\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum} R)^{\operatorname{op}}\)
204	  furnished by \texttt{spec\_le\_iff}.
205	\end{proof}

207	\section{The coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk bridge}
208	\label{sec:coheight_bridge}
209	
210	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2 sketch
211	% L280--296 (the 5-step assembly recipe). Mathlib API surface consumed:
212	%   - \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
213	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:271})
214	%   - \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}, \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}
215	%     (\texttt{AffineScheme.lean:806--813})
216	%   - \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height}
217	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:341--348})
218	%   - \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight}
219	%     (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.Height:45})
220	%   - \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding}, \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}
221	
222	\begin{theorem}
223	
224	\leanok
225	  [Coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge for a scheme point]
226	  \label{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
227	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}
228	  
229	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be any point. Then
230	  \[
231	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z),
232	  \]
233	  i.e.\ the Krull dimension of the stalk at \(z\) equals the coheight of \(z\) in
234	  the underlying topological space of \(X\) (with respect to the specialisation
235	  preorder).
236	\end{theorem}
237	
238	\begin{proof}
239	  
240	  \leanok
241	  The proof proceeds in five steps, assembling Mathlib API rather than
242	  arguing from a single textbook reference.
243	
244	  \emph{Step 1: pick an affine open neighbourhood of \(z\).} By
245	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}
246	  applied to the open set \(X\) itself, there exists an affine open
247	  \(U \subseteq X\) with \(z \in U\). Write \(\langle z, \, z \in U \rangle \in U\)
248	  for \(z\) viewed as a point of the subscheme \(U\).
249	
250	  \emph{Step 2: name the prime corresponding to \(z\) inside \(U\).} Since
251	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\) via the affine equivalence
252	  \(U \simeq \Spec R\) (with \(R := \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)\)), the point
253	  \(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\) corresponds to a prime ideal $\mathfrak{p}
254	  \subseteq R$. In Mathlib this prime is named
255	  \texttt{IsAffineOpen.primeIdealOf}\,\(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\).
256	
257	  \emph{Step 3: the stalk is the localisation of \(\Gamma(U)\) at
258	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The classical affine-stalk identification says
259	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z} \cong R_{\mathfrak{p}}\). In Mathlib this is the
260	  instance \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk}, which witnesses
261	  \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\) as a localisation of \(R\) at \(\mathfrak{p}\) in the
262	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime} sense.
263	
264	  \emph{Step 4: Krull dim of the localisation equals the height of
265	  \(\mathfrak{p}\).} The Mathlib lemma
266	  \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} gives
267	  \[
268	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) \;=\;
269	    \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R_{\mathfrak{p}}) \;=\;
270	    \operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p}),
271	  \]
272	  where \(\operatorname{height}(\mathfrak{p})\) is the prime-ideal height in
273	  \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)\). By
274	  \texttt{Ideal.height\_eq\_primeHeight} this matches the
275	  \(\operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}\) used in
276	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum}.
277	
278	  \emph{Step 5: lift back from \(U\) to \(X\).} By
279	  \cref{lem:coheight_spec_eq_height_primeSpectrum} applied to \(R\) and the
280	  affine identification \(U \simeq \Spec R\),
281	  \[
282	    \operatorname{height}_{\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum}(R)}(\mathfrak{p}) \;=\;
283	    \operatorname{coheight}_{\Spec R}(\langle\text{image of } z\rangle) \;=\;
284	    \operatorname{coheight}_{U}\bigl(\langle z, \, z \in U\rangle\bigr).
285	  \]
286	  Then \cref{lem:coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding} applied to the open
287	  \(U \subseteq X\) promotes the right-hand side back to
288	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z)\). Concatenating the equalities from
289	  Steps~4--5 gives the conclusion.
290	\end{proof}
291	
292	\section{Codim-1 specialisation: synthesising \texorpdfstring{\(\operatorname{KrullDimLE} 1\)}{KrullDimLE 1}}
293	\label{sec:coheight_le_one_instance}
294	
295	% Source pointer: \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} Decision 2
296	% recipe scaffold L298--307 + Decision 2 hreg\_dim refactor sketch
297	% L319--340. This block is the consumer-facing wrapper of the bridge
298	% \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} for the codim-\(1\) case used in
299	% \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and
300	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}.
301	
302	\begin{lemma}
303	
304	\leanok
305	  [Coheight \(= 1\) forces stalk \(\operatorname{Krull\,dim} \leq 1\)]
306	  \label{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}
308	  
309	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(z \in X\) be a point with
310	  \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\). Then the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
311	  has Krull dimension at most \(1\), equivalently $\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,
312	  \mathcal{O}_{X,z}$ holds.
313	\end{lemma}
314	
315	\begin{proof}
316	  
317	  \leanok
318	  By \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight},
319	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = \operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\).
320	  The Mathlib predicate \(\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,R\) unfolds to
321	  \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(R) \leq 1\), so the inequality \(1 \leq 1\)
322	  discharges the goal.
323	\end{proof}
324	
325	\section{Consumer wiring and the Stacks 00TT carve-out}
326	\label{sec:coheight_consumers}
327	
328	\paragraph{Consumer 1: the \texttt{hreg\_dim} refactor in
329	\texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}}{the codim-1 extension chapter}.}
330	The private helper
331	\texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot}
332	inside that chapter's Lean target currently carries an internal
333	\texttt{sorry} of the form
334	\[
335	  \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\;\wedge\;
336	  \operatorname{ringKrullDim}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z} = 1.
337	\]
338	After this chapter's
339	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} (and its underlying
340	\cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}) land, the conjunction splits into
341	two independent goals:
342	\begin{itemize}
343	  \item The right conjunct \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1\)
344	    is discharged by
345	    \(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(z) = 1\) (the standing codim-\(1\) hypothesis
346	    on \(z\)) plus \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight}; the antisymmetric
347	    pair \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \leq 1\) (\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one})
348	    and \(\operatorname{ringKrullDim} \geq 1\) (also from the bridge, since
349	    \(1 \leq 1\)) gives equality.
350	  \item The left conjunct \(\operatorname{IsRegularLocalRing}\,\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\)
351	    is the genuinely-irreducible \emph{Stacks tag \texttt{00TT}} statement
352	    ``smooth ring map over a field forces regular stalks''. It is a separate,
353	    out-of-scope sub-project (Mathlib does \emph{not} ship the implication
354	    \texttt{Algebra.Smooth k A \(\to\) IsRegularLocalRing \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\)} at
355	    \texttt{b80f227}; the project-side build is \(\sim 200\)--\(300\)~LOC of
356	    cotangent-space and pure-dimension machinery, not packaged in this
357	    chapter).
358	\end{itemize}
359	Thus the present chapter \emph{halves} the \texttt{hreg\_dim} obligation in
360	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}: one conjunct closes via the bridge,
361	one remains as a named Mathlib gap.
362	
363	\paragraph{Consumer 2: the \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} threading
364	hygiene in \texorpdfstring{\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}}{the Weil-divisor chapter}.}
365	The order-at-a-prime-divisor map currently carries an explicit instance
366	argument
367	\[
368	  [\,\operatorname{Ring.KrullDimLE}\,1\,(X.\text{presheaf.stalk}\,Y.\text{point})\,]
369	\]
370	at every use site. Because \(Y\) is a prime divisor of \(X\) (so
371	\(\operatorname{coheight}_{X}(Y.\text{point}) = 1\) by the definition of a prime
372	divisor as a codim-\(1\) irreducible closed subset),
373	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} synthesises the instance
374	automatically from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\), so the explicit instance
375	argument can be dropped from \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order}'s signature.
376	
377	\paragraph{Consumer 3: future codim-\(1\) infrastructure.} Any subsequent
378	project lemma that needs to discharge ``the stalk at a codim-\(1\) point has
379	Krull dim \(\leq 1\)'' --- the implicit hypothesis behind every DVR-at-codim-\(1\)
380	argument (cf.\ \cref{lem:smooth_codim_one_dvr} of
381	\cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}) --- now has a one-liner consequence.
382	The instance form
383	\cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} is the right shape for
384	typeclass-search to find it without ceremony.

474	  Let \(\mu \colon G \times_{k} G \to G\) denote the multiplication of \(G\) and let \(\sigma \colon G \times_{k} G \to G \times_{k} G\) be the \emph{shear isomorphism}
475	  \[
476	    \sigma \;=\; \langle \pr_{1},\, \mu \rangle, \qquad (a, b) \;\mapsto\; (a,\, a \cdot b),
477	  \]
478	  built categorically from \(\mu\) and the binary-product projections in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\). Then \(\sigma\) is an isomorphism over the first projection, and pullback along \(\sigma\) together with the canonical first-projection-cotangent identification \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \cong \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) (\cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj}) yields a natural isomorphism of presheaves of modules on \(G\)
479	  \[
480	    \Omega_{G/k} \;\cong\; \pi_{G}^{*}\!\bigl(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\bigr) \qquad \text{on } G,
481	  \]
482	  where \(\pi_{G} \colon G \to \Spec k\) is the structure map (in the standard category-theory notation for the binary product in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\), this is the "first projection" of the singleton-product-with-base, sometimes written \(\pr_{1}\); we use \(\pi_{G}\) to avoid confusion with the binary-product first projection \(\pr_{1} \colon G \times_{k} G \to G\) appearing above). Informally: the relative cotangent of \(G\) is canonically isomorphic to the constant \(\mathcal O_{G}\)-module with fibre \(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\), trivialised along the structure map.
483	
484	  \paragraph{Iter-127 over-k risk register.} The globalisation \textbf{must} be formulated functorially via the shear scheme map \(\sigma\) — which is a morphism in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\), valid for any base field — and \textbf{not} as a pointwise translation by closed (or \(\bar k\)-rational) points of \(G\). The pointwise-translation argument requires either alg-closure of \(k\) or a Nullstellensatz-style \(\bar k\)-point lifting (as in Mathlib's \texttt{pointEquivClosedPoint}, used in the alg-closed-base branch of \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Group.Smooth.lean:50--51}); the functorial shear formulation sidesteps that obstacle entirely. The iter-133 mathlib-analogist verdict (\texttt{analogies/mulright-globalises-cotangent.md} Decision 4) explicitly rules out the pointwise idioms of \texttt{Mathlib.Geometry.Manifold.GroupLieAlgebra.mulInvariantVectorField} (requires \texttt{[Group G]}) and \texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.Group.Basic.Homeomorph.shearMulRight} (requires \texttt{[Group G]} on the carrier) as the over-k-forbidden alternatives.

486	  \paragraph{Lean target name vs.\ construction (option (i), iter-133).} The named target \texttt{mulRight\_globalises\_cotangent} retains the \texttt{mulRight} naming convention because the shear iso \(\sigma = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle\) on \(G \times_{k} G\) is the binary-product upgrade of \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight} (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp\_.lean:277--281}, which packages a one-input automorphism \(a \mapsto a \cdot f\) for a fixed global element \(f \colon \mathbf{1} \to G\)): the map \(\sigma\) parametrises the family of right-translations \(\{\mathrm{mulRight}\,a\}_{a \in G}\) globally across \(G\) by taking the first coordinate as the parameter, and the second projection \(\pr_{2} \circ \sigma = \mu\) realises the right-translation action on the second coordinate. The "globalises the cotangent" phrasing in the name records that this family-of-translations is what triviallises \(\Omega_{G/k}\) via pullback along \(\sigma\) (cf.\ the inverse-pair proof shape of \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.isPullback}, \texttt{Grp\_.lean:293--323}, which uses the structurally identical \texttt{lift \dots (lift \dots \,\iota\, \dots) ≫ μ} construction). No standalone \texttt{mulRight}-name function appears in the Lean signature itself; the connection is purely nomenclatural, and the underlying Lean construction is the binary-product shear iso, not a one-input \texttt{mulRight} automorphism.

493	  \emph{Step 1: Build the shear iso \(\sigma\).} By \cref{lem:GrpObj_shearMulRight}, the binary-product shear \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle \colon G \times_{k} G \to G \times_{k} G\) in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\) — informally \((a, b) \mapsto (a, a \cdot b)\) — is a self-iso of \(G \times_{k} G\) with explicit inverse \(\sigma_{\mathrm{inv}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \circ (\iota \circ \pr_{1}, \pr_{2}) \rangle\), and satisfies the companion identifications \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{1} = \pr_{1}\) and \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} \circ \pr_{2} = \mu\). The Lean target is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.shearMulRight} (closed iter-134, \(\sim 30\) LOC of \texttt{lift\_lift\_assoc} / \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} / \texttt{lift\_comp\_one\_left} calculus on the \(\GrpObj\) axioms, modelled on \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mulRight} and \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.isPullback} of \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Grp\_.lean:277--281,\,293--323}).
494	
495	  \emph{Step 2: Base-change of differentials.} View \(G \times_{k} G\) as a \(G\)-scheme via the first projection \(\pr_{1}\). The relative cotangent of this \(G\)-scheme, \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G}\), is canonically isomorphic to the pullback \(\pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) via \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} (the load-bearing helper sub-lemma, NEEDS\_MATHLIB\_GAP\_FILL, \(\sim 150\)--\(300\) LOC):
496	  \[
497	    \Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \;\cong\; \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k} \qquad \text{on } G \times_{k} G.
498	  \]
499	  Since \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}}\) is an iso over \(\pr_{1}\) (i.e.\ \(\pr_{1} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \pr_{1}\), by construction of \(\sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \langle \pr_{1}, \mu \rangle\) via the binary-product universal property), \(\sigma\) acts as a self-iso of \(G \times_{k} G\) as a \(G\)-scheme via \(\pr_{1}\); functoriality of \(\Omega_{(\cdot)/G}\) under such self-isos then re-routes the Step 2 iso through \(\sigma\) to relate the differentials at the two projections, with \(\pr_{2} \circ \sigma_{\mathrm{hom}} = \mu\).
500	
501	  \emph{Step 3: Restrict along the section \(s := \langle \mathrm{id}_{G},\, \eta_{G} \rangle \colon G \to G \times_{k} G\).} The section \(s\) satisfies \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\) and \(\pr_{2} \circ s = \eta_{G} \circ \pi_{G}\) in \(\Over\,(\Spec k)\), where \(\pi_{G} \colon G \to \Spec k\) is the structure map. Applying \(s^{*}\) to both sides of the Step 2 iso \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G} \cong \pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\):
502	  \begin{itemize}
503	    \item The LHS \(s^{*}(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G})\) is, by the \(G\)-scheme structure given by \(\pr_{1}\) and the section identity \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\), canonically isomorphic to \(\Omega_{G/k}\) (restricting the relative cotangent of \(G \times_{k} G\) over \(G\) to a section of the \(G\)-projection recovers \(\Omega_{G/k}\) — a standard relative-cotangent identification for a section of a smooth morphism). Mathlib-wise this combines \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackComp} with \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullbackId} on the composition \(\pr_{1} \circ s = \mathrm{id}_{G}\).
504	    \item The RHS \(s^{*}(\pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\) is, by \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_restrict_to_identity_section} applied to the section identity \(\pr_{2} \circ s = \eta_{G} \circ \pi_{G}\), canonically isomorphic to \(\pi_{G}^{*}(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\).
505	  \end{itemize}
506	  Composing the LHS and RHS identifications with the pulled-back Step 2 iso yields the displayed iso \(\Omega_{G/k} \cong \pi_{G}^{*}(\eta_{G}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k})\). Estimated build: \(\sim 30\)--\(80\) LOC (per the iter-133 mathlib-analogist verdict Decision 4, step 3 of the LOC envelope). The role of \(\sigma\) in this proof is the Step 2 functorial book-keeping — it pins the "right-translation" interpretation of the trivialisation (the second-coordinate cotangent \(\pr_{2}^{*}\,\Omega_{G/k}\) is identified with the first-projection cotangent \(\Omega_{(G \times_{k} G)/G}\) via Step 2's helper, and \(\sigma\) realises the consistency of these two views as a single \(G\)-scheme structure on \(G \times_{k} G\)).
507

514	[(i.b-packaging) Adjunction-transpose compatibility of structure presheaves of rings along a scheme morphism]
515	  \label{def:GrpObj_schemeHomRingCompatibility}
516	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GrpObj.schemeHomRingCompatibility}
517	  
518	  Let \(f \colon Y \to Z\) be a morphism of schemes. The \emph{adjunction-transpose compatibility morphism along \(f\)} packages the canonical morphism of presheaves of commutative rings on \(Y\)
519	  \[
520	    \varphi_f \;\colon\; f_{\mathrm{top}}^{-1}\,\mathcal O_Z \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal O_Y,
521	  \]
522	  obtained as the image of the structure-sheaf morphism \(f^{\sharp} \colon \mathcal O_Z \to (f_{\mathrm{top}})_{*}\,\mathcal O_Y\) under the symmetric direction of the presheaf-pullback / pushforward adjunction \(f_{\mathrm{top}}^{-1} \dashv (f_{\mathrm{top}})_{*}\) on \(\mathrm{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of the underlying topological map \(f_{\mathrm{top}}\). This is the precise compatibility-of-rings shape consumed by the project's \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf} on Lean targets whose ring-side input comes from a scheme morphism: the \texttt{Differentials.lean:52} construction of \(\varphi'\) from \(f.c\) via the same adjunction transpose is the running model. The helper is used in piece (i.b) wherever a scheme morphism's structure-presheaf data must be fed into \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf} in this transposed shape.
523	\end{definition}

525	\begin{remark}[\(\varphi_f\) vs the \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback} compatibility morphism]
526	  \label{rem:GrpObj_schemeHomRingCompatibility_vs_toRingCatSheafHom}
527	  
528	  The compatibility morphism \(\varphi_f\) of \cref{def:GrpObj_schemeHomRingCompatibility} is \emph{structurally distinct} from the compatibility morphism required by \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}, which expects a morphism of presheaves of rings on the \emph{codomain} \(Y\) (informally \(\mathcal O_Y \to (f_{\mathrm{top}})_{*} \mathcal O_Z\), in the homEquiv shape inverse to \(\varphi_f\)) obtained via Mathlib's \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}. Per the iter-135 mathlib-analogist verdict (\texttt{analogies/phi-compatibility-morphisms.md}), the canonical Mathlib idiom for the latter is \texttt{(Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom \(f\)).hom}. The two conventions serve distinct downstream consumers inside piece (i.b): \(\varphi_f\) feeds \texttt{relativeDifferentialsPresheaf} on the source, while \texttt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom} feeds \texttt{PresheafOfModules.pullback} on a pulled-back module-presheaf. Both shapes appear in the closure of \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_basechange_proj} and \cref{lem:GrpObj_omega_restrict_to_identity_section} below.
529	\end{remark}

1	\chapter{Flattening Stratification of a Coherent Sheaf}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FlatteningStratification}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.a sub-row of Route~A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem, the technical heart that
8	% turns the absolute Quot-functor representability problem on a projective
9	% morphism \(X \to S\) with coherent input \(\F\) into a finite collection of
10	% locally-closed strata over which \(\F\) becomes flat. Mathlib (master
11	% \texttt{b80f227}) has neither a coherent-sheaf flatness predicate over a
12	% scheme base nor the flattening-stratification construction; this chapter
13	% is the prover-ready specification for the new file
14	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean} which a
15	% subsequent iter (file-skeleton lane) will scaffold.
16	%
17	% This chapter is independently parallel-startable: it does not gate on the
18	% Route~A.1.a relative-Spec chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}.
19	
20	\section{Setup and motivation}
21	\label{sec:flatstrat_setup}
22	
23	The construction of the Quot scheme \(\Quot_{\F/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) in Nitsure's exposition
24	of Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite{nitsure-hilbert-quot}, \S5) proceeds by
25	embedding the functor of points of \(\Quot\) into a Grassmannian via Castelnuovo--Mumford
26	regularity, then cutting out the locus of \emph{quotients with flat fibres of the
27	prescribed Hilbert polynomial} by a closed-subscheme condition.  Cutting out this
28	locus uses, as a primary technical input, the existence of the
29	\emph{flattening stratification} of a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on a projective
30	\(S\)-scheme: a finite collection of locally-closed subschemes of \(S\) on each of which
31	the restriction of \(\F\) becomes flat.
32	
33	This chapter packages the Nitsure~\S4 flattening-stratification theorem as the
34	foundational A.2.a building block. The Quot-scheme construction itself (A.2.b),
35	the relative Picard functor (A.1.c), and the assembly of the Picard scheme
36	representability theorem on a smooth proper curve (A.2.c) live in separate sibling
37	chapters.
38	
39	We work throughout with a noetherian base scheme \(S\), a projective morphism
40	\(\pi : X \to S\), and a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module \(\F\). The conclusion of Nitsure's
41	theorem covers an arbitrary noetherian base with a projective \(X \to S\), which
42	specialises directly to the Route~A consumer setting where \(S = T\) is an arbitrary
43	\(k\)-scheme, \(X = C \times_k T\) for \(C\) a smooth proper curve over the base field
44	\(k\), and \(\F\) is a coherent quotient sheaf produced by the Grassmannian embedding
45	of the Quot functor (cf.\ \ below).
46	
47	\section{Coherent flatness over a base}
48	\label{sec:coh_flat}
49	
50	\begin{definition}
51	
52	\leanok
53	[Coherent sheaf flat over the base]
54	  \label{def:coherent_sheaf_flat}
55	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.CoherentSheafFlat}
56	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(f : X \to S\) a morphism of finite type, and
57	  \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module.  We say \(\F\) is \emph{flat over \(S\)}
58	  (equivalently, \(\F\) is \emph{\(f\)-flat}, or \emph{\(S\)-flat}) if for every
59	  point \(x \in X\) the stalk \(\F_x\) is a flat \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module, where the
60	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)}\)-module structure is given by the composite
61	  \(\OO_{S, f(x)} \to \OO_{X, x} \to \End(\F_x)\).
62	  \textit{Source: cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 00HB (flat modules); [Nitsure], \S4
63	  passim.}
64	  Equivalently (after pulling back to an affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq S\) and
65	  an affine open \(V = \Spec B \subseteq f^{-1}(U)\) with \(\F|_V = \widetilde{M}\)
66	  for \(M\) a finite \(B\)-module), \(\F|_V\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat in the sense that \(M\) is a
67	  flat \(A\)-module (with the \(A\)-module structure coming from the ring map
68	  \(A \to B\)).
69	\end{definition}
70	
71	\begin{remark}
72	\label{rem:coh_flat_pullback}
73	  The condition is stable under base change: for any morphism \(g : S' \to S\),
74	  pulling back along the cartesian square
75	  \[
76	    \begin{tikzcd}
77	      X' \ar[r, "g'"] \ar[d, "f'"'] & X \ar[d, "f"] \\
78	      S' \ar[r, "g"]                & S
79	    \end{tikzcd}
80	  \]
81	  yields a coherent \(\OO_{X'}\)-module \(\F' := (g')^* \F\) which is \(S'\)-flat
82	  whenever \(\F\) is \(S\)-flat.  This is the routine cancellation of tensor-product
83	  associativity at the level of stalks. The converse fails in general.
84	\end{remark}
85	
86	\section{Generic flatness}
87	\label{sec:generic_flatness}
88	
89	The construction of the flattening stratification proceeds by Noetherian
90	induction on \(S\). The induction step relies on the following classical
91	\emph{generic flatness} theorem of Grothendieck: over a noetherian integral
92	base, a coherent sheaf is flat over a non-empty open subscheme. Algebraically,
93	this is the assertion that a finitely generated module over a finite-type
94	algebra over a noetherian domain is generically free after inverting a single
95	non-zero element of the base ring.
96	
97	\begin{theorem}
98	[Generic flatness, algebraic form]
99	  \label{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}
100	  
101	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''
102	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1711-L1716).
103	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
104	  % "Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain,
105	  %  and \(B\) a finite type \(A\) algebra. Let \(M\) be a finite \(B\)-module.
106	  %  Then there exists an \(f\in A\), \(f\ne 0\), such that
107	  %  the localisation \(M_f\) is a free module over \(A_f\)."
108	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness''.}
109	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a finite-type \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a finite
110	  \(B\)-module. Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that the localisation
111	  \(M_f\) is a free \(A_f\)-module.
112	\end{theorem}
113	
114	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Over the quotient field \(K\) of \(A\), the \(K\)-algebra
115	% \(B_K = K\otimes _AB\) is of finite type, and \(M_K = K\otimes_AM\) is a
116	% finite module over \(B_K\). Let \(n\) be the dimension of the support
117	% of \(M_K\) over \(\spec B_K\). We argue by induction on \(n\), starting
118	% with \(n=-1\) which is the case when \(M_K =0\). [...]
119	% Now let \(n\ge 0\), and let the lemma be proved for smaller values.
120	% As \(B\) is noetherian and \(M\) is assumed to be a finite \(B\)-module,
121	% there exists a finite filtration
122	% \(0=M_0 \subset \ldots  \subset M_r =M\)
123	% where each \(M_i\) is a \(B\)-submodule of \(M\) such that for each \(i\ge 1\)
124	% the quotient module \(M_i/M_{i-1}\) is isomorphic to \(B/\pp_i\) for
125	% some prime ideal \(\pp_i\) in \(B\). [...] By Noether normalisation lemma,
126	% there exist elements \(b_1,\ldots,b_n \in B\), such that \(K\otimes_AB\) is
127	% finite over its subalgebra \(K[b_1,\ldots, b_n]\) [...]"
128	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1719-L1772; full
129	% proof omitted for length, structural steps restated below in project notation.)
130	\begin{proof}
131	  
132	  Following [Nitsure], \S4: pass to the fraction field \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) and
133	  set \(B_K = K \otimes_A B\), \(M_K = K \otimes_A M\), \(n := \dim \mathrm{supp}_{\Spec B_K} M_K\).
134	  Argue by induction on \(n\), with base case \(n = -1\) (i.e.\ \(M_K = 0\)): each
135	  generator of \(M\) is annihilated by some non-zero element of \(A\); taking the
136	  product of finitely many annihilators yields \(f \in A\) with \(f M = 0\), hence
137	  \(M_f = 0\) is trivially free.  For \(n \ge 0\), a prime filtration of \(M\) as a
138	  finite \(B\)-module
139	  \[
140	    0 = M_0 \subset M_1 \subset \cdots \subset M_r = M
141	  \]
142	  with successive quotients \(M_i / M_{i-1} \cong B / \mathfrak{p}_i\) reduces the
143	  problem (via the splicing fact: \(M_{f'f''}\) is \(A_{f'f''}\)-free when \(M'_{f'}\)
144	  and \(M''_{f''}\) are free) to the case \(M = B/\mathfrak{p}\) for a prime
145	  \(\mathfrak{p} \subset B\), then to the case \(B\) a domain and \(M = B\).
146	  Noether normalisation applied to \(B_K / K\) yields algebraically independent
147	  \(b_1, \dots, b_n \in B\) with \(B_K\) finite over \(K[b_1, \dots, b_n]\); choosing
148	  \(g \ne 0\) to clear denominators of integrality equations, \(B_g\) is finite over
149	  \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\). Let \(m\) be the generic rank: the short exact sequence
150	  \[
151	    0 \to A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0
152	  \]
153	  has \(T\) a finite torsion module over \(A_g[b_1, \dots, b_n]\), so $\dim
154	  \mathrm{supp}_{B_K} (K \otimes_{A_g} T) < n$.  The inductive hypothesis
155	  yields \(h \ne 0\) in \(A\) with \(T_h\) free over \(A_{gh}\); set \(f := gh\).
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\begin{theorem}
159	
160	\leanok
161	[Generic flatness, geometric form]
162	  \label{thm:generic_flatness}
163	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
164	  
165	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
166	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
167	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
168	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
169	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
170	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
171	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
172	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
173	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
174	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
175	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
176	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
177	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
178	\end{theorem}
179	
180	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
181	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
182	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
183	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
184	% Theorem~REF by restricting to a non-empty
185	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
186	\begin{proof}
187	  
188	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(\Spec A \subseteq S\) where \(A\) is a
189	  noetherian domain. The morphism \(p\) is of finite type, so locally on \(X\)
190	  above this affine \(\F\) is the sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) for \(M\) a finite module
191	  over a finite-type \(A\)-algebra \(B\). Theorem~REF
192	  produces \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), with \(M_f\) free over \(A_f\) on each such patch;
193	  shrinking to the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec A\) globalises by patching
194	  finitely many such opens (using noetherianity of \(X\) above the affine).
195	\end{proof}

197	\section{Existence of the flattening stratification}
198	\label{sec:flatstrat_exists}
199	
200	\begin{theorem}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Existence of the flattening stratification]
204	  \label{thm:flattening_stratification_exists}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatteningStratification}
206	  
207	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification''
208	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1811-L1841).
209	  % Parallel pointer: [Stacks Project], tag 052H (verbatim text not yet
210	  % retrieved into a local references/ file; see ``Notes for Plan Agent'').
211	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
212	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, and let
213	  %  \(\F\) be a coherent sheaf on the projective space \(\PP^n_S\)
214	  %  over \(S\). Then the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials of
215	  %  restrictions of \(\F\) to fibers of \(\PP^n_S\to S\)
216	  %  is a finite set. Moreover, for each \(f\in I\) there exist
217	  %  a locally closed subscheme \(S_f\) of \(S\),
218	  %  such that the following conditions are satisfied.
219	  %
220	  %  \textbf{(i) Point-set: } The underlying set \(|S_f|\) of \(S_f\) consists of
221	  %  all points \(s\in S\) where the Hilbert polynomial of the
222	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_s\) is \(f\). In particular,
223	  %  the subsets \(|S_f|\subset |S|\) are disjoint,
224	  %  and their set-theoretic union is \(|S|\).
225	  %
226	  %  \textbf{(ii) Universal property: }
227	  %  Let \(S'=\coprod S_f\) be the coproduct of the \(S_f\), and let
228	  %  \(i:S'\to S\) be the morphism induced by the inclusions
229	  %  \(S_f\hra S\). Then the sheaf \(i^*(\F)\) on \(\PP^n_{S'}\)
230	  %  is flat over \(S'\). Moreover, \(i:S'\to S\) has the universal property that
231	  %  for any morphism \(\varphi :T\to S\) the pullback \(\varphi^*(\F)\) on
232	  %  \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\) if and only if \(\varphi\) factors through
233	  %  \(i:S'\to S\).  The subscheme \(S_f\) is
234	  %  uniquely determined by the polynomial \(f\).
235	  %
236	  %  \textbf{(iii) Closure of strata: }
237	  %  Let the set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials be given a total
238	  %  ordering, defined by putting \(f<g\) whenever \(f(n) < g(n)\) for all
239	  %  \(n \gg  0\). Then the closure in \(S\) of the subset \(|S_f|\)
240	  %  is contained in the union of all \(|S_g|\) where \(f\le g\)."
241	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'';
242	  cf.\ [Stacks Project], tag 052H.}
243	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on the relative
244	  projective space \(\PP^n_S\) over \(S\).  Then:
245	  \begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
246	    \item The set \(I\) of Hilbert polynomials
247	      \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) that arise as \(s\) ranges over \(S\) is
248	      finite.
249	    \item For each \(f \in I\) there exists a locally-closed subscheme
250	      \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set \(|S_f|\) consists of exactly
251	      those \(s \in S\) with \(P_s = f\).  The collection \(\{|S_f|\}_{f \in I}\) is
252	      pairwise disjoint with set-theoretic union \(|S|\).  Forming the coproduct
253	      \(S' := \coprod_{f \in I} S_f\) and the morphism \(i : S' \to S\) assembled
254	      from the inclusions \(S_f \hookrightarrow S\), the pullback \(i^* \F\) on
255	      \(\PP^n_{S'}\) is flat over \(S'\), and \(i : S' \to S\) is universal with this
256	      property: for any morphism \(\varphi : T \to S\) such that \(\varphi^* \F\) on
257	      \(\PP^n_T\) is flat over \(T\), \(\varphi\) factors uniquely through \(i\).
258	      Each \(S_f\) is uniquely determined by \(f\).
259	    \item Order \(I\) by \(f < g \iff f(n) < g(n)\) for all \(n \gg 0\). Then the
260	      closure of \(|S_f|\) in \(|S|\) is contained in \(\bigcup_{g \ge f} |S_g|\).
261	  \end{enumerate}
262	\end{theorem}
263	
264	The proof is long; we split it into sub-lemmas below.  The first sub-lemma is
265	the special case \(n = 0\) (when \(\PP^n_S = S\) and \(\F\) is just a coherent sheaf
266	on \(S\)), which already contains the flat-locus-as-locally-closed-subscheme
267	mechanism. The remaining inputs are (A) the Noetherian-induction reduction to
268	the flat case via generic flatness; (B) uniform vanishing of higher cohomology
269	of \(\F(m)\) for \(m \gg 0\) on \(\F_{V_i}\) for the flat pieces; and (C) base-change
270	isomorphisms for the direct images \(\pi_* \F(m)\), which allow comparing
271	strata for different twists.
272	
273	\begin{lemma}
274	[Flat locus on \(S\) for a coherent sheaf is a locally-closed stratification]
275	  \label{lem:flat_locus_open}
276	  
277	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', Special case
278	  % \(n=0\) (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
279	  % L1849-L1885).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "\textbf{Special case: } Let \(n=0\), so that \(\PP^n_S=S\).
282	  %  For any \(s\in S\), the \textbf{fiber} \(\F|_s\) of \(\F\) over \(s\)
283	  %  will mean the pull-back of \(\F\) to the subscheme
284	  %  \(\spec \kappa(s)\) [...]. The Hilbert polynomial of the
285	  %  restriction of \(\F\) to the fiber over \(s\) is the degree \(0\)
286	  %  polynomial \(e \in \Q[\lambda]\), where \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)}\F|_s\).
287	  %
288	  %  By Nakayama lemma, any basis of \(\F|_s\) prolongs to a neighbourhood \(U\) of \(s\)
289	  %  to give a set of generators for \(F|_U\). Repeating this argument,
290	  %  we see that there exists a smaller neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) in which
291	  %  there is a right-exact sequence
292	  %  ${\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus m} \stackrel{\psi}{\to} {\mathcal O}_V^{\oplus e}
293	  %  \stackrel{\phi}{\to} \F \to 0$.
294	  %  Let \(I_e\subset {\mathcal O}_V\) be the ideal sheaf
295	  %  formed by the entries of the \(e\times m\) matrix
296	  %  \((\psi_{i,j})\) [...].
297	  %  Let \(V_e\) be the closed subscheme of \(V\) defined by \(I_e\).
298	  %  For any morphism of schemes
299	  %  \(f: T\to V\), the pull-back sequence [...]
300	  %  is exact, by right-exactness of tensor products.
301	  %  Hence the pull-back \(f^*\F\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_T\)-module of
302	  %  rank \(e\) if and only if \(f^*\psi =0\), that is,
303	  %  \(f\) factors via the subscheme \(V_e\hra V\) defined by the vanishing of all
304	  %  entries \(\psi_{i,j}\).
305	  %  Thus we have proved assertions \textbf{(i)} and \textbf{(ii)} of the theorem.
306	  %
307	  %  As the rank of the matrix \((\psi_{i,j})\) is lower semi-continuous,
308	  %  it follows that the function \(e\) is upper semi-continuous, which
309	  %  proves the assertion \textbf{(iii)} of the theorem, completing its proof
310	  %  when \(n=0\)."
311	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (special case \(n=0\) of the main theorem).}
312	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(\F\) be a coherent \(\OO_S\)-module.
313	  For each integer \(e \ge 0\), there exists a locally-closed subscheme
314	  \(S_e \hookrightarrow S\) whose underlying set is
315	  \(\{s \in S : \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s = e\}\). The family \(\{S_e\}_e\) is a
316	  finite, mutually disjoint, set-theoretically covering stratification of \(S\),
317	  and a morphism \(f : T \to S\) pulls back \(\F\) to a locally-free
318	  \(\OO_T\)-module of rank \(e\) if and only if \(f\) factors through \(S_e\).  The
319	  function \(s \mapsto \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\) is upper-semicontinuous on \(S\).
320	\end{lemma}
321	
322	\begin{proof}
323	  
324	  By [Nitsure], \S4 (verbatim above): for \(s \in S\), Nakayama produces a
325	  neighbourhood \(V\) of \(s\) and a right-exact local presentation
326	  \(\OO_V^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\psi} \OO_V^{\oplus e} \to \F|_V \to 0\) where
327	  \(e = \dim_{\kappa(s)} \F|_s\).  The closed subscheme \(V_e \hookrightarrow V\)
328	  defined by the ideal sheaf generated by the matrix entries of \(\psi\)
329	  represents the locus where \(\F\) becomes locally free of rank \(e\): by
330	  right-exactness of tensor product, a base change \(f : T \to V\) has
331	  \(f^* \F\) locally free of rank \(e\) iff \(f^* \psi = 0\), iff \(f\) factors
332	  through \(V_e\).  Glueing the \(V_e\) over the cover of \(S\) by such
333	  neighbourhoods (and intersecting with \(V \setminus \bigcup_{e' > e} V_{e'}\)
334	  to get the strictly-equal-to-\(e\) stratum) produces the locally-closed
335	  \(S_e\). Upper-semicontinuity of \(e\) follows because the rank of \(\psi\) is
336	  lower-semicontinuous.
337	\end{proof}

339	\begin{lemma}
340	[Noetherian induction reduction to the flat case]
341	  \label{lem:nonflat_locus_proper}
342	  
343	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``flattening stratification'', General case
344	  % opening paragraphs (read from
345	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1888-L1929).
346	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
347	  % "\textbf{General case: } We now allow the integer \(n\) to be arbitrary.
348	  %  The idea of the proof is as follows:
349	  %  We show the existence of a stratification
350	  %  of \(S\) which is a `g.c.d.' of the flattening
351	  %  stratifications for direct images \(\pi_*\F(i)\) for all \(i \ge N\) for some
352	  %  integer \(N\) [...].
353	  %
354	  %  As \(S\) is noetherian, it is a finite union of irreducible components,
355	  %  and these are closed in \(S\).
356	  %  Let \(Y\) be an irreducible component of \(S\), and let \(U\) be the
357	  %  non-empty open subset of \(Y\) which consists of all points which do not
358	  %  lie on any other irreducible component of \(S\).
359	  %  Let \(U\) be given the reduced subscheme
360	  %  structure. Note that this makes \(U\) an integral scheme, which is a
361	  %  locally closed subscheme of \(S\).
362	  %  By Theorem REF on generic flatness,
363	  %  \(U\) has a non-empty open subscheme \(V\) such that
364	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_V\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_V\).
365	  %  Now repeating the argument with \(S\) replaced by its reduced
366	  %  closed subscheme \(S-V\),
367	  %  it follows by noetherian induction on \(S\) that there exist
368	  %  finitely many reduced, locally closed, mutually disjoint subschemes
369	  %  \(V_i\) of \(S\) such that set-theoretically
370	  %  \(|S|\) is the union of the \(|V_i|\) and
371	  %  the restriction of \(\F\) to \(\PP^n_{V_i}\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_{V_i}\).
372	  %  As each \(V_i\) is a noetherian scheme, and as the Hilbert polynomials
373	  %  are locally constant for a flat family of sheaves, it follows
374	  %  that only finitely many polynomials occur in \(V_i\)
375	  %  in the family of Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\)
376	  %  as \(s\) varies over points of \(V_i\). This allows us to conclude
377	  %  the following:
378	  %
379	  %  \textbf{(A) } Only finitely many distinct Hilbert polynomials
380	  %  \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s,\F_s(m))\) occur, as \(s\)
381	  %  varies over all of \(S\)."
382	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (general case, reduction by Noetherian induction).}
383	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : \PP^n_S \to S\) the relative projective
384	  space, and \(\F\) a coherent sheaf on \(\PP^n_S\).  Then there exist finitely many
385	  reduced, locally-closed, pairwise disjoint subschemes \(V_i \hookrightarrow S\)
386	  whose set-theoretic union is \(|S|\) and such that \(\F|_{\PP^n_{V_i}}\) is
387	  flat over \(\OO_{V_i}\) for each \(i\). Consequently, only finitely many
388	  Hilbert polynomials \(P_s(m) = \chi(\PP^n_s, \F_s(m))\) occur as \(s\) varies
389	  over \(S\).
390	\end{lemma}
391	
392	\begin{proof}
393	  
394	  By [Nitsure], \S4: \(S\) being noetherian, decompose \(|S| = \bigcup Y_j\) into
395	  finitely many irreducible components, each closed.  For an irreducible
396	  component \(Y\), let \(U \subseteq Y\) be the open subset not lying on any other
397	  component, given the reduced subscheme structure (an integral, locally-closed
398	  subscheme of \(S\)).  By Theorem~REF applied to
399	  \(\F|_{\PP^n_U}\), there is a non-empty open \(V \subseteq U\) over which \(\F\) is
400	  flat.  Replace \(S\) by the reduced closed complement \(S \setminus V\) and
401	  repeat. Noetherianity of \(S\) terminates the induction in finitely many
402	  steps, producing the \(V_i\).  On each \(V_i\) the Hilbert polynomial is locally
403	  constant (flat families have locally constant Hilbert polynomial), hence
404	  takes finitely many values on the noetherian (hence quasi-compact) scheme
405	  \(V_i\); the finite union of finite sets is finite.
406	\end{proof}
